```mermaid
    D --> E["x2 of t equals x0 plus integral from t0 to t of f s x1 s ds"]
    E --> F["Iterate n times"]
    F --> G{"Contraction Condition q less than 1"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Sequence Converges Exponentially"]
    G -- No --> I["No Convergence or Slow"]
    H --> J["Limit x star is unique solution"]
```

### 4.3 Failure Mode Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Failure of Existence and Uniqueness"] --> B["Failure of Existence"]
    A --> C["Failure of Uniqueness"]
    B --> B1["f is discontinuous at t0 x0"]
    B --> B2["Singular RHS e.g. 1 over x at x0"]
    B --> B3["Solution escapes to infinity in finite time blow up"]
    C --> C1["f not Lipschitz: e.g. sqrt absolute x"]
    C --> C2["Partial f over partial x unbounded near initial point"]
    C --> C3["Multiple trajectories cross at initial point"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Well-Posedness

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs
        direction TB
        I1["Vector field f of t and x"]
        I2["Initial point t0 and x0"]
    end
    subgraph VerificationStage
        direction TB
        V1["Continuity Test"]
        V2["Lipschitz Test"]
        V3["Gradient Bound on Rectangle R"]
    end
    subgraph ComputationStage
        direction TB
        C1["Choose alpha less than 1 over L"]
        C2["Compute Picard iterates x n"]
        C3["Estimate error bound q to power n over 1 minus q"]
    end
    subgraph Output
        direction TB
        O1["Existence Interval I"]
        O2["Unique Solution x star of t"]
        O3["Convergence Certificate"]
    end
    Inputs --> VerificationStage
    VerificationStage --> ComputationStage
    ComputationStage --> Output
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*State the Picard–Lindelöf theorem for the IVP $\dot{x} = f(t,x)$ with $x(t_0) = x_0$. What are the two essential hypotheses?*

**Model Answer**:
> If $f(t,x)$ is continuous in a neighbourhood of $(t_0, x_0)$ and satisfies a **local Lipschitz condition** in $x$ (uniformly in $t$) at that point, then the IVP admits a **unique** solution $x(t)$ on some open interval $I \ni t_0$.
>
> The two hypotheses are:
> 1. **Continuity** of $f$ — ensures existence (Peano).
> 2. **Local Lipschitz condition** in $x$ — ensures uniqueness (Lipschitz/Picard).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Give an example of an IVP for which existence holds but uniqueness fails. Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer**:
> Consider $\dot{x} = \sqrt{\vert x \vert}$, $x(0) = 0$.
> The function $f(t,x) = \sqrt{\vert x \vert}$ is **continuous** at $(0,0)$, so existence is guaranteed (Peano's theorem).
> However, $f$ is **not Lipschitz** at $x = 0$ since $\partial f / \partial x = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\vert x \vert}} \to \infty$ as $x \to 0$.
> Consequently, both $x(t) \equiv 0$ and $x(t) = t^2/4$ for $t \geq 0$ (and $x(t) = 0$ for $t \leq 0$) are valid solutions — uniqueness fails.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice**: KTU ESE Module 1 questions on Existence & Uniqueness typically have a 7+7 split. **Attempt any ONE full question** from the pair below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove the Picard–Lindelöf theorem (Existence and Uniqueness) for the scalar IVP $\dot{x} = f(t,x)$, $x(t_0) = x_0$ where $f$ is continuous and Lipschitz in $x$.

**Model Solution**:

*Statement*: Let $R = \{(t,x) : \vert t - t_0 \vert \leq a, \; \vert x - x_0 \vert \leq b\}$ be a closed rectangle. If $f$ is continuous on $R$ and satisfies a Lipschitz condition in $x$ with constant $L$, then the IVP has a **unique** solution on the interval $I = [t_0 - \alpha, t_0 + \alpha]$ where $\alpha = \min(a, b/M)$ and $M = \max_R \vert f \vert$.

*Proof*: Equivalent integral form: $x(t) = x_0 + \int_{t_0}^t f(s, x(s)) \, ds$. Define the operator $\mathcal{T}x(t) = x_0 + \int_{t_0}^t f(s, x(s)) \, ds$ on the closed ball $B = \{x \in C(I) : \Vert x - x_0 \Vert_\infty \leq b\}$.

(1) **$\mathcal{T}: B \to B$**: For $x \in B$ and $t \in I$:
$$\vert (\mathcal{T} x)(t) - x_0 \vert \leq \int_{t_0}^t \vert f \vert \, ds \leq M \vert t - t_0 \vert \leq M \alpha \leq M \cdot \frac{b}{M} = b$$
Hence $\mathcal{T} x \in B$. $\checkmark$

(2) **Contraction**: For $x, y \in B$:
$$\vert (\mathcal{T} x)(t) - (\mathcal{T} y)(t) \vert \leq L \int_{t_0}^t \vert x(s) - y(s) \vert \, ds \leq L \alpha \Vert x - y \Vert_\infty$$
Since $\alpha < 1/L$, the constant $q = L \alpha < 1$. $\checkmark$

(3) **Banach Fixed Point Theorem** gives a unique fixed point $x^* \in B$ of $\mathcal{T}$, which is the unique solution of the IVP. $\blacksquare$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Verify the existence and uniqueness of the IVP $\dot{x} = t^2 + x^2$, $x(0) = 0$ on some interval around $t_0 = 0$. Compute the first three Picard iterations.

**Model Solution**:

Let $f(t, x) = t^2 + x^2$. This is a polynomial, hence **continuous** everywhere. For Lipschitz:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x$$

On the rectangle $R = \{(t,x) : \vert t \vert \leq a, \; \vert x \vert \leq b\}$, $\vert \partial f / \partial x \vert \leq 2b$. Choose $b = 1$, so $L = 2$. Then $M = \max_R (t^2 + x^2) = a^2 + 1$. We need $\alpha = \min(a, 1/(a^2+1))$. For $a = 0.4$, $M = 1.16$, $b/M \approx 0.86$, so $\alpha = 0.4 < 1/L = 0.5$. Unique solution exists on $[-0.4, 0.4]$.

**Picard iterations**:

- $x_0(t) = 0$
- $x_1(t) = 0 + \int_0^t (s^2 + 0^2) \, ds = \frac{t^3}{3}$
- $x_2(t) = 0 + \int_0^t \left( s^2 + \left(\frac{s^3}{3}\right)^2 \right) \, ds = \frac{t^3}{3} + \frac{t^7}{63}$
- $x_3(t) = 0 + \int_0^t \left( s^2 + x_2(s)^2 \right) \, ds = \frac{t^3}{3} + \frac{t^7}{63} + \frac{2 t^{11}}{2079} + \frac{t^{15}}{59535}$

The series solution is the unique solution on the local interval.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a Lipschitz condition. Why is it essential for uniqueness of solutions to IVPs? Show that $f(t,x) = x \sin(x^2)$ is Lipschitz on $\mathbb{R}$ with some constant $L$, but $f(t,x) = \sqrt{x}$ on $[0, \infty)$ is not.

**Model Solution**:

*Definition*: $f$ is **Lipschitz** in $x$ on a set $S$ if there exists $L \geq 0$ such that $\vert f(t, x_1) - f(t, x_2) \vert \leq L \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert$ for all $x_1, x_2 \in S$.

*Importance*: Lipschitz condition bounds the rate at which $f$ can change between two $x$-values, preventing two solutions from "merging" or "splitting" at the same initial point — guaranteeing **uniqueness**.

*Case 1*: $f(x) = x \sin(x^2)$. Then $f'(x) = \sin(x^2) + 2x^2 \cos(x^2)$. We have $\vert f'(x) \vert \leq 1 + 2x^2$, which is unbounded. So $f$ is **not globally Lipschitz on $\mathbb{R}$**. Let us instead verify on a bounded set: for $x \in [-R, R]$, $L = 1 + 2R^2$.

*Correction*: For a cleaner example, take $f(t,x) = \sin(x)$. Then $\vert f(x_1) - f(x_2) \vert = \vert \int_{x_2}^{x_1} \cos(u) \, du \vert \leq \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert$ by the Mean Value Theorem. So $L = 1$ works — Lipschitz on $\mathbb{R}$.

*Case 2*: $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ on $[0, \infty)$. Take $x_1 = \varepsilon^2$, $x_2 = 0$. Then:
$$\frac{\vert f(x_1) - f(x_2) \vert}{\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon^2} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \to \infty \text{ as } \varepsilon \to 0$$
Hence $f$ is **not Lipschitz** at $x = 0$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the failure of uniqueness for the IVP $\dot{x} = \sqrt{\vert x \vert}$, $x(0) = 0$. Construct two distinct solutions and plot/describe them.

**Model Solution**:

The function $f(x) = \sqrt{\vert x \vert}$ is **continuous** at $x = 0$, so Peano's theorem guarantees **at least one** solution exists. However, $\partial f / \partial x = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\vert x \vert}} \to \infty$ as $x \to 0$, so the Lipschitz condition fails near $x = 0$, and uniqueness is **not guaranteed**.

**Solution 1 (Trivial)**: $x_1(t) \equiv 0$ for all $t$. This clearly satisfies $\dot{x}_1 = 0 = \sqrt{\vert 0 \vert}$ and $x_1(0) = 0$. $\checkmark$

**Solution 2 (Non-trivial)**: Define
$$
x_2(t) = \begin{cases} 0 & t \leq 0 \\ \frac{t^2}{4} & t \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

*Verification for $t \geq 0$*: $\dot{x}_2(t) = t/2$. And $\sqrt{\vert x_2(t) \vert} = \sqrt{t^2/4} = t/2$ (since $t \geq 0$). So $\dot{x}_2 = \sqrt{x_2}$. At $t = 0$, $x_2(0) = 0$. $\checkmark$

**Conclusion**: Two distinct solutions exist for the same IVP, demonstrating that continuity alone does not imply uniqueness. The Lipschitz hypothesis is the critical additional requirement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Confusing existence with uniqueness** (loses 1–2 marks): Always state both hypotheses separately.
> 2. **Forgetting the rectangle bound $R$** when computing $L$ (loses 1 mark): The Lipschitz constant must be evaluated on a *closed, bounded* rectangle — not on the whole space.
> 3. **Using global Lipschitz when local suffices** (loses 1 mark): The theorem is a *local* result; do not claim global existence without further argument.
> 4. **Incomplete Picard iteration** (loses 2 marks): When asked to "compute the first $n$ iterations", students often skip the $x_0(t) \equiv x_0$ step — this is mandatory.
> 5. **Writing $f$ is continuous $\Rightarrow$ unique** (loses 2 marks): This is the most common conceptual error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Existence & Uniqueness Theorem (Picard–Lindelöf / Cauchy–Lipschitz)** is the foundation of all ODE theory in non-linear dynamics.
- **Two hypotheses**:
  1. $f(t, \mathbf{x})$ is **continuous** at $(t_0, \mathbf{x}_0)$ $\Rightarrow$ **Existence**.
  2. $f(t, \mathbf{x})$ is **Lipschitz** in $\mathbf{x}$ (locally) at $(t_0, \mathbf{x}_0)$ $\Rightarrow$ **Uniqueness**.
- **Lipschitz shortcut**: If $\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$ exists and is **continuous and bounded** on a closed rectangle $R$ around $(t_0, \mathbf{x}_0)$, then $f$ is locally Lipschitz on $R$, with $L = \sup_R \Vert \partial f / \partial \mathbf{x} \Vert$.
- **Picard iteration** is the **constructive proof** of the theorem: $x_{n+1}(t) = x_0 + \int_{t_0}^t f(s, x_n(s)) \, ds$.
- **Banach Fixed Point Theorem** underpins the proof: $\mathcal{T}$ is a **contraction** with $q = L \alpha < 1$.
- **Convergence rate**: $\Vert x_n - x^* \Vert_\infty \leq \frac{q^n}{1-q} \Vert x_1 - x_0 \Vert_\infty$ — **exponential**.
- **Classical counterexample** to uniqueness (but not existence): $\dot{x} = \sqrt{\vert x \vert}$, $x(0) = 0$ — has the solutions $x \equiv 0$ and $x = t^2/4$ for $t \geq 0$.
- **Maximal existence interval**: $I_{\max} = (t_0 - \alpha, t_0 + \beta)$ where $\alpha, \beta$ depend on $M$ and $L$.
- **Blow-up**: Solutions can escape to infinity in finite time even if $f$ is smooth everywhere (e.g., $\dot{x} = x^2$, $x(0) = 1$ blows up at $t = 1$).
- **Peano's theorem** (existence only): Continuity of $f$ alone is enough to guarantee at least one solution.
- **Engineering utility**: Uniqueness is the **mathematical certificate of determinism** — without it, predictions from non-linear models would be unreliable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Potentials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the rigorous mathematical framework of **Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos**, a **potential function** (often denoted as $V(x)$ or $U(x)$) is a scalar-valued function defined on the state space of a dynamical system, whose spatial gradient reproduces the underlying vector field of the system. Formally, for a one-dimensional autonomous system:

$$\dot{x} = f(x)$$

the function $V(x)$ is called a **potential** if the dynamics can be rewritten as:

$$\dot{x} = -\frac{dV(x)}{dx} = -V'(x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** A system expressible in this form is termed a **Gradient System** (or **Conservative System**). The vector field is irrotational, i.e., $\nabla \times f = 0$, and the dynamics is purely dissipative in the sense that the system monotonically descends (or ascends) the potential landscape.

For an $n$-dimensional system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$, the potential is defined by:

$$\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = -\nabla V(\mathbf{x})$$

In this case, the system possesses a **strict Lyapunov function**, guaranteeing that the trajectories can never form closed orbits (limit cycles are forbidden) — every trajectory must terminate at a critical point of $V$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Valley and Ball" Intuition

Imagine a small **marble** placed on a hilly terrain shaped by the function $V(x)$ (where $x$ is horizontal position and $V$ is the height).

- **The Marble's Motion** represents the state $x(t)$ of the dynamical system.
- **The Force** experienced by the marble at any point is $f(x) = -V'(x)$. This is precisely **Newton's second law** for a mass in a gravitational field: force equals the negative gradient of potential energy.
- **Hilltops (local maxima of $V$)** correspond to **unstable equilibria** — the marble, if nudged even infinitesimally, rolls away.
- **Valleys (local minima of $V$)** correspond to **stable equilibria** — the marble naturally settles there.
- **Flat plateaus (saddle points / inflection points)** correspond to marginal equilibria where the linearization test is inconclusive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this useful in Nonlinear Dynamics?**
> Potentials convert a *vector-field problem* (analyzing direction fields) into a *scalar-calculus problem* (analyzing critical points of a single function). This dramatically simplifies stability analysis, bifurcation tracking, and the visualization of catastrophic transitions such as the **saddle-node bifurcation**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V(x)$ | Potential Energy (or Potential Function) | $J$ (Joules) in physics, dimensionless in abstract dynamics |
| $x$ | State variable | Position $m$, or dimensionless |
| $k$ | Stiffness of a harmonic well | $N/m$ |
| $\mu$ | Bifurcation / control parameter | Dimensionless |
| $\nabla V$ | Gradient of the potential | Force field vector |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Note:** In the abstract setting of a course on Nonlinear Dynamics, the potential is *purely a mathematical construct* — it need not represent physical energy. It is a tool for *visualizing and classifying* the qualitative structure of the flow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The existence of a potential $V(x)$ and the stability classification proceed through the following structured chain of reasoning:

1. **Existence Criterion:** A continuously differentiable potential $V(x)$ exists for a 1D system $\dot{x} = f(x)$ **if and only if** $f$ is itself the negative derivative of some scalar. Since every scalar $C^1$ function on $\mathbb{R}$ has a well-defined derivative, *any* 1D autonomous system can, in principle, be integrated to produce a potential:
$$V(x) = -\int f(x)\, dx + C$$
where $C$ is an arbitrary additive constant (irrelevant to dynamics).

2. **Critical Point Condition:** Fixed points (equilibria) of the system satisfy $\dot{x} = 0$, which translates to:
$$V'(x^*) = 0$$
i.e., fixed points are precisely the **stationary points of the potential**.

3. **Stability Classification (Second-Derivative Test):**
   - If $V''(x^*) > 0$ (concave-up / local **minimum** of $V$) → $x^*$ is a **linearly stable** fixed point (attractor).
   - If $V''(x^*) < 0$ (concave-down / local **maximum** of $V$) → $x^*$ is a **linearly unstable** fixed point (repeller).
   - If $V''(x^*) = 0$ → **higher-order test required**; typically a saddle-node bifurcation scenario.

4. **Topological Flow Direction:** Because $\dot{x} = -V'(x)$, the system flows to the **right** wherever the potential is decreasing and to the **left** wherever it is increasing. Thus the state is monotonically "funneled" toward minima of $V$.

5. **Energy-Like Monotonicity:** Define $E(t) = V(x(t))$. Then:
$$\frac{dE}{dt} = V'(x)\cdot \dot{x} = V'(x)\cdot\bigl(-V'(x)\bigr) = -\bigl[V'(x)\bigr]^2 \le 0$$
This shows $V$ is a **strict Lyapunov function** (non-increasing) for the gradient system, with $dE/dt = 0$ only at equilibria.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Name / Meaning** | **Stability Implication** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\dot{x} = -V'(x)$ | Gradient system definition | Trajectories descend the potential |
| $V(x) = -\int f(x)\,dx$ | Construction of potential | Unique up to constant $C$ |
| $V'(x^*) = 0$ | Fixed-point condition | Equilibria are critical points |
| $V''(x^*) > 0$ | Local minimum of $V$ | Stable fixed point (attractor) |
| $V''(x^*) < 0$ | Local maximum of $V$ | Unstable fixed point (repeller) |
| $V''(x^*) = 0$ | Inflection point | Degenerate / bifurcation candidate |
| $\dfrac{d}{dt}V(x(t)) = -\bigl[V'(x)\bigr]^2$ | Lyapunov decay rate | Strict monotonic energy decrease |
| $V(x) = \frac{1}{2}kx^{2}$ | Harmonic / quadratic well | Single stable minimum at $x=0$ |
| $V(x) = -\frac{\mu}{2}x^{2} + \frac{1}{4}x^{4}$ | Duffing-type double well | Supercritical pitchfork at $\mu=0$ |
| $V(x) = \frac{1}{3}x^{3} - \mu x$ | Cubic normal form | Saddle-node at $\mu=0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical-Bar LaTeX Rule:** Whenever the absolute value or determinant notation is required, write it as $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert V'' \vert$ — **never** use a bare pipe `|` inside this table, since it breaks markdown rendering.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Mechanics:** Post-buckling of columns and beams is analyzed through asymmetric potentials where the load parameter plays the role of $\mu$.
- **Microelectromechanical Systems (MEMS):** Doubly-clamped micro-beams exhibit Duffing-type nonlinearity; their potential landscape determines pull-in instability voltages.
- **Nonlinear Optics:** Optical bistability in ring resonators corresponds to a double-well refractive-index potential.
- **Power Systems:** The "energy function" method for transient stability assessment of large electrical grids is a direct generalization of the potential concept to high-dimensional systems.
- **Population Dynamics & Economics:** Gene-frequency dynamics and consumer-preference models admit gradient-form descriptions with interpretable potentials.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — The Quadratic (Harmonic) Potential

**Problem Setup:** Consider the linear system $\dot{x} = -kx$ with $k > 0$. We will derive the potential and classify its fixed point.

**Step 1: Write the governing ODE.**
$$\dot{x} = -kx$$

**Step 2: Identify the structure.** Comparing with $\dot{x} = -V'(x)$, we conclude that $V'(x) = kx$.

**Step 3: Integrate to obtain $V(x)$.**
$$V(x) = \int kx\,dx = \frac{1}{2}kx^{2} + C$$

Dropping the irrelevant constant $C$ (it does not affect dynamics):
$$V(x) = \frac{1}{2}kx^{2}$$

**Step 4: Find the fixed points.** Setting $V'(x^*) = 0$:
$$kx^* = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x^* = 0$$

**Step 5: Apply the second-derivative stability test.**
$$V''(x^*) = k > 0$$

Since $k$ is strictly positive, the potential is a **convex parabola** with a global minimum at $x^* = 0$. Hence $x^* = 0$ is a **stable fixed point**.

**Step 6: Compute the Lyapunov decay.**
$$\frac{d}{dt}V(x(t)) = V'(x)\cdot \dot{x} = kx \cdot (-kx) = -k^{2}x^{2} = -\bigl[V'(x)\bigr]^{2}$$

This confirms that $V$ decreases monotonically along trajectories at a quadratic rate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — The Duffing-Type Double-Well Potential

**Problem Setup:** Consider the bistable system $\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{3}$, with $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ as the bifurcation parameter. We will determine the potential, the fixed points, and their stability as $\mu$ varies.

**Step 1: Identify the form.** The right-hand side is a polynomial in $x$:
$$f(x) = \mu x - x^{3}$$

**Step 2: Set $f(x) = -V'(x)$, so $V'(x) = -f(x) = x^{3} - \mu x$.**

**Step 3: Integrate to recover $V(x)$.**
$$V(x) = \int (x^{3} - \mu x)\,dx = \frac{x^{4}}{4} - \frac{\mu x^{2}}{2} + C$$

Discarding the constant:
$$V(x) = \frac{1}{4}x^{4} - \frac{\mu}{2}x^{2}$$

**Step 4: Find the fixed points.** Solve $V'(x) = x^{3} - \mu x = 0$:
$$x(x^{2} - \mu) = 0$$

This yields:
$$x_{1}^{*} = 0, \quad x_{2,3}^{*} = \pm\sqrt{\mu} \quad \text{(real only when } \mu \ge 0\text{)}$$

**Step 5: Compute $V''(x)$ to classify stability.**
$$V''(x) = 3x^{2} - \mu$$

Evaluate at each fixed point:

- At $x_{1}^{*} = 0$: $\;V''(0) = -\mu$.
- At $x_{2}^{*} = +\sqrt{\mu}$: $\;V''(\sqrt{\mu}) = 3\mu - \mu = 2\mu > 0$.
- At $x_{3}^{*} = -\sqrt{\mu}$: $\;V''(-\sqrt{\mu}) = 3\mu - \mu = 2\mu > 0$.

**Step 6: Tabulate the stability outcome.**

| Fixed Point | Existence | $V''$ | Stability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x^{*} = 0$ | Always | $-\mu$ | Stable if $\mu < 0$, unstable if $\mu > 0$ |
| $x^{*} = \pm\sqrt{\mu}$ | $\mu > 0$ only | $2\mu > 0$ | Always stable for $\mu > 0$ |

**Step 7: Bifurcation Analysis.** At $\mu = 0$, the three fixed points *collide* and exchange stability. The qualitative structure changes: the single-well potential (for $\mu < 0$) transitions to a double-well potential (for $\mu > 0$). This is the hallmark of a **supercritical pitchfork bifurcation**.

For $\mu < 0$ (single-well regime):
$$V(x) = \frac{x^{4}}{4} + \frac{\vert \mu \vert}{2}x^{2}$$
a single bowl-shaped well with stable minimum at $x=0$.

For $\mu > 0$ (double-well regime):
$$V(x) = \frac{x^{4}}{4} - \frac{\mu}{2}x^{2}$$
two symmetric wells separated by a hump at the origin; the origin becomes an unstable local maximum.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — The Cubic (Saddle-Node) Potential

**Problem Setup:** Consider $\dot{x} = \mu - x^{2}$. Determine the potential and the fixed-point structure.

**Step 1:** Identify $V'(x) = x^{2} - \mu$.

**Step 2:** Integrate:
$$V(x) = \frac{x^{3}}{3} - \mu x$$

**Step 3:** Fixed points satisfy $x^{2} = \mu$, giving $x^{*} = \pm\sqrt{\mu}$ (real only for $\mu \ge 0$).

**Step 4:** Compute $V''(x) = 2x$.
- At $x^{*} = +\sqrt{\mu}$: $\;V'' = 2\sqrt{\mu} > 0$ → **stable** (local minimum).
- At $x^{*} = -\sqrt{\mu}$: $\;V'' = -2\sqrt{\mu} < 0$ → **unstable** (local maximum).

**Step 5:** As $\mu$ decreases through zero, the minimum and maximum of $V$ **collide at the origin and annihilate**. For $\mu < 0$, no real fixed points exist, and the flow is monotonically negative (all trajectories escape to $-\infty$). This is the **saddle-node bifurcation** mechanism.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Does a Potential Exist?

| **System Class** | **Potential Exists?** | **Reason** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1D autonomous: $\dot{x}=f(x)$ | **Yes, always** | Single-variable integration is path-independent |
| 2D autonomous: $\dot{\mathbf{x}}=\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ | **Only if** $\dfrac{\partial F_{1}}{\partial x_{2}} = \dfrac{\partial F_{2}}{\partial x_{1}}$ (curl-free) | Poincaré integrability condition |
| Non-autonomous / time-driven | **Generally no** | Time-dependence breaks gradient structure |
| Hamiltonian: $\dot{q}=\partial H/\partial p,\;\dot{p}=-\partial H/\partial q$ | **Yes, via** $H$ | Hamiltonian is conserved; phase portrait has level sets of $H$ |
| Damped driven: e.g. Duffing oscillator | **No single scalar** | Non-conservative forcing prevents exact gradient form |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Potential-Field Correspondence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System ODE dot x = f of x"] --> B["Is f expressible as negative gradient"]
    B -- Yes --> C["Integrate to obtain V of x = minus integral f dx"]
    B -- No --> D["System is non-gradient: limit cycles possible"]
    C --> E["Compute critical points V prime equals zero"]
    E --> F{"V double prime at x star"}
    F -- "> 0" --> G["Local minimum: STABLE attractor"]
    F -- "< 0" --> H["Local maximum: UNSTABLE repeller"]
    F -- "= 0" --> I["Higher order test: BIFURCATION candidate"]
    G --> J["Trajectories descend toward minimum"]
    H --> K["Trajectories diverge from maximum"]
    I --> L["Track parameter mu: pitchfork or saddle node"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Double-Well Potential Evolution Across Pitchfork

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhaseA["Mu less than 0: Single Well"]
        A1["Convex bowl shape"] --> A2["Single minimum at origin"]
        A2 --> A3["x star equals 0 STABLE"]
    end
    subgraph PhaseB["Mu equals 0: Bifurcation Point"]
        B1["Quartic flatness at origin"] --> B2["Triple fixed point collision"]
        B2 --> B3["Stability exchange"]
    end
    subgraph PhaseC["Mu greater than 0: Double Well"]
        C1["Two wells with central hump"] --> C2["Stable at plus minus sqrt mu"]
        C2 --> C3["Unstable origin"]
    end
    PhaseA --> PhaseB
    PhaseB --> PhaseC
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation of the Pitchfork Bifurcation Potential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Parameter mu on horizontal axis"] --> Q["Region mu less than 0"]
    P --> R["Critical mu equals 0"]
    P --> S["Region mu greater than 0"]
    Q --> Q1["Potential V = x to the 4 over 4 plus absolute mu x squared over 2"]
    R --> R1["Potential V = x to the 4 over 4 inflection at origin"]
    S --> S1["Potential V = x to the 4 over 4 minus mu x squared over 2"]
    Q1 --> Q2["Single well: x equals 0 stable minimum"]
    R1 --> R2["Transitional: cubic flat point at origin"]
    S1 --> S2["Double well: x equals 0 unstable maximum, plus minus sqrt mu stable minima"]
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Gradient System Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X["State vector x at time t"] --> XD["Differentiate: dot x"]
    XD --> N["Compute negative gradient of V at x"]
    N --> U["Lyapunov decay: dV by dt equals minus gradient V squared"]
    U --> R["Result: monotonic descent to local minimum"]
    R --> M["Equilibrium reached when gradient V equals zero"]
    M --> S["Stability: sign of Hessian matrix of V"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Every block above corresponds to a *mathematical operation* on the scalar potential $V$, and the entire gradient-system machinery can be summarized as: **state evolution = negative gradient descent on a scalar landscape**. This is the conceptual bridge between dynamical-systems theory and modern optimization (e.g., gradient descent in machine learning).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a potential function for a one-dimensional autonomous system $\dot{x} = f(x)$. How are the fixed points of the system related to the critical points of the potential?**

**Model Answer:**
A scalar function $V(x)$ is called a *potential* for the system $\dot{x} = f(x)$ if $f(x) = -V'(x)$, equivalently, $V(x) = -\int f(x)\,dx + C$. The fixed points satisfy $f(x^*) = 0$, which translates to $V'(x^*) = 0$. Hence the equilibria of the system are exactly the **stationary points** of $V$. Stability is determined by the sign of $V''(x^*)$: positive → stable (minimum of $V$); negative → unstable (maximum of $V$).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain why a potential function acts as a strict Lyapunov function for a gradient system.**

**Model Answer:**
For a gradient system $\dot{x} = -V'(x)$, the time derivative of $V$ along trajectories is:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = V'(x)\cdot \dot{x} = V'(x)\cdot\bigl(-V'(x)\bigr) = -\bigl[V'(x)\bigr]^{2} \le 0$$

with equality only when $V'(x) = 0$, i.e., at equilibria. Since $dV/dt$ is non-positive and vanishes only at the critical points, $V$ is a **strict Lyapunov function**, guaranteeing global asymptotic stability of the minimum of $V$ (for any initial condition, the trajectory converges to a local minimum of $V$).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice]`

**(a)** Consider the nonlinear system $\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{3}$, where $\mu$ is a real parameter. **(7 marks)**
- (i) Construct the potential function $V(x)$.
- (ii) Determine the fixed points and classify their stability.
- (iii) Identify and describe the bifurcation that occurs at $\mu = 0$.

**(b)** With the aid of a potential-landscape diagram, explain the mechanism of a **saddle-node bifurcation** for the system $\dot{x} = \mu - x^{2}$. Show how a stable–unstable pair of fixed points is created as $\mu$ crosses zero. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**(i) Construction of $V(x)$:**

Given $\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{3} = -V'(x)$, we have $V'(x) = x^{3} - \mu x$. Integrating:

$$V(x) = \frac{x^{4}}{4} - \frac{\mu x^{2}}{2} + C$$

Dropping the constant:

$$V(x) = \frac{x^{4}}{4} - \frac{\mu}{2}x^{2}$$

**(ii) Fixed points and stability:**

Setting $V'(x) = x^{3} - \mu x = x(x^{2}-\mu) = 0$:

$$x_{1}^{*} = 0, \qquad x_{2,3}^{*} = \pm\sqrt{\mu} \;\;(\mu \ge 0)$$

The second derivative is $V''(x) = 3x^{2} - \mu$. Evaluating:

- $V''(0) = -\mu$: stable if $\mu < 0$, unstable if $\mu > 0$.
- $V''(\pm\sqrt{\mu}) = 2\mu > 0$: stable for all $\mu > 0$.

**(iii) Bifurcation identification:**

At $\mu = 0$, the three fixed points coincide and exchange stability: the stable origin becomes unstable, and two new stable symmetric equilibria $\pm\sqrt{\mu}$ emerge. This is a **supercritical pitchfork bifurcation**.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**System:** $\dot{x} = \mu - x^{2}$, so $V'(x) = x^{2} - \mu$ and the potential is $V(x) = \dfrac{x^{3}}{3} - \mu x$.

**Fixed points:** $x^{2} = \mu \;\Longrightarrow\; x^{*} = \pm\sqrt{\mu}$, real only for $\mu \ge 0$.

**Stability:** $V''(x) = 2x$. At $x^{*}=+\sqrt{\mu}$, $V''>0$ → stable (minimum of $V$). At $x^{*}=-\sqrt{\mu}$, $V''<0$ → unstable (maximum of $V$).

**Mechanism:** As $\mu$ increases through zero, the potential $V(x) = \dfrac{x^{3}}{3} - \mu x$ transitions from a monotonically increasing cubic (for $\mu < 0$, no real critical points) to one with a local minimum followed by a local maximum (for $\mu > 0$). The pair of fixed points is "born" exactly at $\mu = 0$ via the *touching* of the potential curve with the horizontal axis. This annihilation/creation of a stable–unstable pair is the **saddle-node (fold) bifurcation**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Choice]`

**(a)** Construct the potential for the gradient system $\dot{x} = \sin(x)$. Hence determine the stability of the fixed points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system $\dot{x} = x - x^{3}$:
- (i) Derive the potential function. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) Find the fixed points and classify them using the second-derivative test. **(3 marks)**
- (iii) Sketch the phase line and potential landscape. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

We have $\dot{x} = \sin(x) = -V'(x)$, so $V'(x) = -\sin(x)$. Integrating:

$$V(x) = \int -\sin(x)\,dx = \cos(x) + C$$

Choosing $C = 0$ (or equivalently $C = 1$ to make $V(0) = 0$):

$$V(x) = \cos(x)$$

**Fixed points:** $V'(x) = -\sin(x) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \sin(x) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x^{*} = n\pi,\; n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Stability via $V''(x) = -\cos(x)$:**

- At $x^{*} = 2n\pi$ (even multiples): $V'' = -\cos(0) = -1 < 0$ → **local maximum of $V$** → **unstable fixed point**.
- At $x^{*} = (2n+1)\pi$ (odd multiples): $V'' = -\cos(\pi) = +1 > 0$ → **local minimum of $V$** → **stable fixed point**.

The potential $V(x) = \cos(x)$ is a periodic "egg-carton" landscape with alternating maxima (at $0, \pm 2\pi, \ldots$) and minima (at $\pm\pi, \pm 3\pi, \ldots$).

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**(i) Derivation:** With $\dot{x} = x - x^{3} = -V'(x)$, we have $V'(x) = x^{3} - x$. Integrating:

$$V(x) = \frac{x^{4}}{4} - \frac{x^{2}}{2}$$

**(ii) Fixed points and stability:** $V'(x) = x^{3} - x = x(x-1)(x+1) = 0$ yields:

$$x_{1}^{*} = -1, \quad x_{2}^{*} = 0, \quad x_{3}^{*} = +1$$

The second derivative: $V''(x) = 3x^{2} - 1$.

- $V''(-1) = 3 - 1 = 2 > 0$ → stable (local minimum).
- $V''(0) = 0 - 1 = -1 < 0$ → unstable (local maximum).
- $V''(+1) = 3 - 1 = 2 > 0$ → stable (local minimum).

**(iii) Phase line and potential sketch:**

The potential is a symmetric **double-well** with two minima at $x = \pm 1$ (where $V = \tfrac{1}{4} - \tfrac{1}{2} = -\tfrac{1}{4}$) and a local maximum at $x = 0$ (where $V = 0$). The phase line on the $x$-axis shows flow to the right for $x \in (-\infty,-1) \cup (0,1)$ and to the left for $x \in (-1,0) \cup (1,\infty)$, with stable arrows pointing toward $\pm 1$ and an unstable arrow repelling from $0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign confusion:** Students frequently write $\dot{x} = +V'(x)$ instead of $-V'(x)$. The minus sign is conventional; stick to it consistently to avoid losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant $C$:** $V(x)$ is defined only up to an additive constant. Examiners will *not* deduct for any choice of $C$, but you must show the integration step explicitly.
> 3. **Stability test misapplied:** The condition $V''(x^*) > 0$ implies stable **only** for gradient systems. For general ODEs, you must compute $f'(x^*)$, not $V''(x^*)$.
> 4. **Imaginary fixed points:** When $\mu < 0$ in the Duffing case, students often incorrectly report $\pm\sqrt{\mu}$ as fixed points — they are **complex** and not physical equilibria.
> 5. **Missing the bifurcation parameter:** A complete KTU answer must explicitly state *where* the bifurcation occurs (the critical $\mu_c$) and describe the *qualitative change* in the phase portrait.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A potential $V(x)$ satisfies $\dot{x} = -V'(x)$; in $n$D, $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = -\nabla V(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Construction:** $V(x) = -\int f(x)\,dx + C$, applicable to *any* 1D autonomous system.
- **Fixed points:** Located at critical points of $V$, i.e., $V'(x^*) = 0$.
- **Stability Test (gradient systems only):** $V''(x^*) > 0$ → stable; $V''(x^*) < 0$ → unstable.
- **Lyapunov property:** $dV/dt = -[V'(x)]^{2} \le 0$, so $V$ is monotonically non-increasing.
- **Canonical potentials to memorize:**
  - *Harmonic:* $V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^{2}$ → single stable minimum.
  - *Duffing-type:* $V(x) = \tfrac{1}{4}x^{4} - \tfrac{\mu}{2}x^{2}$ → pitchfork at $\mu = 0$.
  - *Cubic:* $V(x) = \tfrac{1}{3}x^{3} - \mu x$ → saddle-node at $\mu = 0$.
  - *Trigonometric:* $V(x) = \cos(x)$ → periodic infinite sequence of stable/unstable equilibria.
- **Bifurcation Signature:** Whenever $V''(x^*) = 0$, the system is at a *degenerate* fixed point — varying the parameter produces a bifurcation.
- **Limitation:** A scalar potential need not exist for 2D+ systems unless the curl-free condition $\partial F_1/\partial x_2 = \partial F_2/\partial x_1$ holds.
- **Engineering Relevance:** MEMS pull-in, structural buckling, optical bistability, power-system energy methods — all leverage the potential-landscape paradigm.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saddle-Node Bifurcation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Saddle-Node Bifurcation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

A **Saddle-Node Bifurcation** (also called a **fold bifurcation** or **tangent bifurcation**) is a local codimension-one bifurcation of a continuous-time dynamical system in which, as a control parameter $\mu$ crosses a critical threshold $\mu_c$, two equilibrium points (one stable **node** and one unstable **saddle**) approach each other, coalesce into a single **semi-stable** equilibrium, and subsequently annihilate each other — leaving the system with no real equilibrium in that neighborhood for $\mu > \mu_c$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** Saddle-node bifurcation is the *first* and most fundamental local bifurcation students must master, because every other codimension-one bifurcation (transcritical, pitchfork) can be viewed as a collision of two saddle-node structures.

The **canonical normal form** of the saddle-node bifurcation is:

$$
\dot{x} = r + x^{2}
$$

where $r \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bifurcation parameter and $x \in \mathbb{R}$ is the state variable.

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Tilted Valley" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a smooth curved surface shaped like a hill-and-valley pair — a shallow **depression** (potential well = stable node) sitting right next to a **hump** (potential barrier = unstable saddle). Now slowly tilt the surface.
>
> - When the tilt is **small (r < 0):** the well and the hump coexist as two distinct equilibria.
> - At the **critical tilt (r = 0):** the well and hump *kiss* — they become a single horizontal inflection point.
> - When the tilt is **too large (r > 0):** both vanish off the surface entirely. The ball now just rolls away — no equilibrium remains.

This is precisely what happens in **laser threshold turn-on**, where below the pump threshold the lasing mode (stable) coexists with the off-state (unstable), and above threshold the off-state is destroyed.

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Note |
|---|---|---|
| $r$ | Bifurcation parameter | Dimensionless, swept across $\mathbb{R}$ |
| $x^*$ | Equilibrium coordinate | $x^* = \pm\sqrt{-r}$ when $r \le 0$ |
| $r_c$ | Critical bifurcation value | $r_c = 0$ for the canonical form |
| $\lambda$ | Eigenvalue of Jacobian | $\lambda = f'(x^*) = 2x^*$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Logic — The Seven Pillars of Saddle-Node Bifurcation

The following bullet chain captures *exactly* the KTU board examiner's expected reasoning order:

1. **Bifurcation Condition Setup.** A local bifurcation in the 1-D system $\dot{x} = f(x, r)$ requires *two simultaneous* conditions to be satisfied at the bifurcation point $(x_c, r_c)$:
$$
f(x_c, r_c) = 0 \qquad \text{and} \qquad \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\bigg|_{(x_c, r_c)} = 0.
$$
2. **Non-Degeneracy Condition.** The mixed second partial must be non-zero, ensuring the bifurcation is *transversal* and not a higher-codimension event:
$$
\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}}\bigg|_{(x_c, r_c)} \neq 0.
$$
3. **Parameter Slope Condition.** The system must genuinely depend on $r$ at the bifurcation point, i.e.,
$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial r}\bigg|_{(x_c, r_c)} \neq 0.
$$
4. **Normal Form Derivation.** Applying a Taylor expansion of $f$ around $(x_c, r_c)$ and then applying *coordinate changes* (shifts in $x$ and $r$, plus a state-space rescaling), the generic system is reducible to:
$$
\dot{x} = r + x^{2}.
$$
5. **Fixed-Point Solution.** Setting $\dot{x} = 0$ yields $r + x^{2} = 0 \implies x = \pm\sqrt{-r}$. These are real only when $r \le 0$.
6. **Linear Stability.** The Jacobian of the 1-D scalar system is $J = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x$. Hence at the fixed points:
$$
J\big|_{x^*=\pm\sqrt{-r}} = \pm 2\sqrt{-r}.
$$
- $x^* = -\sqrt{-r}$ gives $J = -2\sqrt{-r} < 0$ → **stable node** (attractor).
- $x^* = +\sqrt{-r}$ gives $J = +2\sqrt{-r} > 0$ → **unstable saddle** (repeller).
7. **Critical Transition.** At $r = 0$ the two fixed points collide at $x^* = 0$. The Jacobian there equals zero, giving an **infinitely slow (degenerate)** eigenvalue — this is the hallmark of a saddle-node.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Meaning / Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{x} = r + x^{2}$ | Canonical normal form of saddle-node |
| 2 | $f(x_c, r_c) = 0$ | Fixed-point existence condition |
| 3 | $f_{x}(x_c, r_c) = 0$ | Criticality (zero eigenvalue) condition |
| 4 | $f_{xx}(x_c, r_c) \neq 0$ | Non-degeneracy (genuine quadratic) |
| 5 | $f_{r}(x_c, r_c) \neq 0$ | Parameter transversality condition |
| 6 | $x^* = \pm\sqrt{-r}$ | Equilibrium branches for $r \le 0$ |
| 7 | $\lambda = 2x^*$ | Eigenvalue/Jacobian at equilibrium |
| 8 | $r_c = 0$ | Critical parameter value |
| 9 | $\tau \sim \vert r\vert^{-1/2}$ | Slowing-down timescale near $r=0$ |
| 10 | $\dot{x} = r - x^{2}$ | Alternative (inverted) form — same bifurcation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the system slow down near $r = 0$?** The eigenvalue $\lambda = 2x^* = \pm 2\sqrt{-r} \to 0$ as $r \to 0^-$. In a continuous dynamical system, the relaxation time $\tau = 1/\vert\lambda\vert$ therefore *diverges* — a phenomenon called **critical slowing down**, observed experimentally in optical bistability and superconducting transitions.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Laser Physics:** The lasing threshold is a saddle-node bifurcation — the off-state and on-state of the photon population annihilate at the pump threshold.
- **Buckling of Columns (Euler Buckling):** A vertical column under axial load loses its straight-equilibrium (stable) and a buckled-state (unstable) collide at the critical load $P_{cr} = \pi^{2}EI/L^{2}$.
- **Population Dynamics:** A population at the *Allee threshold* experiences saddle-node extinction — below a critical population, the species goes extinct.
- **Electronic Circuits (Chua's Diode):** Hysteresis loops in memristive elements are built from saddle-node pairs.
- **Mechanical Failure of Shallow Arches:** Snap-through buckling is a textbook saddle-node bifurcation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation A — Reduction to the Canonical Normal Form

We begin with a generic 1-D autonomous system:

$$
\dot{x} = f(x, r)
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the bifurcation point.** Suppose a saddle-node bifurcation occurs at $(x, r) = (0, 0)$ (we can always translate coordinates to achieve this). At this point the function $f$ must vanish and have zero slope:

$$
f(0, 0) = 0, \qquad f_{x}(0, 0) = 0.
$$

**Step 2 — Taylor-expand $f$ in a small neighborhood.** Retaining terms up to second order in $(x, r)$:

$$
f(x, r) = f(0, 0) + f_{x} x + f_{r} r + \tfrac{1}{2} f_{xx} x^{2} + f_{xr} x r + \tfrac{1}{2} f_{rr} r^{2} + \cdots
$$

Substituting the conditions $f(0,0)=0$ and $f_{x}(0,0)=0$:

$$
f(x, r) = f_{r} \, r + \tfrac{1}{2} f_{xx} \, x^{2} + f_{xr} \, x r + \tfrac{1}{2} f_{rr} \, r^{2} + \cdots
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the non-degeneracy assumptions.** Per the KTU 2024 syllabus conditions, we require:

$$
f_{xx}(0, 0) \neq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad f_{r}(0, 0) \neq 0.
$$

So the dominant quadratic-and-linear terms are $f_{r}\, r + \tfrac{1}{2}f_{xx}\, x^{2}$.

**Step 4 — Rescale to canonical form.** Define new variables:

$$
X = \sqrt{\tfrac{\vert f_{xx}\vert}{2}} \, x, \qquad R = f_{r} \, r.
$$

Then the leading-order dynamics read:

$$
\dot{X} = R + X^{2}.
$$

Renaming $X \to x$ and $R \to r$ recovers the **canonical normal form**:

$$
\dot{x} = r + x^{2}.
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every smooth saddle-node bifurcation is *locally topologically equivalent* to this universal equation. This is the content of the **Center Manifold / Normal Form Theorem** of dynamical-systems theory.

---

## Derivation B — Equilibrium Branches and Their Stability

Starting from the canonical normal form:

$$
\dot{x} = r + x^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Set $\dot{x} = 0$ to find equilibria.**

$$
r + x^{*2} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x^{*2} = -r.
$$

**Step 2 — Case analysis on $r$.**

- **Case 1: $r > 0$.** Then $-r < 0$, so $x^{*2} < 0$. No real fixed points exist. The flow $\dot{x} = r + x^{2} > 0$ for all $x$ — the system is **globally monotonically increasing** in $x$.

- **Case 2: $r = 0$.** Then $x^{*2} = 0 \implies x^{*} = 0$ is a *single* fixed point, but with $f'(0) = 0$. It is a **degenerate** (semi-stable) equilibrium.

- **Case 3: $r < 0$.** Then $x^{*2} = -r > 0$, giving **two real equilibria**:
$$
x_1^{*} = -\sqrt{-r}, \qquad x_2^{*} = +\sqrt{-r}.
$$

**Step 3 — Linear stability via the Jacobian $J = \partial f / \partial x = 2x$.**

- At $x_1^* = -\sqrt{-r}$: $J = -2\sqrt{-r} < 0$ → **asymptotically stable** (node).
- At $x_2^* = +\sqrt{-r}$: $J = +2\sqrt{-r} > 0$ → **unstable** (saddle, in 1-D terminology: a repeller).

**Step 4 — Verdict.** A pair consisting of *one stable node* and *one unstable saddle* coalesces at $(x, r) = (0, 0)$ and disappears for $r > 0$. This is the hallmark of a **saddle-node bifurcation**.

---

## Derivation C — Critical Slowing Down Timescale

Near $r = 0^{-}$, write $r = -\varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon > 0$ small. Then:

$$
x^* = \pm\sqrt{\varepsilon}, \qquad \lambda = \pm 2\sqrt{\varepsilon}.
$$

The relaxation time constant for trajectories near either equilibrium is:

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{\vert \lambda \vert} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\varepsilon}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{-r}} \quad \xrightarrow{r \to 0^{-}} \quad \infty.
$$

This **divergence** of the relaxation time is the famous **critical slowing down** phenomenon — a favorite KTU board question!

---

## Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Summary

| Property | Expression / Value | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed-point equation | $x^{*2} + r = 0$ | Algebraic bifurcation equation |
| Real fixed points exist iff | $r \le 0$ | Existence threshold |
| Stable branch | $x_{-}^{*} = -\sqrt{-r}$ | Lower energy / preferred state |
| Unstable branch | $x_{+}^{*} = +\sqrt{-r}$ | Higher energy / barrier state |
| Eigenvalue (stable) | $\lambda_{-} = -2\sqrt{-r}$ | Negative — attractive |
| Eigenvalue (unstable) | $\lambda_{+} = +2\sqrt{-r}$ | Positive — repulsive |
| Type of equilibrium at $r=0$ | $x^* = 0$, $\lambda = 0$ | Semi-stable / degenerate |
| Timescale near $r=0$ | $\tau \sim 1/\sqrt{-r}$ | Critical slowing down |
| Global flow for $r > 0$ | $\dot{x} > 0$ everywhere | No equilibria — pure drift |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram A — Bifurcation Diagram in the $(r, x)$ Plane

The classical saddle-node bifurcation diagram is a *parabola* in the $(r, x)$ plane opening towards the **negative $r$** side, with the upper branch dashed (unstable) and the lower branch solid (stable).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: r greater than 0] --> B[No real fixed points]
    A --> C[Flow is strictly positive]
    C --> D[Trajectories diverge to plus infinity]
    E[r equals 0 Critical Point] --> F[Single equilibrium at x equals 0]
    F --> G[Degenerate semi stable]
    H[r less than 0] --> I[Two equilibria appear]
    I --> J[Stable Node at x equals minus sqrt of minus r]
    I --> K[Unstable Saddle at x equals plus sqrt of minus r]
    J --> L[Solid curve: lower branch]
    K --> M[Dashed curve: upper branch]
    style A fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c00
    style E fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#aa0
    style H fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#0a0
    style J fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#06c
    style K fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#c00
```

## Diagram B — Phase Portrait Evolution Across the Bifurcation

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PhaseA["Phase Portrait r greater than 0"]
        PA1[All arrows point right] --> PA2[No fixed point]
    end
    subgraph PhaseB["Phase Portrait r equals 0"]
        PB1[Arrow from left into zero] --> PB2[Zero is semi stable]
        PB2 --> PB3[Arrow from zero to right]
    end
    subgraph PhaseC["Phase Portrait r less than 0"]
        PC1[Stable node at x less than 0] --> PC2[Trajectories converge]
        PC3[Unstable saddle at x greater than 0] --> PC4[Trajectories diverge]
    end
    style PA1 fill:#ffe5e5
    style PB1 fill:#fff3b0
    style PC1 fill:#bde0fe
    style PC3 fill:#ffb3b3
```

## Diagram C — Functional Architecture: The Mechanism of Coalescence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[System at r less than 0] --> S2[Two equilibria: stable minus and unstable plus]
    S2 --> S3[Parameter r increases toward zero]
    S3 --> S4[Distance between branches shrinks]
    S4 --> S5[Critical point r equals 0 reached]
    S5 --> S6[Branches merge at x equals 0]
    S6 --> S7[Single semi stable equilibrium]
    S7 --> S8[Parameter r passes zero]
    S8 --> S9[Both equilibria vanish]
    S9 --> S10[System now has no fixed point: pure drift]
    style S5 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px
    style S7 fill:#ffd6a5
    style S9 fill:#ffadad
    style S10 fill:#caffbf
```

## Diagram D — Stability Switch on the x-axis

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Left of origin x less than zero] --> B[Negative eigenvalue Stable]
    C[Origin x equals zero] --> D[Zero eigenvalue Semi stable at r equals 0]
    E[Right of origin x greater than zero] --> F[Positive eigenvalue Unstable]
    style B fill:#bde0fe
    style D fill:#fff3b0
    style F fill:#ffb3b3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid (blue) line = stable branch, dashed (red) line = unstable branch, the yellow vertex at $(0,0)$ is the bifurcation point. The parabola opens to the *left* (toward $r < 0$) because the equilibria exist only when $-r \ge 0$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Define a saddle-node bifurcation. State the canonical normal form and the two non-degeneracy conditions that must be satisfied at the bifurcation point.**

**Model Answer:**

A saddle-node bifurcation is a local codimension-one bifurcation in which a pair of fixed points — one stable and one unstable — collide and annihilate each other as a parameter crosses a critical value. The canonical normal form is $\dot{x} = r + x^{2}$. The two non-degeneracy conditions are:

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}}\bigg|_{(0,0)} \neq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial r}\bigg|_{(0,0)} \neq 0.
$$

The first ensures the bifurcation is genuinely quadratic (not higher-order), while the second guarantees the system depends non-trivially on the parameter at the bifurcation point.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Explain the phenomenon of critical slowing down near a saddle-node bifurcation and write the scaling law for the relaxation time.**

**Model Answer:**

As the bifurcation parameter $r$ approaches its critical value $r_c = 0$ from below, the eigenvalue $\lambda$ of the linearized system at the equilibrium goes to zero:

$$
\lambda = \pm 2\sqrt{-r} \;\to\; 0 \quad \text{as} \quad r \to 0^{-}.
$$

The relaxation time, being the inverse magnitude of the eigenvalue, therefore *diverges*:

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{\vert \lambda \vert} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{-r}} \;\to\; \infty.
$$

Physically, the system takes arbitrarily long to settle near its equilibrium as the bifurcation is approached. This is **critical slowing down**, and it is observed in laser threshold turn-on, supercritical phase transitions, and the snapping of shallow arches.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Consider the one-dimensional system $\dot{x} = r + x^{2}$. Find all fixed points as a function of the parameter $r$ and determine their stability. Sketch the bifurcation diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system $\dot{x} = r - x^{2} + x^{3}$, identify the type of bifurcation occurring at the origin and find the critical parameter value. Discuss what happens for $r > 0$ and $r < 0$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Find fixed points.** Setting $\dot{x} = 0$:

$$
r + x^{*2} = 0 \implies x^{*2} = -r.
$$

**Step 2 — Case analysis.**
- For $r > 0$: $-r < 0$, so no real fixed points exist.
- For $r = 0$: single fixed point $x^* = 0$ (degenerate, since $f'(0) = 0$).
- For $r < 0$: two real fixed points $x_1^* = -\sqrt{-r}$ and $x_2^* = +\sqrt{-r}$.

**Step 3 — Stability via Jacobian.** $J = f'(x) = 2x$.

- $J\big|_{x_1^*} = -2\sqrt{-r} < 0$ → **asymptotically stable** (node).
- $J\big|_{x_2^*} = +2\sqrt{-r} > 0$ → **unstable** (saddle/repeller).

**Step 4 — Bifurcation diagram sketch.** A parabola opening leftward in the $(r, x)$ plane with vertex at the origin, lower branch solid (stable), upper branch dashed (unstable). The branches exist only for $r \le 0$.

> **Bifurcation type:** Saddle-node bifurcation at $r_c = 0$, $x_c = 0$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Verify the bifurcation conditions at $(x, r) = (0, 0)$.**

Let $f(x, r) = r - x^{2} + x^{3}$. Then:

$$
f(0, 0) = 0, \qquad f_x(x, r) = -2x + 3x^{2}, \qquad f_x(0, 0) = 0.
$$

So the origin is indeed a candidate bifurcation point.

**Step 2 — Check non-degeneracy.**

$$
f_{xx}(x, r) = -2 + 6x, \qquad f_{xx}(0, 0) = -2 \neq 0.
$$

$$
f_r(0, 0) = 1 \neq 0.
$$

Both non-degeneracy conditions are satisfied.

**Step 3 — Classify.** Since $f(0,0) = 0$, $f_x(0,0) = 0$, $f_{xx}(0,0) = -2 \neq 0$, and $f_r(0,0) = 1 \neq 0$, this is a **saddle-node bifurcation** at $r_c = 0$.

**Step 4 — Behavior for $r > 0$ and $r < 0$.**

- **For $r > 0$:** $f(x, 0^+) = r - x^{2} + x^{3}$. The cubic term dominates for large $x$, so $f > 0$ at both extremes. Numerically, solving $f = 0$ for $r = 0.1$ shows a real root at $x \approx 0.302$ and a complex conjugate pair — exactly one real fixed point.
- **For $r < 0$:** $f(x, 0^-) = r - x^{2} + x^{3}$ has three real roots — the saddle-node bifurcation at $r = 0$ has *created* two new equilibria (one stable, one unstable) as $r$ decreased through zero.

The bifurcation diagram in the $(r, x)$ plane shows a parabola-like fold for the dominant quadratic term $r - x^2$, perturbed by the small cubic $x^3$ term.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** State the conditions for a saddle-node bifurcation in a 1-D system $\dot{x} = f(x, r)$ and use them to identify the bifurcation in $\dot{x} = r + e^{x}$. Find the critical point and the equilibria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the canonical normal form $\dot{x} = r + x^{2}$, derive the scaling law for the relaxation time near the bifurcation and explain the engineering significance in laser threshold turn-on. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — State the conditions.** A saddle-node bifurcation in $\dot{x} = f(x, r)$ at the point $(x_c, r_c)$ requires:

$$
f(x_c, r_c) = 0, \qquad f_x(x_c, r_c) = 0, \qquad f_{xx}(x_c, r_c) \neq 0, \qquad f_r(x_c, r_c) \neq 0.
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to $f(x, r) = r + e^{x}$.** The equations $f = 0$ and $f_x = 0$ are:

$$
r + e^{x} = 0 \quad (1)
$$
$$
e^{x} = 0 \quad (2)
$$

Equation (2) has *no real solution* because $e^x > 0$ for all real $x$. Therefore, **no real saddle-node bifurcation exists** in this system. (The complex saddle-node would occur at $r_c = 0$, $x_c \to -\infty$.)

**Step 3 — Therefore,** for any real $r$, $f(x, r) = r + e^{x}$ has a single real root $x^* = \ln(-r)$ *only when* $r < 0$. Since $f_x(x^*) = e^{x^*} = -r > 0$, this unique equilibrium is always **unstable** (no pair of equilibria can form). Thus the system exhibits *no* saddle-node bifurcation on the real line — a subtle but important point.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Trap:** Students often mechanically set $f_x = 0$ and forget to check whether the resulting $x_c$ is *real*. In the system $\dot{x} = r + e^x$, the equation $e^x = 0$ has no real solution, so the system has *no real saddle-node bifurcation*. Marks are lost for claiming $r_c = 0$ and $x_c = -\infty$ without the caveat that the bifurcation is in the *complex* plane.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Eigenvalue near the equilibrium.** For $\dot{x} = r + x^{2}$, the equilibria are $x_{\pm}^{*} = \pm\sqrt{-r}$ for $r < 0$. The Jacobian is:

$$
J = f'(x) = 2x.
$$

At the stable branch $x_{-}^{*} = -\sqrt{-r}$:

$$
\lambda_{-} = -2\sqrt{-r}.
$$

**Step 2 — Define the relaxation time.** For a linear system $\dot{\xi} = \lambda \xi$, the deviation decays as $e^{\lambda t}$, so the characteristic relaxation time is:

$$
\tau = \frac{1}{\vert \lambda \vert} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{-r}}.
$$

**Step 3 — Critical slowing down.** As $r \to 0^{-}$, $-r \to 0^{+}$, hence:

$$
\tau \sim \frac{1}{2\sqrt{-r}} \;\to\; \infty.
$$

This is the **square-root divergence** of the relaxation time.

**Step 4 — Engineering significance in laser turn-on.** In a single-mode laser below threshold, the off-state and on-state coexist as a saddle-node pair. As the pump parameter is increased toward threshold ($r \to 0^-$), the relaxation time of fluctuations around the on-state diverges as $1/\sqrt{-r}$. Experimentally this manifests as **giant intensity fluctuations** and **1/f-type noise** in semiconductor lasers near threshold — a measurable signature of the underlying saddle-node bifurcation.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Five Ways KTU Students Lose Marks on Saddle-Node Bifurcation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the parameter transversality condition** $f_r(x_c, r_c) \neq 0$. The system $\dot{x} = x^2$ has $f_r = 0$ identically — it is *not* a bifurcation at all, just a degenerate equilibrium!
> 2. **Confusing the sign convention.** The canonical form $\dot{x} = r + x^2$ has equilibria only for $r \le 0$. Students sometimes write "for $r \ge 0$" — instantly losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up saddle-node with pitchfork.** A pitchfork has a *symmetry* axis and three branches; a saddle-node has *two* branches only. Always count branches first.
> 4. **Omitting the stability analysis.** A complete KTU answer must include the Jacobian evaluation at *both* equilibrium branches.
> 5. **Missing the critical slowing down.** When asked about timescales, students often answer "the system slows down" without writing the explicit $\tau \sim 1/\sqrt{-r}$ scaling law.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **canonical normal form** of a saddle-node bifurcation is $\dot{x} = r + x^2$.
- Equilibria exist for $r \le 0$ only: $x^* = \pm\sqrt{-r}$.
- The lower branch ($x^* = -\sqrt{-r}$) is **stable**; the upper branch ($x^* = +\sqrt{-r}$) is **unstable**.
- The bifurcation point is $(x_c, r_c) = (0, 0)$.
- The eigenvalue at equilibrium is $\lambda = 2x^* = \pm 2\sqrt{-r}$, which **tends to zero** as $r \to 0^-$.
- The relaxation time scales as $\tau \sim 1/\sqrt{-r}$ — this is **critical slowing down**.
- The four non-degeneracy conditions are: $f = 0$, $f_x = 0$, $f_{xx} \neq 0$, $f_r \neq 0$.
- Real-world examples: **laser threshold**, **Euler buckling**, **Allee effect in ecology**, **snap-through of arches**, **Chua's circuit hysteresis**.
- For the inverted form $\dot{x} = r - x^2$, the parabola opens *rightward* (toward $r > 0$) — the sign of the $x^2$ term determines which side of parameter space has equilibria.
- In 2-D systems, the saddle-node bifurcation creates a **saddle-node equilibrium**, which has one real eigenvalue passing through zero and one remaining nonzero.
- Master the Taylor expansion + coordinate rescaling derivation — it is the *single most testable* skill in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transcritical and Pitchfork Bifurcations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transcritical and Pitchfork Bifurcations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Transcritical Bifurcation

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Transcritical Bifurcation** is a codimension-1 local bifurcation in which two branches of fixed points (equilibria) **collide** at the bifurcation point and **exchange their linear stability**. Both branches exist on **both sides** of the critical parameter value, which is the defining geometric feature of this bifurcation.

The canonical one-dimensional normal form of the transcritical bifurcation is:

$$\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{2}$$

where $x \in \mathbb{R}$ is the state variable and $\mu \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bifurcation (control) parameter.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a see-saw with two children of equal weight sitting on either end.** As the children shift positions, the pivot transfers the "down" side from one end to the other. Neither child leaves the see-saw; they simply trade roles. In a transcritical bifurcation, the two equilibrium branches never vanish — they just swap which one is "stable" (heavy side) and which is "unstable" (light side).

### Key Characteristics
- The two equilibria $x^{*}_{1}=0$ and $x^{*}_{2}=\mu$ are **always simultaneously real**.
- At the critical value $\mu_{c}=0$, they coincide and exchange stability.
- The eigenvalue condition at bifurcation is $f'(x^{*})=\mu=0$, with $f''(0)=-2 \neq 0$.

---

## 1.2 Pitchfork Bifurcation

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Pitchfork Bifurcation** is a codimension-1 local bifurcation, generic for systems with a built-in symmetry $x \leftrightarrow -x$, in which a symmetric equilibrium loses stability and gives rise to a **symmetric pair** of new equilibria. It has two sub-families: **supercritical** (stable new branches) and **subcritical** (unstable new branches).

The **supercritical** normal form is:

$$\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{3} = x(\mu - x^{2})$$

The **subcritical** normal form is:

$$\dot{x} = \mu x + x^{3} = x(\mu + x^{2})$$

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a ball balanced exactly on top of a smooth hill (μ < 0 — stable) versus sitting in a valley between two hills (μ > 0 — unstable at the peak).** When the wind (parameter μ) shifts, the ball cannot stay at the peak forever. In a **supercritical** pitchfork, two new valleys appear softly and the ball rolls into one of them — a graceful, continuous birth of new states. In a **subcritical** pitchfork, the unstable peaks already existed on the other side of the hill and slam into the original equilibrium catastrophically, leading to a large jump.

### Key Characteristics
- Reflects the symmetry $f(-x,\mu) = -f(x,\mu)$ (odd function in $x$).
- The bifurcation value is $\mu_{c}=0$.
- The non-linear coefficient of $x^{3}$ determines the super- vs. sub-critical nature: **negative** $x^{3}$ coefficient → supercritical; **positive** $x^{3}$ coefficient → subcritical.
- Supercritical pitchforks are **generic** and "safe"; subcritical pitchforks are typically **non-generic** and often associated with catastrophic jumps and hysteresis.

---

## 1.3 Comparison Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three codimension-1 bifurcations in this module — saddle-node, transcritical, and pitchfork — share the normal-form structure $\dot{x} = \mu x \pm x^{2} \pm x^{3}$, but differ in **how many equilibria cross the bifurcation and whether they vanish, exchange, or are born in pairs.**

| Property | Transcritical | Supercritical Pitchfork | Subcritical Pitchfork |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal form | $\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{2}$ | $\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{3}$ | $\dot{x} = \mu x + x^{3}$ |
| Equilibria at $\mu>0$ | $x^{*}=0$ (U), $x^{*}=\mu$ (S) | $x^{*}=0$ (U), $x^{*}=\pm\sqrt{\mu}$ (S) | $x^{*}=0$ (U) only |
| Equilibria at $\mu<0$ | $x^{*}=0$ (S), $x^{*}=\mu$ (U) | $x^{*}=0$ (S) only | $x^{*}=0$ (S), $x^{*}=\pm\sqrt{-\mu}$ (U) |
| Symmetry required | No (universal) | Yes ($x \to -x$) | Yes ($x \to -x$) |
| (S)=stable, (U)=unstable |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transcritical Bifurcation — Detailed Mechanics

### Step-by-Step Analysis of $\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{2}$

- **Step 1 — Identify the equilibria.** Setting $\dot{x}=0$:
$$x(\mu - x) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x^{*}_{1} = 0, \quad x^{*}_{2} = \mu.$$
- **Step 2 — Linear stability analysis.** Compute $f'(x) = \mu - 2x$.
  * At $x^{*}_{1} = 0$: $f'(0) = \mu$. Stable if $\mu<0$, unstable if $\mu>0$.
  * At $x^{*}_{2} = \mu$: $f'(\mu) = \mu - 2\mu = -\mu$. Stable if $\mu>0$, unstable if $\mu<0$.
- **Step 3 — Exchange of stability.** At $\mu=0$, both eigenvalues pass through zero, and the two branches **cross** the $\mu$-axis and swap stability — neither branch disappears.
- **Step 4 — Why?** A transcritical bifurcation requires the constant term of the Taylor expansion to vanish at $x=0$ AND the linear term to vanish at $\mu=0$. This double-zero condition forces two branches to "share" the bifurcation point.

### Engineering & Real-World Utility
- **Laser threshold (mode competition):** Two competing optical modes exchange dominance at the lasing threshold.
- **Population dynamics:** Two species (predator vs. prey, or competing for the same niche) cross critical density thresholds and exchange dominance.
- **Mechanical buckling with imperfections:** When a slender column is loaded, the trivial (straight) and non-trivial (buckled) solutions exchange stability.

---

## 2.2 Supercritical Pitchfork — Detailed Mechanics

### Step-by-Step Analysis of $\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{3}$

- **Step 1 — Equilibria.** Set $x(\mu - x^{2}) = 0$:
$$x^{*}_{1} = 0, \qquad x^{*}_{2,3} = \pm\sqrt{\mu}.$$
The pair $\pm\sqrt{\mu}$ is **real only when $\mu \ge 0$**.
- **Step 2 — Linear stability.** $f'(x) = \mu - 3x^{2}$.
  * At $x^{*}_{1}=0$: $f'(0) = \mu$. Stable for $\mu<0$, unstable for $\mu>0$.
  * At $x^{*}_{2,3}=\pm\sqrt{\mu}$: $f'(\pm\sqrt{\mu}) = \mu - 3\mu = -2\mu$. Stable for $\mu>0$, **non-existent** (complex) for $\mu<0$.
- **Step 3 — Bifurcation picture.** For $\mu<0$, the origin is a unique, stable equilibrium. As $\mu$ crosses $0$ smoothly, the origin destabilizes and **two new stable equilibria** are born continuously out of the origin. The new branches form the iconic "pitchfork" shape.
- **Step 4 — Why supercritical?** Because the cubic coefficient is **negative**, the new branches bend *toward* the stable side, ensuring the post-bifurcation dynamics are bounded and well-behaved.

### Engineering & Real-World Utility
- **Convection onset (Rayleigh–Bénard):** When heating a fluid layer beyond a critical Rayleigh number, the symmetric conductive state loses stability to a pair of stable convective rolls rotating in opposite senses.
- **Buckling of symmetric columns:** A perfectly symmetric column under symmetric axial load bifurcates into two mirror-image bent configurations.
- **Superconductivity (Ginzburg–Landau):** The order parameter $\psi$ exhibits a pitchfork as the temperature crosses $T_{c}$, with the symmetric phase becoming unstable to a pair of symmetry-broken states.
- **Optical bistability:** A pair of stable output intensities emerges from a single symmetric branch in certain nonlinear optical cavities.

---

## 2.3 Subcritical Pitchfork — Detailed Mechanics

### Step-by-Step Analysis of $\dot{x} = \mu x + x^{3}$

- **Step 1 — Equilibria.** Set $x(\mu + x^{2}) = 0$:
$$x^{*}_{1} = 0, \qquad x^{*}_{2,3} = \pm\sqrt{-\mu}.$$
The pair is real only when $\mu \le 0$.
- **Step 2 — Linear stability.** $f'(x) = \mu + 3x^{2}$.
  * At $x^{*}_{1}=0$: $f'(0) = \mu$. Stable for $\mu<0$, unstable for $\mu>0$.
  * At $x^{*}_{2,3}=\pm\sqrt{-\mu}$ (only for $\mu<0$): $f' = \mu + 3(-\mu) = -2\mu > 0$. **Unstable**.
- **Step 3 — Bifurcation picture.** For $\mu<0$, the origin is stable, but two **unstable** symmetric branches exist in the surrounding state space. As $\mu \to 0^{-}$, these unstable branches collide with the stable origin, turning it unstable for any $\mu>0$. The system then jumps far away to a remote attractor (catastrophic).
- **Step 4 — Why dangerous?** The cubic coefficient is **positive**, so the new branches bend *away* from stability. There is no local attractor on the $\mu>0$ side, forcing a finite-amplitude jump.

### Engineering & Real-World Utility
- **Subcritical convection (rotating Rayleigh–Bénard):** Heating rate must *exceed* the threshold for convection, but the system remains in the conductive state until finite perturbations trigger a sudden jump.
- **Buckling of imperfect columns:** Even for subcritical loads, sufficiently large imperfections can trigger large-amplitude buckling — a classic engineering hazard.
- **Shear-induced turbulence:** Subcritical transitions in pipe flow are notoriously hard to predict and can cause sudden turbulent bursts.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important thing in any bifurcation problem is the sign of the leading nonlinear term in the Taylor expansion about the bifurcation point.** Negative $x^{3}$ coefficient ⇒ supercritical (safe); Positive $x^{3}$ coefficient ⇒ subcritical (catastrophic).

| Quantity | Transcritical | Supercritical Pitchfork | Subcritical Pitchfork |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal form | $\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{2}$ | $\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{3}$ | $\dot{x} = \mu x + x^{3}$ |
| Bifurcation value $\mu_{c}$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Equilibria $x^{*}$ | $0,\ \mu$ | $0,\ \pm\sqrt{\mu}$ | $0,\ \pm\sqrt{-\mu}$ |
| Existence (all $\mu$) | always | $\pm\sqrt{\mu}$ only for $\mu \ge 0$ | $\pm\sqrt{-\mu}$ only for $\mu \le 0$ |
| $f'(0)$ | $\mu$ | $\mu$ | $\mu$ |
| $f'(x^{*}_{2,3})$ | $-\mu$ | $-2\mu$ | $-2\mu$ |
| Stability of $0$ | flips at $\mu=0$ | flips at $\mu=0$ | flips at $\mu=0$ |
| Stability of side branches | flips at $\mu=0$ | stable for $\mu>0$ | unstable for $\mu<0$ |
| Symmetry $x \leftrightarrow -x$ | not required | required | required |
| $x^{3}$ coefficient sign | — (only $x^{2}$ term) | negative | positive |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Transcritical Bifurcation: Exhaustive Derivation

Consider the canonical form:

$$\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{2} \equiv f(x,\mu).$$

**Derivation Step 1 — Find equilibrium branches.** Equilibria satisfy $f(x,\mu) = 0$, which we factor by inspection (recognizing that the bifurcation point is a double root when $\mu=0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x,\mu) &= \mu x - x^{2} \\
&= x(\mu - x) = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

The two equilibrium branches are therefore:

$$x^{*}_{1}(\mu) = 0, \qquad x^{*}_{2}(\mu) = \mu.$$

**Derivation Step 2 — Linearization (Jacobian / derivative).** For a 1D system, stability is determined by the sign of $f'(x^{*}) = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\big\vert_{x^{*}}$. Differentiating:

$$f'(x) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(\mu x - x^{2}) = \mu - 2x.$$

**Derivation Step 3 — Evaluate at each branch.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(0) &= \mu - 2(0) = \mu, \\
f'(\mu) &= \mu - 2\mu = -\mu.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation Step 4 — Sign analysis.**

- $f'(0) = \mu < 0 \iff \mu < 0$ ⇒ $x^{*}_{1} = 0$ is **stable** for $\mu<0$.
- $f'(0) = \mu > 0 \iff \mu > 0$ ⇒ $x^{*}_{1} = 0$ is **unstable** for $\mu>0$.
- $f'(\mu) = -\mu < 0 \iff \mu > 0$ ⇒ $x^{*}_{2} = \mu$ is **stable** for $\mu>0$.
- $f'(\mu) = -\mu > 0 \iff \mu < 0$ ⇒ $x^{*}_{2} = \mu$ is **unstable** for $\mu<0$.

**Derivation Step 5 — Conclusion.** The two equilibrium branches intersect at the bifurcation point $(\mu,x) = (0,0)$, where **both** eigenvalues equal zero. The branches exchange stability at $\mu = 0$.

---

## 3.2 Supercritical Pitchfork: Exhaustive Derivation

Consider the canonical form:

$$\dot{x} = \mu x - x^{3} = f(x,\mu).$$

**Derivation Step 1 — Find equilibrium branches.** Factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x,\mu) &= \mu x - x^{3} \\
&= x(\mu - x^{2}) = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

The three equilibrium branches are:

$$x^{*}_{1} = 0, \qquad x^{*}_{2} = +\sqrt{\mu}, \qquad x^{*}_{3} = -\sqrt{\mu}.$$

Note that $x^{*}_{2,3}$ are **real** only when $\mu \ge 0$, and are **complex conjugates** for $\mu < 0$ (so no real side-branch equilibria exist there).

**Derivation Step 2 — Linearization.** Differentiate $f$ with respect to $x$:

$$f'(x) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(\mu x - x^{3}) = \mu - 3x^{2}.$$

**Derivation Step 3 — Evaluate at $x^{*}_{1} = 0$.**

$$f'(0) = \mu - 0 = \mu.$$

- Stable for $\mu < 0$, unstable for $\mu > 0$.

**Derivation Step 4 — Evaluate at $x^{*}_{2,3} = \pm\sqrt{\mu}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(\pm\sqrt{\mu}) &= \mu - 3(\pm\sqrt{\mu})^{2} \\
&= \mu - 3\mu \\
&= -2\mu.
\end{aligned}
$$

- For $\mu > 0$: $-2\mu < 0$ ⇒ **stable**.
- For $\mu = 0$: $f' = 0$ (marginal — bifurcation point).
- For $\mu < 0$: $x^{*}_{2,3}$ are not real.

**Derivation Step 5 — Center-manifold / amplitude equation (optional KTU extension).** Setting $\dot{x} = 0$ and writing $x = \sqrt{\mu}\,\rho$ for the steady-state amplitude, we find $\rho = \pm 1$ when $\mu > 0$. Near the bifurcation, the slow dynamics on the center manifold obey $\dot{\rho} = \mu\rho - \rho^{3}$, confirming the bifurcation is **supercritical** because the new equilibria appear on the side $\mu>0$ where the original equilibrium has lost stability.

**Derivation Step 6 — Conclusion.** The supercritical pitchfork is a **soft, safe** bifurcation: the new stable equilibria emerge continuously and absorb the system trajectories without jumps.

---

## 3.3 Subcritical Pitchfork: Exhaustive Derivation

Consider the canonical form:

$$\dot{x} = \mu x + x^{3} = f(x,\mu).$$

**Derivation Step 1 — Find equilibrium branches.** Factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x,\mu) &= \mu x + x^{3} \\
&= x(\mu + x^{2}) = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

The three equilibrium branches are:

$$x^{*}_{1} = 0, \qquad x^{*}_{2} = +\sqrt{-\mu}, \qquad x^{*}_{3} = -\sqrt{-\mu}.$$

Note that $x^{*}_{2,3}$ are **real** only when $\mu \le 0$.

**Derivation Step 2 — Linearization.** Differentiate $f$ with respect to $x$:

$$f'(x) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(\mu x + x^{3}) = \mu + 3x^{2}.$$

**Derivation Step 3 — Evaluate at $x^{*}_{1} = 0$.**

$$f'(0) = \mu.$$

- Stable for $\mu < 0$, unstable for $\mu > 0$.

**Derivation Step 4 — Evaluate at $x^{*}_{2,3} = \pm\sqrt{-\mu}$ (only for $\mu \le 0$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(\pm\sqrt{-\mu}) &= \mu + 3(\pm\sqrt{-\mu})^{2} \\
&= \mu + 3(-\mu) \\
&= \mu - 3\mu \\
&= -2\mu.
\end{aligned}
$$

- For $\mu < 0$: $-2\mu > 0$ ⇒ **unstable**.
- For $\mu = 0$: $f' = 0$ (marginal).

**Derivation Step 5 — Catastrophic jump analysis.** For $\mu > 0$, the origin is the **only** real equilibrium, and it is **unstable**. Trajectories starting near $x=0$ escape to $\pm\infty$ (or to a remote attractor not captured by the local normal form). The amplitude of escape is large because the unstable side branches that existed for $\mu<0$ have collided with the origin and annihilated its stability. This is the hallmark of a **first-order / discontinuous** (catastrophic) transition.

**Derivation Step 6 — Hysteresis (KTU high-yield remark).** If the system includes a small stabilizing quintic term, e.g., $\dot{x} = \mu x + x^{3} - x^{5}$, the subcritical pitchfork becomes a **subcritical pitchfork with fold** and exhibits **hysteresis**: the transition $\mu_{c}^{(1)}$ (forward) and $\mu_{c}^{(2)} < \mu_{c}^{(1)}$ (backward) differ, creating a bistable region. This is the key mechanism behind catastrophic mechanical buckling.

**Derivation Step 7 — Conclusion.** The subcritical pitchfork is a **hard, dangerous** bifurcation: a small parameter change can trigger a large, finite-amplitude jump, often with engineering consequences (sudden buckling, sudden turbulence onset, sudden laser mode-locking).

---

## 3.4 Stability Summary Table (with eigenvalues)

| Bifurcation | Branch | Eigenvalue $\lambda = f'(x^{*})$ | Sign $\Rightarrow$ Stability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transcritical | $x^{*}=0$ | $\mu$ | $\mu<0$ Stable, $\mu>0$ Unstable |
| Transcritical | $x^{*}=\mu$ | $-\mu$ | $\mu>0$ Stable, $\mu<0$ Unstable |
| Supercritical PF | $x^{*}=0$ | $\mu$ | $\mu<0$ Stable, $\mu>0$ Unstable |
| Supercritical PF | $x^{*}=\pm\sqrt{\mu}$ | $-2\mu$ | $\mu>0$ Stable (else complex) |
| Subcritical PF | $x^{*}=0$ | $\mu$ | $\mu<0$ Stable, $\mu>0$ Unstable |
| Subcritical PF | $x^{*}=\pm\sqrt{-\mu}$ | $-2\mu$ | $\mu<0$ Unstable (else complex) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid does not have a native "bifurcation diagram" shape, so each diagram is rendered as a **labeled block-level functional architecture flow** showing the state-space branches, parameter regimes, and stability assignment — exactly the structure students reproduce in KTU exam answers.

## 4.1 Transcritical Bifurcation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["AXIS mu equal to zero point BIFURCATION POINT x=0, mu=0"] --> B["REGION mu less than zero LEFT REGIME"]
    A --> C["REGION mu greater than zero RIGHT REGIME"]
    B --> D["BRANCH xstar1 = 0 STABLE solid line"]
    B --> E["BRANCH xstar2 = mu UNSTABLE dashed line"]
    C --> F["BRANCH xstar1 = 0 UNSTABLE dashed line"]
    C --> G["BRANCH xstar2 = mu STABLE solid line"]
    D -. "exchange stability at mu=0" .-> F
    E -. "exchange stability at mu=0" .-> G
```

**Reading the diagram:** the two branches $x^{*}=0$ and $x^{*}=\mu$ coexist for all $\mu$ and meet at the bifurcation point $(0,0)$. The solid (stable) and dashed (unstable) labels switch sides as $\mu$ passes through zero.

---

## 4.2 Supercritical Pitchfork — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["AXIS mu equal to zero point BIFURCATION POINT x=0, mu=0"] --> B["REGION mu less than zero LEFT REGIME"]
    A --> C["REGION mu greater than zero RIGHT REGIME"]
    B --> D["BRANCH xstar1 = 0 STABLE solid line"]
    C --> E["BRANCH xstar1 = 0 UNSTABLE dashed line"]
    C --> F["BRANCH xstar2 = +sqrt mu STABLE solid line"]
    C --> G["BRANCH xstar3 = -sqrt mu STABLE solid line"]
    E -. "destabilizes" .-> F
    E -. "destabilizes" .-> G
    F -. "symmetry pair" .-> G
```

**Reading the diagram:** the central branch $x=0$ is the only equilibrium for $\mu<0$ (stable). At $\mu=0$, two symmetric stable branches $x=\pm\sqrt{\mu}$ "fork out" of the origin, forming the pitchfork silhouette. The cubic term is negative, ensuring the new branches are stable.

---

## 4.3 Subcritical Pitchfork — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["AXIS mu equal to zero point BIFURCATION POINT x=0, mu=0"] --> B["REGION mu less than zero LEFT REGIME"]
    A --> C["REGION mu greater than zero RIGHT REGIME"]
    B --> D["BRANCH xstar1 = 0 STABLE solid line"]
    B --> E["BRANCH xstar2 = +sqrt -mu UNSTABLE dashed line"]
    B --> F["BRANCH xstar3 = -sqrt -mu UNSTABLE dashed line"]
    C --> G["BRANCH xstar1 = 0 UNSTABLE dashed line ONLY REAL EQUILIBRIUM"]
    D -. "collides with unstable side branches at mu=0" .-> G
    E -. "merges into origin at mu=0" .-> G
    F -. "merges into origin at mu=0" .-> G
```

**Reading the diagram:** the unstable side branches $x=\pm\sqrt{-\mu}$ exist for $\mu<0$ and lean *toward* the origin. At $\mu=0$ they annihilate the central stable equilibrium, leaving the origin **unstable and isolated** for all $\mu>0$. Trajectories are forced to escape to large amplitudes — a catastrophic jump.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Bifurcation Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["START Codimension-1 Bifurcation in 1D"] --> Q1{"Does x=0 remain a real equilibrium for ALL mu?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> T["TRANSCRITICAL: 2 branches that exchange stability"]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{"Does the cubic x^3 coefficient have negative or positive sign?"}
    Q2 -- NEGATIVE --> SP["SUPERCRITICAL PITCHFORK: new STABLE branches born at mu=0"]
    Q2 -- POSITIVE --> SB["SUBCRITICAL PITCHFORK: new UNSTABLE branches collide with origin at mu=0, causing catastrophic jump"]
```

This is the **decision-flowchart** to use in the KTU exam when classifying any given 1D normal form: first check whether the zero equilibrium persists, then check the sign of the cubic, and you immediately have the bifurcation type.
