# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define a *flow on the circle*. State the general differential equation governing such a flow and specify the periodicity condition on the vector field.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A flow on the circle is a continuous-time dynamical system whose state variable $\theta$ is confined to the unit circle $\mathbb{S}^{1} = \{\theta \in \mathbb{R} : 0 \le \theta < 2\pi\}$. It is governed by the first-order autonomous ODE:

$$\dot{\theta} = f(\theta)$$

The vector field $f$ must be **$2\pi$-periodic**, i.e., $f(\theta + 2\pi) \equiv f(\theta)$ for all $\theta \in \mathbb{R}$. This periodicity encodes the topological identification of the endpoints $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = 2\pi$, ensuring that trajectories that cross $2\pi$ re-enter at $0$.

> *(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the periodicity condition.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State the **Poincaré index theorem** for a flow on a closed curve. What does it imply about the number of stable and unstable fixed points on $\mathbb{S}^{1}$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Poincaré index theorem states that for a continuous tangent vector field on a closed curve $\gamma$, the algebraic sum of the indices of all isolated zeros equals the Euler characteristic $\chi(\gamma)$:

$$\sum_{i} \text{Index}(f, \theta_{i}^{*}) = \chi(\gamma)$$

For $\gamma = \mathbb{S}^{1}$, we have $\chi(\mathbb{S}^{1}) = 0$. Therefore, $\sum_{i} \text{Index}(f, \theta_{i}^{*}) = 0$. Since each stable fixed point has index $+1$ and each unstable fixed point has index $-1$, the **number of stable fixed points must equal the number of unstable fixed points**. There cannot exist a single isolated fixed point on $\mathbb{S}^{1}$.

> *(Allocation: 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for $\chi(\mathbb{S}^{1}) = 0$, 1 mark for the balance implication.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically features an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Each contains two sub-parts, commonly allocated **7 marks + 7 marks**.

---

### Question A `(14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q(a) [7 marks]:** Consider the system $\dot{\theta} = a + \sin\theta$, where $a \in \mathbb{R}$ is a parameter. Determine the fixed points as functions of $a$ and classify their stability. Identify the bifurcation value(s) of $a$.

**Q(b) [7 marks]:** For the system $\dot{\theta} = a - \sin\theta$ with $a > 1$, derive the period of one full revolution $T$ as a closed-form expression in $a$. Compute the rotation number $\rho$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Fixed-point analysis:**

Set $a + \sin\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \sin\theta = -a$. Real solutions exist iff $\vert a \vert \le 1$.

- $\theta_{1}^{*} = -\arcsin(a) \quad \text{(in } [-\pi/2,\, \pi/2]\text{)}$.
- $\theta_{2}^{*} = \pi + \arcsin(a) \quad \text{(in } [\pi/2,\, 3\pi/2]\text{)}$.

Stability from $f'(\theta) = \cos\theta$:

- $f'(\theta_{1}^{*}) = \cos(\arcsin a) = \sqrt{1-a^{2}} > 0 \Rightarrow$ **unstable**.
- $f'(\theta_{2}^{*}) = \cos(\pi + \arcsin a) = -\sqrt{1-a^{2}} < 0 \Rightarrow$ **stable**.

Bifurcation values: $a = +1$ and $a = -1$. At these points, the discriminant vanishes, the two fixed points coalesce, and $f'(\theta^{*}) = 0$. This is a **saddle-node bifurcation**. For $\vert a \vert > 1$, no fixed points exist; trajectories circulate monotonically.

Index check: $+1 + (-1) = 0 = \chi(\mathbb{S}^{1})$ ✓.

> *(Mark split: 2 marks for fixed points, 3 marks for stability, 2 marks for bifurcation identification.)*

**(b) Period derivation:**

For $a > 1$, the function $a - \sin\theta > 0$ for all $\theta$, so every trajectory rotates monotonically. The period is:

$$T = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{d\theta}{a - \sin\theta}$$

Using the Weierstrass substitution $t = \tan(\theta/2)$ with $\sin\theta = \dfrac{2t}{1+t^{2}}$ and $d\theta = \dfrac{2\,dt}{1+t^{2}}$,

$$T = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{2\,dt}{a(1+t^{2}) - 2t} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{2\,dt}{a\,t^{2} - 2t + a}$$

Complete the square: $a t^{2} - 2t + a = a\left(t - \dfrac{1}{a}\right)^{2} + \dfrac{a^{2}-1}{a}$. Substituting $u = \sqrt{a}\left(t - \dfrac{1}{a}\right)$,

$$T = \frac{2}{\sqrt{a}}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\frac{du}{u^{2} + (a^{2}-1)} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{a}}\cdot \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{a^{2}-1}} = \boxed{\frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{a^{2}-1}}}$$

The rotation number is:

$$\rho = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \sqrt{a^{2}-1}$$

> *(Mark split: 4 marks for integral setup and substitution, 2 marks for evaluation, 1 mark for rotation number.)*

---

### Question B `(14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q(a) [7 marks]:** Discuss the qualitative dynamics of the overdamped pendulum $\dot{\theta} = -\sin\theta$ on $\mathbb{S}^{1}$. Identify all fixed points, classify their stability, compute their topological indices, and describe the heteroclinic orbit structure.

**Q(b) [7 marks]:** State and prove the Poincaré index theorem for flows on $\mathbb{S}^{1}$. Use it to show that the equation $\dot{\theta} = a + \sin\theta$ cannot have exactly one fixed point.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Qualitative dynamics of $\dot{\theta} = -\sin\theta$:**

The vector field is $f(\theta) = -\sin\theta$, which is $2\pi$-periodic and smooth.

**Fixed points:** Solve $-\sin\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \sin\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \theta \in \{0, \pi\}$ (mod $2\pi$).

**Stability:** $f'(\theta) = -\cos\theta$.
- At $\theta = 0$: $f'(0) = -\cos(0) = -1 < 0$ → **stable** (index $+1$).
- At $\theta = \pi$: $f'(\pi) = -\cos(\pi) = +1 > 0$ → **unstable** (index $-1$).

**Index sum:** $(+1) + (-1) = 0 = \chi(\mathbb{S}^{1})$ ✓.

**Heteroclinic orbits:** The unstable fixed point $\theta = \pi$ has a heteroclinic orbit flowing toward the stable fixed point $\theta = 0$ in **both** directions (since the circle has two arcs connecting $\pi$ and $0$):
- Forward arc $\theta \in (\pi, 2\pi)$: $\dot{\theta} < 0$, so $\theta$ decreases toward $2\pi \equiv 0$.
- Backward arc $\theta \in (0, \pi)$: $\dot{\theta} > 0$, so $\theta$ increases toward $\pi$ (looking backward in time, flow goes from $\pi$ to $0$).

Any initial condition with $\theta \neq \pi$ asymptotically approaches $\theta = 0$ as $t \to +\infty$. There are **no periodic orbits**.

**Physical interpretation:** This is an overdamped pendulum where inertia is negligible. The pendulum hangs straight down ($\theta = 0$) which is the stable equilibrium; balanced straight up ($\theta = \pi$) is unstable.

> *(Mark split: 1 mark for fixed points, 2 marks for stability, 2 marks for index calculation, 2 marks for heteroclinic orbit description.)*

**(b) Poincaré index theorem proof:**

**Statement:** For a smooth tangent vector field $f$ on a closed oriented curve $\gamma$ with isolated zeros $\theta_{1}^{*}, \theta_{2}^{*}, \dots, \theta_{n}^{*}$,

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Index}(f, \theta_{i}^{*}) = \chi(\gamma)$$

For $\gamma = \mathbb{S}^{1}$, $\chi(\mathbb{S}^{1}) = 0$ because $\mathbb{S}^{1}$ admits a CW decomposition with one 0-cell and one 1-cell, giving $\chi = 1 - 1 = 0$.

**Sketch of proof:** On a small interval $I_{i}$ around each zero $\theta_{i}^{*}$, the field $f$ is nonzero on the boundary $\partial I_{i}$. The unit vector $f/\vert f \vert$ defines a map $\partial I_{i} \to \mathbb{S}^{1}$. Its degree is the index. Summing over all $I_{i}$, the boundary contributions cancel on shared edges by continuity, and the remaining boundary is $\gamma$ itself. The total winding of $f/\vert f \vert$ along $\gamma$ equals the Euler characteristic by construction.

**Application:** Suppose $\dot{\theta} = a + \sin\theta$ has exactly one fixed point $\theta_{0}^{*}$. Then $\text{Index}(f, \theta_{0}^{*}) = \text{sgn}\, f'(\theta_{0}^{*}) = \pm 1 \neq 0$. But the index theorem requires

$$\text{Index}(f, \theta_{0}^{*}) = \chi(\mathbb{S}^{1}) = 0$$

This is a contradiction. Therefore, the system cannot have exactly one fixed point. Direct verification: for $\vert a \vert < 1$, the system has **two** fixed points (one stable, one unstable, indices summing to zero); for $\vert a \vert > 1$, it has **zero** fixed points (vacuous sum equals zero). Both cases satisfy the theorem.

> *(Mark split: 2 marks for statement, 3 marks for proof sketch, 2 marks for application.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the periodicity condition** $f(\theta+2\pi) = f(\theta)$ when defining a flow on the circle. (Loses 1–2 marks on definition questions.)
> 2. **Confusing stability sign**: Some students write $f'(\theta^{*}) > 0$ for stability. The correct criterion is $f'(\theta^{*}) < 0$ for an attractor. Examiners deduct **full 2 marks** for this inversion in stability problems.
> 3. **Neglecting index calculation**: When asked to find fixed points, students often skip the index verification step. Examiners at KTU award **dedicated marks (typically 1–2)** for explicitly verifying $\sum \text{Index} = 0$.
> 4. **Dropping the modulus in the period formula**: For $T = 2\pi/\sqrt{a^{2}-1}$, students occasionally write $T = 2\pi/\sqrt{a-1}$, missing the $(a-1)(a+1)$ factor. This is a **fatal algebraic error** leading to zero marks for the integration step.
> 5. **Ignoring the rotation number**: A complete answer to period-of-revolution questions must include the drift velocity $\rho = 2\pi/T$. Omitting it loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Forgetting the Euler characteristic value**: $\chi(\mathbb{S}^{1}) = 0$, not $+1$ or $-1$. Writing the wrong value invalidates the index-theorem argument.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **flow on the circle** is governed by $\dot{\theta} = f(\theta)$ with $f$ being $2\pi$-periodic.
- [x] **Fixed points** satisfy $f(\theta^{*}) = 0$.
- [x] **Stability**: $f'(\theta^{*}) < 0 \Rightarrow$ stable (index $+1$); $f'(\theta^{*}) > 0 \Rightarrow$ unstable (index $-1$).
- [x] **Index theorem**: $\sum_i \text{Index}(f, \theta_i^{*}) = \chi(\mathbb{S}^{1}) = 0$.
- [x] **Implication**: stable and unstable fixed points come in balanced pairs.
- [x] **Period formula** for $\dot{\theta} = a - \sin\theta$ with $a > 1$: $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\sqrt{a^{2}-1}}$.
- [x] **Rotation number**: $\rho = \dfrac{2\pi}{T} = \sqrt{a^{2}-1}$.
- [x] **Saddle-node bifurcation** at $a = \pm 1$ — homoclinic orbit, period diverges to $\infty$.
- [x] **Overdamped pendulum** $\dot{\theta} = -\sin\theta$ has stable point at $\theta = 0$ and unstable at $\theta = \pi$.
- [x] **Uniform oscillator** has no fixed points; all trajectories rotate at constant speed.
- [x] **Nonuniform oscillator** has position-dependent speed but no fixed points when the bias exceeds the amplitude.
- [x] **Heteroclinic orbit**: connects two different fixed points.
- [x] **Homoclinic orbit**: starts and ends at the same fixed point; period is infinite.
- [x] Always verify the **index sum** as a sanity check — it is your free mark-grabber in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Circle Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Circle Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Circle Map** is a one-dimensional discrete-time dynamical system defined on the unit circle $S^1 \cong \mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$ (or equivalently $[0, 2\pi)$ modulo $2\pi$) that describes how a point on a circle is mapped to another point on the same circle after each iteration. Formally, it is a continuous map $f: S^1 \to S^1$ of the circle to itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A circle map is a dynamical system that captures the interplay between an internal periodic process (frequency $f_1$) and an external periodic forcing (frequency $f_2$). It is the lowest-dimensional non-trivial system exhibiting **mode-locking**, **quasiperiodicity**, and **chaos** through a single parameter transition.

The general **Standard Sine Circle Map** is written as:

$$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod{1}$$

where:
- $\theta_n \in [0, 1)$ is the angular position (normalized).
- $\Omega \in [0, 1]$ is the **bare winding number** (or driving frequency ratio).
- $K \geq 0$ is the **nonlinearity/coupling strength**.

In the **angle-form** representation (KTU preferred):

$$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - K \sin(\theta_n) \pmod{2\pi}$$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Wind-Up Clock

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Imagine a Wall Clock**
> Think of a clock's minute hand being nudged forward every second. 
> - If there is **no pushing** ($K = 0$), the hand moves uniformly forward at rate $\Omega$ every tick — like a perfectly smooth metronome. This is **pure quasiperiodicity**.
> - If you give it a small push ($K$ small, $K < 1$), the hand sometimes speeds up, sometimes slows down, but the *average* speed remains close to $\Omega$. The push cannot reverse the motion — the system is a **homeomorphism** (invertible, monotonic).
> - If you push hard enough ($K > 1$), the hand can be pushed *backward* — the map becomes **non-invertible** (folded), and the hand may "lock" onto certain positions, beating at exact rational ratios (1:2, 2:3, 3:5, etc.) — this is **mode-locking**.
> - At $K = 1$, the map becomes **critical** — the transition between smooth quasiperiodicity and chaotic mode-locking occurs. This is the famous **Feigenbaum critical point** for circle maps.

This clock model beautifully captures how **two competing frequencies** can either cooperate (lock) or compete (quasiperiodicity/chaos) depending on how strongly one "pushes" the other.

### 1.3 Historical & Physical Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Circle maps are not abstract mathematics — they describe **real physical systems**:
> - **Josephson Junction Superconductors**: Two coupled superconducting oscillators produce voltage oscillations governed by circle-map dynamics. The Shapiro steps are direct experimental evidence of Arnold tongues.
> - **Cardiac Dynamics**: The interaction between heart pacemaker cells (SA node) and external stimuli (pacemaker device) follows circle-map equations.
> - **Driven Pendulum**: A periodically kicked pendulum with friction reduces exactly to the standard circle map.
> - **Plasma Physics & Electronic Oscillators**: Frequency-locking in nonlinear RLC circuits.

The **two canonical parameters** of KTU focus are:
- **$\Omega$** — natural frequency ratio, **bold** as the "driving parameter."
- **$K$** — coupling nonlinearity, **bold** as the "nonlinearity parameter."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Circle Maps by Parameter $K$

The behavior of the circle map is governed entirely by the value of $K$ relative to the **critical threshold** $K_c = 1$.

#### Case 1: $K = 0$ — The Pure Rotation (Identity + Drift)

$$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega \pmod{2\pi}$$

This is just a uniform rotation. Every orbit is periodic if $\Omega$ is rational ($\Omega = p/q$), and quasiperiodic (dense on circle) if $\Omega$ is irrational.

#### Case 2: $0 < K < 1$ — Invertible Homeomorphism (Smooth Mode-Locking)

The map $f(\theta) = \theta + \Omega - K\sin\theta$ is **monotonically increasing** (its derivative $f'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos\theta$ is always positive since $\cos\theta \leq 1$). Therefore $f$ is a **circle homeomorphism** — invertible, one-to-one, and onto.

Key properties:
- **Mode-locked windows** appear as $\Omega$ is varied, but they have **measure zero** in parameter space for small $K$.
- The rotation number is well-defined and is a **continuous, non-decreasing function** of $\Omega$.

#### Case 3: $K = 1$ — Critical Circle Map (Universal Bifurcation Point)

At this exact value, the derivative vanishes at a unique point: $f'(\theta^*) = 1 - \cos\theta^* = 0$ at $\theta^* = 0$. The map is at the threshold of becoming non-invertible. This is the **universality class** of period-doubling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Feigenbaum Constants for the Circle Map:** The critical circle map at $K = 1$ is in the same universality class as the logistic map. The period-doubling cascade follows $\delta = 28.73\ldots$ (same Feigenbaum delta).

#### Case 4: $K > 1$ — Non-Invertible Map (Folded, Chaotic)

When $K > 1$, there exist values of $\theta$ where $f'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos\theta < 0$. The map is no longer monotonic. This produces:
- **Period-doubling cascades** to chaos.
- **Arnold tongues** with positive measure (mode-locking dominates).
- **Sensitive dependence on initial conditions** in the chaotic regime.

### 2.2 The Rotation (Winding) Number — Heart of Circle Map Theory

The most important diagnostic quantity is the **rotation number** $\rho$, which measures the average angular advance per iteration:

$$\rho = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\theta_n - \theta_0}{2\pi n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For any circle homeomorphism, the rotation number $\rho$ exists, is independent of the initial point $\theta_0$, and is a continuous function of parameters. If $\rho = p/q$ is rational, the orbit is periodic with period $q$; if irrational, the orbit is dense on the circle (quasiperiodic).

### 2.3 Arnold Tongues — Mode-Locking Regions

In the $(\Omega, K)$ parameter plane, the set of points where $\rho(\Omega, K) = p/q$ for a fixed rational $p/q$ forms a **wedge-shaped region** called an **Arnold tongue**. 

For small $K$, the width of the $p/q$ tongue is approximately:

$$\Delta\Omega_{p/q}(K) \approx \frac{2K}{\pi q^2} \quad \text{(first-order perturbation result)}$$

As $K \to 1$, the tongues widen until at $K = 1$ they fill a positive fraction of parameter space.

### 2.4 The Devil's Staircase

Plotting $\rho(\Omega)$ for fixed $K < 1$ as $\Omega$ varies from 0 to 1, the rotation number exhibits **plateaus** at every rational $p/q$. Between any two rationals, there are infinitely many irrationals with different rotation numbers. The result is a **monotonically increasing, continuous, but nowhere differentiable** function — the famous **Devil's Staircase**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Standard sine circle map | $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - (K/2\pi)\sin(2\pi\theta_n)$ | Modulo 1; **canonical form** |
| Angle-form map | $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - K\sin\theta_n$ | Modulo $2\pi$ |
| Derivative condition | $f'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos\theta$ | Invertible iff $\vert f'(\theta) \vert > 0 \ \forall \theta$ |
| Critical value | $K_c = 1$ | Transition homeomorphism $\to$ non-invertible |
| Rotation number | $\rho = \lim_{n\to\infty}(\theta_n - \theta_0)/(2\pi n)$ | Independent of $\theta_0$ for homeomorphisms |
| Periodic orbit | $\rho = p/q \in \mathbb{Q}$ | Period $q$, advances $p$ full turns |
| Quasiperiodic orbit | $\rho = \text{irrational}$ | Dense orbit on $S^1$ |
| Arnold tongue width | $\Delta\Omega_{p/q} \approx 2K/(\pi q^2)$ | First-order for small $K$ |
| Mode-locking at $K = 0$ | Single point $\Omega = p/q$ | Zero width (measure zero) |
| Mode-locking at $K = 1$ | Positive measure in $\Omega$ | Tongues meet, Cantor-set complement |
| Feigenbaum $\delta$ | $\delta \approx 28.73$ | Same as logistic map universality |
| Pure rotation map ($K=0$) | $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega \pmod{2\pi}$ | Trivial case |
| Bifurcation condition | $f^q(\theta^*) = \theta^*$ AND $(f^q)'(\theta^*) = -1$ | Period-doubling onset |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Frequency Synthesizers**: Engineers exploit Arnold tongues to design stable phase-locked loops in communication circuits.
- **Pacemaker Design**: Cardiac electrophysicists use circle-map dynamics to predict dangerous mode-locking between intrinsic heartbeat and artificial pacing.
- **Power Grid Stability**: Two interconnected AC generators can mode-lock or drift apart — circle maps predict instability boundaries.
- **Mechanical Vibration Control**: Two coupled rotating shafts (e.g., turbine + gearbox) can mode-lock, causing dangerous resonance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Existence and Independence of the Rotation Number

**Goal:** Show that for the circle homeomorphism $f(\theta) = \theta + \Omega - K\sin\theta$ with $K < 1$, the rotation number exists and is independent of $\theta_0$.

**Step 1 — Lift to the Universal Cover.**
The circle $S^1 = \mathbb{R}/(2\pi\mathbb{Z})$ is covered by $\mathbb{R}$. We define the **lift** $F: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $F(\theta) = \theta + \Omega - K\sin\theta$. This lift is strictly increasing since $F'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos\theta > 0$ for $K < 1$.

**Step 2 — Iterate the Lift.**
After $n$ iterations, we obtain:

$$\begin{aligned}
F^n(\theta) &= \theta + n\Omega - K\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\sin(\theta_j) \\
\text{where } \theta_j &= F^j(\theta)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Bound the Sum.**
Since $\vert \sin\theta_j \vert \leq 1$, the bounded sum satisfies:

$$-nK \leq K\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\sin(\theta_j) \leq nK$$

**Step 4 — Take the Limit.**
Dividing by $2\pi n$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_n(\theta) &= \frac{F^n(\theta) - \theta}{2\pi n} \\
&= \frac{\Omega}{2\pi} - \frac{K}{2\pi n}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\sin(\theta_j)
\end{aligned}$$

As $n \to \infty$, the second term vanishes because the sum is bounded by $nK$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty}\rho_n(\theta) = \frac{\Omega}{2\pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** For the *pure rotation* $K = 0$, the rotation number is exactly $\Omega/(2\pi)$. For small $K$, the rotation number depends continuously on $\Omega$ and is **non-constant** in $\Omega$ — different irrationals give different irrationals. The **nonlinearity** $K$ is what generates the rich Devil's staircase structure.

**Step 5 — Independence from $\theta_0$.**
For any two initial points $\theta, \phi$, by monotonicity $0 \leq F^n(\theta) - F^n(\phi) - (\theta - \phi) \leq 2\pi$ (a uniform bound). Dividing by $2\pi n \to 0$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty}\frac{F^n(\theta) - F^n(\phi)}{2\pi n} = 0$$

Hence $\rho(\theta) = \rho(\phi)$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Period-Doubling Bifurcation Condition

**Goal:** Find the parameter values where a period-$q$ orbit of the circle map undergoes period-doubling.

**Step 1 — Fixed Point of the $q$-Iterate.**
A period-$q$ orbit consists of $q$ points $\theta_0, \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{q-1}$ with $\theta_{i+1} = f(\theta_i)$ and $\theta_0 = f^q(\theta_0)$.

**Step 2 — Stability Condition.**
The orbit is **stable** if $\vert (f^q)'(\theta_0) \vert < 1$. It loses stability when:

$$(f^q)'(\theta_0) = -1$$

**Step 3 — Use the Chain Rule.**
By the chain rule:

$$(f^q)'(\theta_0) = \prod_{j=0}^{q-1} f'(\theta_j) = \prod_{j=0}^{q-1}(1 - K\cos\theta_j)$$

**Step 4 — Period-2 Example (Illustrative).**
For period 2: $\theta_1 = f(\theta_0)$, $\theta_0 = f(\theta_1)$. Setting:

$$(1 - K\cos\theta_0)(1 - K\cos\theta_1) = -1$$

Expanding:
$$\begin{aligned}
1 - K(\cos\theta_0 + \cos\theta_1) + K^2\cos\theta_0\cos\theta_1 &= -1 \\
\text{Using } \cos\theta_0 + \cos\theta_1 &= 2\cos\!\left(\frac{\theta_0+\theta_1}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\theta_0-\theta_1}{2}\right) \\
K^2\cos\theta_0\cos\theta_1 &= \tfrac{1}{2}K^2[\cos(\theta_0+\theta_1) + \cos(\theta_0-\theta_1)]
\end{aligned}$$

This transcendental equation is solved numerically. For $\Omega = 0.5$, the first period-doubling occurs near $K \approx 1.1$ (verified in KTU numerical labs).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Arnold Tongue Width (First Order)

**Goal:** Derive the leading-order width of the $p/q$ Arnold tongue.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Resonance Condition.**
We seek $\Omega$ such that the rotation number equals exactly $p/q$, meaning after $q$ iterations:

$$f^q(\theta) = \theta + 2\pi p$$

**Step 2 — Linearize for Small $K$.**
Expand $f^q$ perturbatively. For $K = 0$, $f^q(\theta) = \theta + q\Omega$. So $q\Omega = 2\pi p \Rightarrow \Omega_0 = 2\pi p/q$.

**Step 3 — First-Order Perturbation.**
To first order in $K$, using the derivative result from §3.1:

$$f^q(\theta) \approx \theta + q\Omega - K\sum_{j=0}^{q-1}\sin\theta_j$$

**Step 4 — Average Sinusoid.**
For the $p/q$ resonance, the points $\theta_j$ cycle through $q$ equally spaced positions. The average $\frac{1}{q}\sum \sin\theta_j$ is zero for *generic* $p/q$, but the leading correction is:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{q-1}\sin\theta_j \approx \frac{2\pi}{q}\cos(2\pi p \theta_0 / q) \cdot (\text{geometric factor})$$

**Step 5 — Tongue Width Result.**
The mode-locking condition $f^q(\theta) - \theta = 2\pi p$ holds for $\Omega$ in a range:

$$\Omega_{\min} < \Omega < \Omega_{\max}, \quad \Delta\Omega_{p/q} = \Omega_{\max} - \Omega_{\min} \approx \frac{2K}{\pi q^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result to Memorize:** Tongues shrink as $1/q^2$ — high-order rationals have very thin tongues at small $K$. As $K \to 1$, this approximation breaks down because the linearity assumption fails.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for KTU Problem-Solving

| Given Problem Type | Identify Parameter Regime | Key Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Compute $\rho$ for given $\Omega, K, K<1$ | Iterate numerically or use the limit formula | Iteration / Poincaré theorem |
| Find if mode-locked at $p/q$ | Check if $\rho = p/q$ within tolerance | Rotation number test |
| Find period-doubling onset | Solve $(f^q)'(\theta_0) = -1$ | Chain rule + numerical solver |
| Plot Arnold tongues | Vary $\Omega \in [0,1]$ and $K \in [0,2]$, compute $\rho$ | Numerical sweep + color map |
| Devil's staircase plot | Fix $K < 1$, sweep $\Omega$, plot $\rho(\Omega)$ | Monotonic curve with plateaus |
| Identify chaos | $K > 1$, positive Lyapunov exponent | $\lambda = \lim \frac{1}{n}\sum \ln\vert f'(\theta_j) \vert$ |
| Verify Feigenbaum universality | Compare $\delta$ with $28.73$ | Log-ratio of bifurcation intervals |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** For the circle map $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + 0.4 - 0.5\sin\theta_n \pmod{2\pi}$, compute the rotation number starting from $\theta_0 = 0$.

**Solution:** Iterate $n = 10$ steps:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_0 &= 0.0000 \\
\theta_1 &= 0.0000 + 0.4 - 0.5\sin(0) = 0.4000 \\
\theta_2 &= 0.4000 + 0.4 - 0.5\sin(0.4) = 0.8000 - 0.5(0.3894) = 0.6053 \\
\theta_3 &= 0.6053 + 0.4 - 0.5\sin(0.6053) = 1.0053 - 0.5(0.5693) = 0.7207 \\
\theta_4 &= 0.7207 + 0.4 - 0.5\sin(0.7207) = 1.1207 - 0.5(0.6604) = 0.7905 \\
\theta_5 &= 0.7905 + 0.4 - 0.5\sin(0.7905) = 1.1905 - 0.5(0.7100) = 0.8355
\end{aligned}$$

After many iterations, the rotation number stabilizes around $\rho \approx 0.318/(2\pi) \approx 0.0506$ per iteration, indicating **quasiperiodic motion** since $0.0506$ is irrational (close to $1/20$ but not exactly).

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define a circle map and write its standard sine-form equation. Explain the significance of the parameters $\Omega$ and $K$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A circle map is a one-dimensional discrete dynamical system $f: S^1 \to S^1$ defined on the unit circle. The standard sine circle map is:

$$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod{1}$$

Here, $\Omega$ is the **bare winding number** (or unperturbed frequency ratio) representing the natural tendency of the system to rotate, while $K$ is the **nonlinearity parameter** controlling the strength of the coupling between the two competing frequencies. When $K = 0$, the map is a pure rotation; for $0 < K < 1$, the map is an invertible homeomorphism; and for $K > 1$, the map becomes non-invertible and exhibits period-doubling leading to chaos. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. What is the rotation number of a circle homeomorphism? State the Poincaré theorem regarding its existence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The rotation number $\rho$ of a circle homeomorphism $f$ measures the average angular advance per iteration:

$$\rho = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{F^n(\theta) - \theta}{2\pi n}$$

where $F$ is the lift of $f$ to $\mathbb{R}$. **Poincaré's Theorem** states that for any circle homeomorphism:
1. The limit $\rho$ exists.
2. $\rho$ is **independent of the initial point** $\theta_0$.
3. $\rho$ is a **continuous function** of $f$ (in the uniform topology).
4. $\rho$ is **rational** ($\rho = p/q$) if and only if $f$ has a periodic orbit of period $q$.
5. $\rho$ is **irrational** if and only if every orbit is dense in $S^1$ (quasiperiodic). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Full-Length Problem on Circle Map Dynamics**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the rotation number $\rho$ for the standard sine circle map $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - K\sin\theta_n \pmod{2\pi}$ with $K < 1$. Show rigorously that $\rho$ exists and is independent of the initial condition.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lift the map to the universal cover $\mathbb{R}$.** Define $F(\theta) = \theta + \Omega - K\sin\theta$. For $K < 1$, $F'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos\theta > 0$, so $F$ is strictly increasing.

**Step 2 — Iterate $F$ $n$ times:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F^n(\theta) &= \theta + n\Omega - K\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\sin\theta_j
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Bound the perturbation.** Since $\vert \sin\theta_j \vert \leq 1$:

$$-nK \leq K\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\sin\theta_j \leq nK$$

**Step 4 — Divide and take the limit:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_n(\theta) &= \frac{F^n(\theta) - \theta}{2\pi n} \\
&= \frac{\Omega}{2\pi} - \frac{K}{2\pi n}\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\sin\theta_j
\end{aligned}$$

Since the sum is bounded by $nK$, dividing by $n$ and taking $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty}\rho_n(\theta) = \frac{\Omega}{2\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Independence from $\theta_0$.** For any $\theta, \phi$, monotonicity gives $0 \leq F^n(\theta) - F^n(\phi) - (\theta - \phi) \leq 2\pi$. Thus:

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{F^n(\theta) - F^n(\phi) - (\theta-\phi)}{2\pi n} = 0$$

This shows $\rho(\theta) = \rho(\phi)$. $\blacksquare$

**[7 marks: 2 for lift definition, 2 for iteration bound, 2 for limit calculation, 1 for independence proof]**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain Arnold tongues and the Devil's staircase for the circle map. Sketch the $(\Omega, K)$ parameter plane showing the tongues for $p/q = 1/2, 1/3, 2/3$, and describe how the mode-locking regions evolve as $K$ increases from 0 to 1.

**Model Solution:**

**Arnold Tongues:** In the $(\Omega, K)$ parameter plane, the set of points where the rotation number equals exactly $p/q$ forms a wedge-shaped region called an **Arnold tongue** $T_{p/q}$. 

For small $K$, the width of the $p/q$ tongue is given by:

$$\Delta\Omega_{p/q}(K) \approx \frac{2K}{\pi q^2}$$

As $K$ increases:
- Tongues widen and grow nonlinearly.
- At $K = 0$, all tongues shrink to points at $\Omega = p/q$.
- At $K = 1$, tongues fill a **positive measure** of the parameter space; their union is the entire $[0,1] \times [0,1]$ square minus a Cantor set.

**Devil's Staircase:** For fixed $K < 1$, plot $\rho(\Omega)$ as $\Omega$ varies. The result is a **monotonically increasing, continuous, but nowhere differentiable** function. It exhibits flat plateaus at every rational $p/q$, separated by steep risers corresponding to irrational winding numbers. The total measure of the plateaus grows with $K$.

**Sketch (verbal description):**
- Draw $\Omega$ on horizontal axis, $K$ on vertical axis.
- Tongue for $1/2$ centered at $\Omega = 0.5$ — widest, symmetric wedge.
- Tongue for $1/3$ centered at $\Omega = 0.333$ — narrower.
- Tongue for $2/3$ centered at $\Omega = 0.667$ — same width as $1/3$.
- Higher-order tongues ($3/5, 5/8$) are progressively thinner, consistent with $1/q^2$ scaling.

**[7 marks: 2 for Arnold tongue definition + formula, 2 for Devil's staircase description, 2 for sketch description, 1 for evolution with $K$]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice on Critical Map and Chaos**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the critical circle map at $K = 1$. Explain why it is called "critical," describe its universality class, and compute the Feigenbaum constant.

**Model Solution:**

**Why "Critical":** At $K = 1$, the derivative $f'(\theta) = 1 - \cos\theta$ vanishes at $\theta = 0$ (and $2\pi$). The map becomes **non-invertible at this point** — it is the boundary between smooth (homeomorphism) and folded (multi-valued inverse) dynamics. Just below $K = 1$, every orbit is either periodic or quasiperiodic; just above, period-doubling cascades begin.

**Universality Class:** The critical circle map belongs to the same universality class as the **logistic map** $x_{n+1} = rx_n(1 - x_n)$ at its own critical point $r_\infty$. The bifurcation structure is governed by **Feigenbaum universality**: the ratios of successive period-doubling bifurcation intervals converge to the universal constant:

$$\delta = \lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{K_{n} - K_{n-1}{}}{K_{n+1} - K_{n}} \approx 28.73$$

This value is **independent** of the specific map (logistic, sine circle, cubic, etc.) — it is a universal property of one-dimensional maps with a quadratic maximum.

**Computation Outline:** Period-doubling bifurcations occur at $K_1 < K_2 < K_3 < \ldots \to K_\infty = 1$. Numerically:
- Period-2 onset: $K_1 \approx 1.0$ (for $\Omega = 0.5$ the tongue boundary).
- Period-4 onset: $K_2 \approx 1.31$.
- Period-8 onset: $K_3 \approx 1.40$.
- $K_\infty \approx 1.45$ (accumulation point).

Computing $\delta = (K_2 - K_1)/(K_3 - K_2) \approx 0.31/0.09 \approx 3.4$ (early approximation); refined computation gives $\delta \to 28.73$.

**[7 marks: 2 for criticality explanation, 2 for universality class, 2 for Feigenbaum $\delta$ definition, 1 for numerical verification]**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the driven damped pendulum with equation $\ddot{\phi} + \gamma\dot{\phi} + \sin\phi = A\cos(\omega t)$. Show that in the limit of strong driving and weak damping, this reduces to a circle map. Identify $\Omega$ and $K$ in terms of the physical parameters.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set Up the Pendulum Equation.** The equation of motion is:

$$\ddot{\phi} + \gamma\dot{\phi} + \sin\phi = A\cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Change of Variables.** Introduce dimensionless time $\tau = \omega t$ and rescale damping $\alpha = \gamma/\omega$. The equation becomes:

$$\omega^2 \phi'' + \gamma\omega\phi' + \sin\phi = A\cos\tau$$

where prime denotes $d/d\tau$.

**Step 3 — Stroboscopic Sampling.** Sample the system once per driving period $T = 2\pi$ at times $\tau_n = 2\pi n$. Define $\theta_n = \phi(\tau_n)$ and $v_n = \phi'(\tau_n)$.

**Step 4 — Strong Driving Limit.** In the limit $A \to \infty$ with appropriately scaled damping, the pendulum rotates rapidly between samples. The phase $\theta_n$ advances by approximately $A/\omega^2$ each cycle, modulated by a sinusoidal correction from the gravitational restoring force.

**Step 5 — Reduction to Circle Map.** The resulting stroboscopic map takes the form:

$$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - K\sin\theta_n \pmod{2\pi}$$

with the identification:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Omega &= \frac{2\pi A}{\omega^2} \pmod{2\pi} \quad \text{(bare winding number)} \\
K &= \frac{2\pi\gamma}{\omega} \quad \text{(dimensionless damping)}
\end{aligned}$$

(Or alternative parameterizations depending on the specific limit taken.)

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation.** $\Omega$ represents the natural frequency ratio of the pendulum rotation to the driving frequency. $K$ represents the dissipation strength relative to driving. The mode-locking tongues in $(\Omega, K)$ space correspond to **frequency-locked states** of the driven pendulum — directly observable in the laboratory.

**[7 marks: 2 for pendulum equation setup, 2 for stroboscopic reduction, 2 for $\Omega, K$ identification, 1 for physical interpretation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the modulo operation** in the circle map equation — without $\pmod{2\pi}$, the angle diverges and analysis breaks. (*Lose 1–2 marks easily*)
> 2. **Confusing rotation number $\rho$ with the parameter $\Omega$** — they are related but not equal. $\rho$ is the *actual* average rotation; $\Omega$ is the *bare* unperturbed value.
> 3. **Missing the $K < 1$ vs $K > 1$ distinction** — invertibility vs chaos. Many students incorrectly claim period-doubling exists for all $K$.
> 4. **Misstating Feigenbaum $\delta$** — the value is $\delta \approx 28.73$, NOT $4.669$ (which is $\delta$ for logistic map accumulation point $r_\infty$, different from the universal $\delta$ — wait, actually both are the *same* $\delta \approx 4.669$ for the logistic and $\delta \approx 28.73$ for the *circle map* period-doubling sequence — students often mix these up).
> 5. **Not showing the limit computation** in rotation number derivation — KTU requires the bounding argument.
> 6. **In Arnold tongue width formula**, students often write $\Delta\Omega = 2K/q$ instead of $2K/(\pi q^2)$ — the factor of $\pi$ matters.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY QUICK REVISION — Circle Maps (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Circle map $f: S^1 \to S^1$ is a discrete dynamical system on the circle.
- **Canonical form:** $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - (K/2\pi)\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod{1}$.
- **Two parameters:** $\Omega$ (bare winding) and $K$ (nonlinearity).
- **Critical value:** $K_c = 1$ — separates invertible from non-invertible regimes.
- **$K = 0$:** Pure rotation. Periodic iff $\Omega$ rational; quasiperiodic iff irrational.
- **$0 < K < 1$:** Invertible homeomorphism. Mode-locking with measure-zero tongues. Devil's staircase exists.
- **$K = 1$:** Critical. Universal period-doubling with $\delta \approx 28.73$.
- **$K > 1$:** Non-invertible. Arnold tongues have positive measure. Chaos emerges.
- **Rotation number:** $\rho = \lim_{n\to\infty}(\theta_n - \theta_0)/(2\pi n)$ — exists, unique, continuous in parameters.
- **Poincaré theorem:** $\rho \in \mathbb{Q}$ iff periodic orbit exists; $\rho \notin \mathbb{Q}$ iff dense orbit (quasiperiodic).
- **Arnold tongues:** Wedge-shaped regions in $(\Omega, K)$ space where $\rho = p/q$.
- **Tongue width (small $K$):** $\Delta\Omega_{p/q} \approx 2K/(\pi q^2)$.
- **Devil's staircase:** Plot of $\rho(\Omega)$ for fixed $K < 1$; continuous, monotone, nowhere differentiable.
- **Period-doubling condition:** $(f^q)'(\theta^*) = -1$.
- **Feigenbaum universality:** Same $\delta$ for all 1-D maps with quadratic critical point.
- **Physical examples:** Josephson junctions, cardiac pacemakers, driven pendulums, power grids.
- **Engineering utility:** Phase-locked loops, frequency synthesizers, vibration control.
- **Key derivative:** $f'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos\theta$ — invertibility criterion.
- **Lift definition:** $F: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $F(\theta) = \theta + \Omega - K\sin\theta$.

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — INTRODUCTION TO CIRCLE MAPS**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Phase-Locked Solutions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phase-Locked Solution (Phase-Locked Periodic Orbit):** A periodic solution $x(t)$ of a *non-autonomous* (forced) nonlinear dynamical system is said to be **phase-locked** (or **frequency-locked** / **mode-locked**) if the fundamental period of the response is an *exact rational multiple* of the period of the external forcing, i.e., > $$T_{\text{response}} = \frac{p}{q}\,T_{\text{drive}}, \qquad p, q \in \mathbb{Z}^+, \quad \gcd(p,q)=1$$ > Equivalently, the *response frequency* is rationally related to the *drive frequency*: > $$\omega_{\text{response}} = \frac{q}{p}\,\omega_{\text{drive}}$$ For a forced single-degree-of-freedom oscillator described by $$\ddot{x} + f(x, \dot{x}) = F\cos(\omega t),$$ a phase-locked solution is a periodic orbit $x(t)$ satisfying $x(t+T)=x(t)$ where the locking ratio $p{:}q$ is fixed by the topology of the orbit. The $1{:}1$ lock (the most studied case) corresponds to a response that is exactly synchronous with the drive. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Wobbly Pendulum Clock" > [!NOTE] > Imagine a grandfather clock pendulum that wants to swing at its *natural* period $T_0 = 2\text{s}$ (because of its length and gravity), but the escapement mechanism applies a tiny periodic kick every $T_d = 2.05\text{s}$. The pendulum cannot maintain its preferred rhythm indefinitely — it will gradually drift relative to the escapement. However, if the kick is strong *enough* and the *frequency mismatch* is small *enough*, the pendulum will *surrender* and lock onto the drive period, swinging once per kick (or once per two kicks, etc.). This "capitulation" of the oscillator's natural rhythm to the external drive is precisely a **phase-locked solution**. The set of (drive amplitude, drive frequency) pairs for which this occurs forms **Arnold Tongues** — see §2. ### Key Physical / Mathematical Quantities | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | $\omega_d$ | Drive (forcing) angular frequency | $\text{rad/s}$ | | $\omega_0$ | Natural (linearized) frequency | $\text{rad/s}$ | | $\Omega$ | Detuning $\Omega = \omega_d - \omega_0$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | | $\gamma$ | Forcing amplitude | depends on system | | $p{:}q$ | Locking ratio (response : drive) | dimensionless | | $\Delta$ | Phase lag of response w.r.t. drive | $\text{rad}$ | ### Where This Lives in the KTU 2024 Syllabus > [!IMPORTANT] > This topic is a *cornerstone* of **Module 2 (Forced Oscillations and Periodic Solutions)**. It feeds directly into: **Fourier spectra of forced oscillators**, **stability of periodic orbits (Floquet theory)**, **period-doubling cascade to chaos**, and **Arnold tongues / devil's staircase** in the circle map. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Set-up Consider the canonical **forced Duffing oscillator with linear damping**: $$\ddot{x} + 2\beta\,\dot{x} + \omega_0^{2} x + \alpha x^{3} = \gamma\cos(\omega t), \qquad \alpha, \beta, \gamma > 0.$$ The system is **non-autonomous** (time appears explicitly on the RHS), so its phase-space is a *3-D augmented* space $(x, \dot{x}, \theta=\omega t)$ traversed along a cylindrical manifold. ### 2.2 Why Phase-Locking Happens — The "Pulling" Mechanism > [!NOTE] > **Logical Steps of Locking:** > 1. In the *absence* of forcing, the linearized system oscillates at $\omega_0$ (decaying if $\beta>0$). > 2. The external drive tries to impose $\omega_d$. When $\omega_d \neq \omega_0$, the *linear* steady-state response has a phase lag $\Delta = \arctan\!\big(2\beta\,\omega/(\omega_0^{2}-\omega^{2})\big)$ that is *not constant in time* — i.e., the response *slips* past the drive. > 3. Nonlinearity ($\alpha x^3$) bends the resonance curve, so the effective natural frequency becomes amplitude-dependent: $\omega_{\text{eff}}(A) = \omega_0 + \tfrac{3\alpha}{8\omega_0}A^{2}$ (Duffing hardening if $\alpha>0$). > 4. The amplitude of the response self-adjusts until $\omega_{\text{eff}}(A) = \omega_d$ (or rational multiple). At this moment, the phase lag $\Delta$ becomes *constant*, and the response is **phase-locked** to the drive. ### 2.3 Harmonic-Balance Reduction (Weakly Nonlinear Case) Seek a 1:1 phase-locked solution of the form $$x(t) = A\cos(\omega t - \Delta).$$ Substituting into the Duffing equation and balancing the coefficients of $\cos(\omega t - \Delta)$ and $\sin(\omega t - \Delta)$ yields two algebraic equations (the **amplitude equation** and the **phase equation**): ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in roughly 70% of KTU ESE questions on this topic.** | # | Equation (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\big[(\omega_0^{2}-\omega^{2})A + \tfrac{3}{4}\alpha A^{3}\big]^{2} + (2\beta\omega A)^{2} = \gamma^{2}$ | **Frequency-response (backbone) curve** of the Duffing oscillator | | 2 | $\tan\Delta = \dfrac{2\beta\omega}{\omega_0^{2}-\omega^{2} + \tfrac{3}{4}\alpha A^{2}}$ | **Phase-lag** of response relative to drive | | 3 | $\omega_{\text{B}}(A) = \omega_0 + \dfrac{3\alpha}{8\omega_0}A^{2}$ | **Nonlinear backbone (Duffing)** frequency | | 4 | $T_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{p}{q}T_d \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \omega_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{q}{p}\omega_d$ | **Locking condition** (rational frequency ratio) | | 5 | $\Delta\phi = \phi(t) - \phi_d(t) = \text{const.}$ | **Phase-locked** definition (constant phase difference) | | 6 | $p/q \;=\; \text{rot. number of circle map}$ | **Winding / rotation number** on the circle | | 7 | $\gamma_{\text{min}}(\Omega) = \big[(\omega_0^{2}-\omega^{2})^{2} + (2\beta\omega)^{2}\big]^{1/2}\cdot A_{\min}$ | **Threshold forcing** for existence of a 1:1 lock | **Critical Reminder for Table Cell Contents:** All vertical-bar absolute values (e.g. $\vert x\vert$) are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` so as **not** to break markdown table syntax. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > Phase-locking is the operating principle of: > - **Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs)** in every radio, Wi-Fi, and GPS receiver. > - **Josephson junctions** in superconducting electronics and metrology (voltage standard). > - **Cardiac pacemaker cells** synchronizing to the SA-node rhythm. > - **Laser mode-locking** for ultra-short pulse generation. > - **Power-grid synchronization** of generators to a common frequency. > - **MEMS/NEMS resonators** where nonlinear stiffening can either *help* (lock-in sensors) or *hurt* (hysteresis in gyroscopes). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Frequency-Response and Phase Equations for 1:1 Phase-Locked Solution of the Forced Duffing Oscillator We start from $$\ddot{x} + 2\beta\dot{x} + \omega_0^{2}x + \alpha x^{3} = \gamma\cos(\omega t). \tag{3.1}$$ **Step 1 — Locking ansatz.** Assume a 1:1 phase-locked periodic solution of the form $$x(t) = A\cos(\omega t - \Delta). \tag{3.2}$$ Here $A>0$ is the response amplitude and $\Delta$ is the (constant) phase lag with respect to the drive. **Step 2 — Compute the time derivatives.** $$\dot{x}(t) = -A\omega\sin(\omega t - \Delta), \tag{3.3}$$ $$\ddot{x}(t) = -A\omega^{2}\cos(\omega t - \Delta). \tag{3.4}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the cubic nonlinearity.** Using the trigonometric identity $$\cos^{3}\theta = \tfrac{1}{4}\cos(3\theta) + \tfrac{3}{4}\cos\theta,$$ we obtain $$x^{3} = A^{3}\cos^{3}(\omega t - \Delta) = \tfrac{A^{3}}{4}\cos\!\big(3(\omega t - \Delta)\big) + \tfrac{3A^{3}}{4}\cos(\omega t - \Delta). \tag{3.5}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the harmonic-balance filter.** Since we are seeking a *fundamental-frequency* 1:1 phase-locked solution, we keep only the $\cos(\omega t-\Delta)$ and $\sin(\omega t-\Delta)$ components (we drop the third-harmonic $\cos 3(\omega t-\Delta)$ — this is the *fundamental-harmonic approximation*, valid when $\alpha A^{2}$ is small compared to $\omega_0^{2}$). The third-harmonic term is what is responsible for the *small* waveform distortion. **Step 5 — Substitute and regroup.** Inserting (3.2)–(3.5) into (3.1), and using $$\cos(\omega t) = \cos\!\big((\omega t - \Delta) + \Delta\big) = \cos(\omega t-\Delta)\cos\Delta - \sin(\omega t-\Delta)\sin\Delta,$$ we collect the coefficient of $\cos(\omega t-\Delta)$: $$\text{coeff}_{\cos} = -A\omega^{2} + \omega_0^{2}A + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{3}}{4} - \gamma\cos\Delta, \tag{3.6}$$ and the coefficient of $\sin(\omega t-\Delta)$: $$\text{coeff}_{\sin} = 2\beta\omega A - \gamma\sin\Delta. \tag{3.7}$$ **Step 6 — Impose equilibrium (vanishing of both coefficients).** For the trial solution (3.2) to be an *exact* periodic orbit, both projections must be zero: $$\begin{aligned} A(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2}) + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{3}}{4} &= \gamma\cos\Delta, \tag{3.8}\\[4pt] 2\beta\omega A &= \gamma\sin\Delta. \tag{3.9} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7 — Eliminate the phase $\Delta$ using $\sin^{2}\Delta + \cos^{2}\Delta = 1$.** Square (3.8) and (3.9) and add: $$\big[(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2})A + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{3}}{4}\big]^{2} + (2\beta\omega A)^{2} = \gamma^{2}. \tag{3.10}$$ Dividing (3.9) by (3.8): $$\tan\Delta = \frac{2\beta\omega}{(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2}) + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{2}}{4}}. \tag{3.11}$$ Equations (3.10) and (3.11) are the **frequency-response** and **phase** equations respectively, listed as formulas #1 and #2 in the cheat-sheet table. **Step 8 — Solve the cubic amplitude equation.** Equation (3.10) is a polynomial of degree 6 in $A$, but because it depends only on $A^{2}$, it reduces to a cubic in $u = A^{2}$: $$\big[(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2}) + \tfrac{3\alpha u}{4}\big]^{2} u + 4\beta^{2}\omega^{2} u = \gamma^{2}. \tag{3.12}$$ This cubic has, in general, 1 or 3 real positive roots — each real positive root corresponds to a *phase-locked periodic solution*. The bi-stability (3 roots) is the origin of the famous **jump phenomenon** in Duffing oscillators. **Step 9 — Saddle-node (saddle-node) creation of phase-locked solutions.** A pair of phase-locked solutions is *born* (or annihilated) when the cubic discriminant vanishes, i.e. when $$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial A}\Big[(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2})A + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{3}}{4}\Big]^{2} + (2\beta\omega A)^{2} - \gamma^{2} = 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{(3.10)} = 0.$$ Geometrically, this is where the frequency-response curve is *vertical*. In the $(\gamma, \omega)$ parameter plane, the locus of these vertical tangents forms the **saddle-node bifurcation curves** — the boundaries of the **Arnold tongues** (1:1 tongue in our case). ### 3.2 General $p{:}q$ Phase-Locked Solution For a $p{:}q$ lock, the response contains $p$ oscillations of the underlying system for every $q$ cycles of the drive. The ansatz generalises to a Fourier series: $$x(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{p-1} A_{k}\cos\!\Big(\tfrac{\omega t}{q} - \Delta_{k}\Big) + \text{higher harmonics},$$ subject to the constraints imposed by the symmetry group of order $p$ of the orbit. The **circle-map reduction** (next subsection) is the cleanest way to handle this. ### 3.3 Reduction to a Circle Map (Winding-Number Picture) > [!NOTE] > The most general KTU-relevant derivation of phase-locking for *any* (weakly) periodically forced oscillator is via the **Poincaré stroboscopic map**: sample the state $(\dot{x}, x)$ at times $t_n = 2\pi n/\omega_d$. The sequence $(x_n, \dot{x}_n)$ is governed by a 2-D area-preserving map (or contracting map if damping is present). In the strongly damped (overdamped) limit, the radial coordinate collapses and the dynamics reduces to a *1-D circle map*: > $$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - \dfrac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod{1},$$ > where $\theta_n$ is the phase difference (mod 1) at the $n$-th strobe, $\Omega$ is the (normalized) detuning, and $K$ is the forcing strength. A **phase-locked solution** corresponds to a *periodic* orbit of this map: $\theta_{n+p} = \theta_n$ for some integer $p$, which forces $\Omega = K\cdot(\text{finite rational})$. The set of $(\Omega, K)$ values for which such a $p$-cycle exists is the **Arnold tongue of order $p$**. ### 3.4 Stability of a Phase-Locked Solution (Floquet Theory) Once a phase-locked periodic orbit $x^{\*}(t)$ is found, its stability is determined by the **monodromy matrix** $\mathbf{M}$, the fundamental-matrix solution of the variational equation evaluated over one drive period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$: $$\dot{\boldsymbol{\eta}} = \mathbf{A}(t)\,\boldsymbol{\eta}, \qquad \mathbf{A}(t) = \mathbf{A}(t+T_d).$$ The phase-locked solution is **orbitally stable** iff both **Floquet multipliers** $\rho_{1,2}$ satisfy $\vert\rho_{1,2}\vert \le 1$. For the forced Duffing oscillator, one multiplier is *always* $\rho_1 = e^{-\beta T_d} < 1$ (damping contraction), and the *non-trivial* multiplier is $$\rho_2 = e^{-\beta T_d}\!\left[1 - \pi T_d\,\dfrac{\partial(\text{phase condition})}{\partial\Delta}\right]$$ (approximately). A **period-doubling bifurcation** occurs when $\rho_2$ crosses $-1$, signalling the birth of a *2:1* subharmonic phase-locked solution. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Concept Map — Phase-Locked Solutions in a Forced Nonlinear Oscillator ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Forced Nonlinear Oscillator] --> B{Augmented Phase Space
x, v, theta = omega t} B --> C[Stroboscopic Sampling
t_n = 2 pi n / omega_d] C --> D[2-D Poincare Map] D --> E[Overdamped Limit] E --> F[1-D Circle Map
theta_{n+1} = F theta_n mod 1] F --> G[Fixed Point: 1:1 Lock] F --> H[p-Cycle: p:q Lock] F --> I[Quasiperiodic Drift] F --> J[Chaotic Orbit] G --> K[Floquet Stability Check] H --> K K -->|rho within unit disk| L[Stable Phase-Locked Solution] K -->|rho crosses +1| M[Saddle-Node Bifurcation] K -->|rho crosses -1| N[Period-Doubling Bifurcation] M --> O[Arnold Tongue Boundary] N --> P[Subharmonic Cascade to Chaos] L --> Q[Engineering Applications:
PLL, MEMS, Power Grid] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) ```mermaid flowchart LR VCO[VCO
Voltage-Controlled
Oscillator] -->|output x_osc| MIX[Phase Detector / Mixer] REF[Reference Input
x_ref at omega_ref] --> MIX MIX -->|error e = x_ref - x_osc| LF[Loop Filter
Low-Pass] LF -->|control voltage u| VCO VCO -.->|"locked when
omega_osc = omega_ref"| OUT[Phase-Locked Output] LF -->|bandwidth sets capture range| CAP[Capture / Lock Range] CAP --> OUT ``` ### 4.3 Schematic — Arnold Tongues in the $(\omega, \gamma)$ Parameter Plane ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Parameter Plane: Drive Frequency omega vs Drive Amplitude gamma] --> T1[1:1 Arnold Tongue
Bounded by Saddle-Node Curves] P --> T2[1:2 Arnold Tongue
Subharmonic] P --> T3[2:1 Arnold Tongue] P --> T4[3:1, 3:2 Tongues] P --> OUTS[Outside All Tongues:
Quasiperiodic / Drift / Chaos] T1 --> INS[Inside Tongue: Phase-Locked Periodic Orbit] T2 --> INS T3 --> INS T4 --> INS OUTS --> DEV[Devil's Staircase on Boundaries] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** Each tongue is widest near the linear resonance $\omega \approx \omega_0$ and narrows to zero as $\gamma \to 0$ (no forcing, no locking). The tongues are organized by their rational winding number $p/q$, and they densely fill the $(\omega, \gamma)$ plane as $\gamma$ grows — an *analytic* realization of the rationals being dense in the reals. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. Define a phase-locked solution. State the locking condition. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **Model Answer:** A periodic solution $x(t)$ of a forced nonlinear system $\ddot{x} + f(x,\dot{x}) = \gamma\cos(\omega t)$ is called **phase-locked** if its period $T_{\text{res}}$ is an exact *rational multiple* of the drive period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega$: $$T_{\text{res}} = \frac{p}{q}\,T_d, \qquad p, q \in \mathbb{Z}^+, \quad \gcd(p,q)=1.$$ Equivalently, in the strobed Poincaré section at $t_n = nT_d$, the phase difference $\Delta\theta_n = \theta(t_n) - \omega t_n$ is **constant** for all $n$ (a fixed point of the stroboscopic map). A $1{:}1$ lock corresponds to *synchrony* — exactly one oscillation of the response per cycle of the drive. --- #### Q2. What is an Arnold tongue? Sketch its qualitative shape. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Model Answer:** An **Arnold tongue** is a region in the parameter plane of *drive frequency $\omega$* (or detuning $\Omega = \omega-\omega_0$) versus *drive amplitude $\gamma$* in which a phase-locked solution with a specific rational rotation number $p/q$ exists. For the 1:1 case it is bounded above and below by two **saddle-node bifurcation curves** of the periodic orbit, both meeting at $\gamma = 0$, $\omega = \omega_0$. Tongues of higher order ($p/q = 1/2, 2/1, 3/2, \dots$) nest between the dominant tongues and collectively fill the parameter plane as $\gamma$ increases. *Sketch:* symmetric "V-shape" or "tongue" widening with $\gamma$, vertex at $(\omega_0, 0)$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students frequently *forget the third-harmonic filter* in harmonic balance and obtain a wrong frequency-response curve. The correct curve is the *fundamental* amplitude equation, and the small third-harmonic content of the true solution is dropped at leading order. **Do not** equate (3.1) to a single sinusoid without explicitly applying the cubic-identity filter. > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** When asked for "Arnold tongues", many students draw a *single* tongue and forget the higher-order rationals. Always mention at least the $1{:}1$, $1{:}2$, and $2{:}1$ tongues and explain the **devil's staircase** structure of the boundaries. #### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]` **(a)** Starting from the forced Duffing oscillator $$\ddot{x} + 2\beta\dot{x} + \omega_0^{2}x + \alpha x^{3} = \gamma\cos(\omega t),$$ use the **method of harmonic balance** to derive the amplitude and phase equations for a 1:1 phase-locked solution $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t - \Delta)$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With $\omega_0 = 1$, $\beta = 0.1$, $\alpha = 0.5$, and $\gamma = 0.3$, compute the amplitude and phase lag for a 1:1 phase-locked solution at drive frequency $\omega = 1.05\,\text{rad/s}$. Discuss the stability of the solution and identify the bifurcation that would occur if $\gamma$ were slowly increased. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### Model Solution — Q.A(a) **Step 1:** Write the trial solution $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t - \Delta)$, with $A>0$. **Step 2:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos^{3}\theta = \tfrac{1}{4}\cos 3\theta + \tfrac{3}{4}\cos\theta$ to the cubic term: $$x^{3} = \tfrac{A^{3}}{4}\cos\!\big(3(\omega t-\Delta)\big) + \tfrac{3A^{3}}{4}\cos(\omega t-\Delta).$$ **Step 3:** Under the **fundamental-harmonic approximation**, discard the third-harmonic term and substitute into the Duffing equation. Project onto $\cos(\omega t - \Delta)$ and $\sin(\omega t - \Delta)$ basis functions. **Step 4:** Equating coefficients yields the two scalar algebraic equations: $$\begin{aligned} A(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2}) + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{3}}{4} &= \gamma\cos\Delta, \\[2pt] 2\beta\omega A &= \gamma\sin\Delta. \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Eliminating $\Delta$ by squaring and adding gives the **frequency-response equation**: $$\big[(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2})A + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{3}}{4}\big]^{2} + (2\beta\omega A)^{2} = \gamma^{2}. \qquad\blacksquare$$ The phase equation is $$\tan\Delta = \frac{2\beta\omega}{(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2}) + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{2}}{4}}. \qquad\blacksquare$$ --- ##### Model Solution — Q.A(b) **Numerical evaluation:** Given: $\omega_0 = 1$, $\beta = 0.1$, $\alpha = 0.5$, $\gamma = 0.3$, $\omega = 1.05$. **Step 1:** Compute the linear detuning: $$\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2} = 1 - 1.05^{2} = 1 - 1.1025 = -0.1025.$$ **Step 2:** Compute the damping term: $$2\beta\omega = 2 \times 0.1 \times 1.05 = 0.21.$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the frequency-response equation. The amplitude equation in $u = A^{2}$ becomes $$\big[-0.1025 + 0.375\,u\big]^{2} u + 0.0441\,u = 0.09.$$ (Here $\tfrac{3\alpha}{4} = 0.375$ and $(2\beta\omega)^{2} = 0.0441$.) **Step 4:** Solving this cubic numerically. Let $f(u) = (-0.1025 + 0.375u)^{2}u + 0.0441u - 0.09$. Trial $u = 0.5$: $f(0.5) = (0.085)^{2}(0.5) + 0.0221 - 0.09 = 0.0036 + 0.0221 - 0.09 = -0.0643$. Trial $u = 0.8$: $f(0.8) = (0.1975)^{2}(0.8) + 0.0353 - 0.09 = 0.0312 + 0.0353 - 0.09 = -0.0235$. Trial $u = 1.0$: $f(1.0) = (0.2725)^{2}(1.0) + 0.0441 - 0.09 = 0.0743 + 0.0441 - 0.09 = 0.0284$. Trial $u = 0.9$: $f(0.9) = (0.235)^{2}(0.9) + 0.0397 - 0.09 = 0.0497 + 0.0397 - 0.09 = -0.0006 \approx 0$. So $u^{\*} \approx 0.901$, giving $A^{\*} = \sqrt{0.901} \approx 0.949$. **Step 5:** Phase lag: $$\tan\Delta = \frac{0.21}{-0.1025 + 0.375 \times 0.901} = \frac{0.21}{-0.1025 + 0.3379} = \frac{0.21}{0.2354} \approx 0.8923,$$ so $\Delta \approx \arctan(0.8923) \approx 41.7^{\circ} \approx 0.728\,\text{rad}$. **Step 6:** Stability. Compute the slope of the frequency-response curve at $A = A^{\*}$. Because $\alpha > 0$ (hardening Duffing), and we are on the *upper* branch of the bi-stable response, the non-trivial Floquet multiplier satisfies $\vert\rho_2\vert < 1$ — the **phase-locked solution is stable**. **Step 7:** Bifurcation. As $\gamma$ is slowly increased, the response amplitude $A$ grows and the effective Duffing frequency $$\omega_{\text{eff}}(A) = \omega_0 + \frac{3\alpha}{8\omega_0}A^{2}$$ increases. When $\omega_{\text{eff}}$ becomes twice $\omega_d$, a **period-doubling (flip) bifurcation** occurs, giving birth to a $2{:}1$ phase-locked (subharmonic) solution — the gateway to the **period-doubling route to chaos**. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2 — Internal Choice]` **(a)** Define the **rotation number** of a phase-locked solution of a forced nonlinear oscillator. Show that for the circle map $$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod{1},$$ a phase-locked solution of period $p$ exists if and only if $\Omega = (K/2\pi)\,g(\theta^{\*})$ for some appropriate $g$, and that the corresponding **Arnold tongue boundary** is given by $\Omega = \pm K/(2\pi)$. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Sketch the **Arnold-tongue diagram** for the circle map in the $(\Omega, K)$ plane up to $|K| \le 4\pi$. Identify the $1{:}1$, $1{:}2$, and $2{:}1$ tongues. Explain the physical meaning of the **devil's staircase** structure on the rotation-number axis and the **mode-locking gap theorem**. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### Model Solution — Q.B(a) **Definition of rotation number.** For the sequence $\theta_n$ generated by iteration of the map $F$, the **rotation number** is $$\rho = \lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{F^{n}(\theta_0) - \theta_0}{n} \pmod{1},$$ which measures the *average* phase advance per strobe. A **phase-locked** solution has rational rotation number $\rho = p/q$, meaning $F^{q}(\theta^{\*}) = \theta^{\*} + p$ for some fixed point $\theta^{\*}$ of the $q$-th iterate. **Derivation of the Arnold-tongue boundary.** For a $p{:}1$ phase-locked solution, set $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + p$ (mod 1) in the circle map: $$p = \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta^{\*}), \quad \text{so} \quad \Omega = p + \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta^{\*}).$$ Since $\sin(\cdot) \in [-1, 1]$, the *extremal* values of $\Omega$ for which a solution can exist are $$\Omega_{\pm} = p \pm \frac{K}{2\pi}.$$ This pair of straight lines is the **boundary of the 1:1 Arnold tongue** in the $(\Omega, K)$ plane — a perfect V-shaped tongue. Higher-order tongues ($p{:}q$) are obtained analogously, yielding tongues with widths $\propto K/q$. --- ##### Model Solution — Q.B(b) **Sketch (verbal description for the student to render):** A family of V-shaped tongues opens from the $\Omega = p$ axis at $K = 0$. The $1{:}1$ tongue is centred on $\Omega = 1$, the $1{:}2$ on $\Omega = 1/2$, the $2{:}1$ on $\Omega = 2$, etc. All tongues widen linearly with $K$. **Devil's staircase.** Plotting the rotation number $\rho$ versus $\Omega$ (at a fixed $K > K_{\text{crit}}$) yields a monotonically non-decreasing function that is *constant* on each interval of $\Omega$ corresponding to a phase-locked solution, and *jumps* by rational increments at the boundaries. Because the rationals are dense, the "staircase" is a fractal-like function: the *measure* of $\Omega$-values giving quasiperiodicity (non-locked) goes to zero as $K$ increases, yet the locked set is nowhere dense in the dynamical sense. **Mode-locking gap theorem (Arnold, 1983).** For the family of circle maps $\theta \mapsto \theta + \Omega + (K/2\pi)\sin(2\pi\theta) \pmod 1$ with $K > 1$, the Lebesgue measure of the set of $\Omega$ for which $\rho$ is *irrational* (i.e., no phase-locking) is *strictly less than* $\sqrt{1 - K^{-2}}$. In particular, when $K$ is large, *almost every* $\Omega$ yields a phase-locked solution — a strong statement of the prevalence of mode-locking in strongly forced systems. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-remember bullets for the KTU ESE viva & written exam:** - A **phase-locked solution** satisfies $T_{\text{res}} = (p/q) T_d$ with $p, q \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, $\gcd(p,q) = 1$. - A 1:1 lock means $\omega_{\text{res}} = \omega_d$ — synchronous response. - **Phase-lag definition:** $\Delta\phi = \phi_{\text{response}} - \phi_{\text{drive}} = \text{const}$ for a true lock. - **Method of harmonic balance** + cubic-identity filter $\cos^{3}\theta = \tfrac{1}{4}\cos 3\theta + \tfrac{3}{4}\cos\theta$ is the workhorse derivation. - **Duffing frequency-response equation:** $\big[(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2})A + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{3}}{4}\big]^{2} + (2\beta\omega A)^{2} = \gamma^{2}$. - **Phase equation:** $\tan\Delta = \dfrac{2\beta\omega}{(\omega_0^{2} - \omega^{2}) + \tfrac{3\alpha A^{2}}{4}}$. - **Nonlinear backbone:** $\omega_{B}(A) = \omega_0 + \dfrac{3\alpha}{8\omega_0}A^{2}$ (hardening if $\alpha > 0$). - A cubic in $u = A^{2}$ yields up to **three real positive amplitudes** → jump phenomenon and bi-stability. - Phase-locked solutions are **born / destroyed in saddle-node bifurcations** as parameters are varied; the locus of these bifurcations is the **Arnold tongue boundary**. - **Arnold tongues** in the $(\omega, \gamma)$ plane are V-shaped regions (or more general shapes) where a $p{:}q$ lock exists. - **Stability** is checked by **Floquet theory**: monodromy matrix $\mathbf{M}$ evaluated over one period; multipliers $\rho_i$ must satisfy $\vert\rho_i\vert \le 1$. - **Period-doubling bifurcation** at $\rho_2 = -1$ creates $2{:}1$ subharmonic phase-locked solutions → cascade to chaos. - **Circle map reduction** (overdamped limit): $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - \tfrac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod 1$. - **Devil's staircase**: the function $\rho(\Omega)$ at fixed $K$ is a non-decreasing fractal-like curve, flat on locked intervals. - **Mode-locking gap theorem** (Arnold): for $K > 1$, irrational rotation numbers occupy a *measure* $\le \sqrt{1 - K^{-2}}$ of the $\Omega$-axis → most parameters give locking. - **Engineering payoff**: PLLs, MEMS, power-grid sync, cardiac dynamics, Josephson junctions, laser mode-locking. ---

topics.title: Bifurcations and Chaos in Circle Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bifurcations and Chaos in Circle Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Circle Map** is a continuous, piecewise-monotone mapping from the unit circle $S^1$ onto itself. It is the simplest dynamical system capable of exhibiting the competition between two incommensurate frequencies — i.e., it sits at the precise mathematical interface between **quasiperiodicity** (locked motion on a torus) and **deterministic chaos**.

The canonical form, introduced by **Vladimir Arnold (1965)** and studied extensively by Feigenbaum, Jensen & Bak, is the **Standard Circle Map (Arnold Map)**:

$$\theta_{n+1} = f_K(\theta_n) = \theta_n + \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod 1$$

where:
- $\theta \in [0, 1)$ is the angular coordinate on the unit circle.
- $\Omega \in [0, 1]$ is the bare (unperturbed) rotation number — the ratio of the two driving frequencies.
- $K \geq 0$ is the **nonlinearity / coupling strength** parameter.
- All arithmetic is taken **modulo 1**, ensuring the state remains on the circle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight**: The circle map is the *prototype* dynamical model for any physical system with two competing periodicities — Josephson junctions, cardiac pacemaker cells, forced pendulums, and phase-locked loops. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly demands understanding the **bifurcation sequence** that takes this map from order (periodicity) to chaos.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Clock" Intuition

Imagine **two clocks** mounted side by side:
- Clock A ticks at frequency $f_1$ (representing the natural drive $\Omega$).
- Clock B ticks at frequency $f_2$ (representing the intrinsic rotation).

If $f_1/f_2$ is rational (say $p/q$), the clocks will beat in unison every $q$ ticks — this is **mode-locking** (periodic behavior). If $f_1/f_2$ is irrational, the hands drift forever and never realign — this is **quasiperiodicity**.

Now introduce a small "tugging" force $K$ between the two clocks (a coupling that pulls them toward synchronization). As $K$ increases:
- The rational peaks **widen** into **Arnold tongues** — wider regions of the parameter space get forcibly locked.
- Beyond $K = 1$, the pulling becomes so strong that the dynamics become **chaotic** — like the two clocks fighting violently, never settling into any rhythm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary**: A circle map is a mathematical "compass needle" that gets nudged by an external rhythm. The competition between its *own* rhythm ($\Omega$) and the *external* rhythm ($K \sin$ term) produces every transition from order → quasiperiodicity → mode-locking → chaos. It is the *Rosetta Stone* of nonlinear dynamics.

---

### 1.3 Phase Space — Why a Circle and Not a Line?

| Property | Line ($f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$) | Circle ($f: S^1 \to S^1$) |
|----------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Boundary | Two unbounded ends | One single point ($\theta=0 \equiv 1$) |
| Topology | Open interval | **Compact** (closed, bounded) |
| Periodic orbit uniqueness | May have many | Inherently single-cycle wrap |
| Rotation number | Not defined | **Well-defined** $\rho$ |
| Mode-locking | Not natural | Native phenomenon |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams**: The compactness of $S^1$ guarantees that **every trajectory returns arbitrarily close to its initial condition** (Poincaré Recurrence). This is what makes the rotation number $\rho$ a *well-defined asymptotic invariant* — and it is the central object in circle map theory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric and Analytical Structure

The circle map $f_K(\theta)$ is a **degree-1 circle homeomorphism** with the following hierarchical structural properties:

1. **Lifting to the real line**: Every circle map lifts to a monotone-nondecreasing function $F: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ satisfying:
$$F(x+1) = F(x) + 1$$
2. **Conjugacy to a pure rotation**: For $K = 0$, the map degenerates to a pure rigid rotation $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega \pmod 1$, i.e., $F(x) = x + \Omega$.
3. **Smoothness**: $f_K \in C^\infty$ (infinitely differentiable), allowing classical calculus-based stability analysis.
4. **Monotonicity threshold**: $f_K$ is monotone-increasing iff $K \leq 1$ (the **critical nonlinearity**).

The map is **invertible** for all $K$ because its derivative never changes sign on the relevant region — chaos here arises not from folding (as in the logistic map) but from **sensitive dependence on initial conditions** combined with topological constraints.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — The Unperturbed Rotation ($K = 0$).**
For $K = 0$, $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega \pmod 1$. The orbit is:
$$\theta_n = \theta_0 + n\Omega \pmod 1$$
- If $\Omega = p/q$ (rational): orbit is periodic with period $q$.
- If $\Omega$ is irrational: orbit is **dense** on $S^1$ (never repeats).

**Step 2 — Perturbation Onset ($0 < K \ll 1$).**
The sinusoidal term slightly distorts the rigid rotation. Rational orbits persist by **structural persistence** (topological conjugacy preserved), irrational orbits become *quasiperiodic* on a deformed curve.

**Step 3 — Mode-Locking Onset.**
Around each rational $\Omega = p/q$, an **Arnold tongue** opens — a region in $(\Omega, K)$ parameter space within which the rotation number locks to exactly $p/q$.

**Step 4 — Tangent Bifurcation ($K = 1$).**
At the critical nonlinearity $K = 1$, the map $f_K$ has an **inflection point** where $f'(\theta) = 0$ (vanishing derivative). This is the **saddle-node bifurcation** in disguise — and marks the boundary of monotonicity.

**Step 5 — Period-Doubling Cascade ($K > 1$).**
Inside the Arnold tongues, a **Feigenbaum period-doubling cascade** unfolds. Each successive bifurcation doubles the period: $q \to 2q \to 4q \to \cdots$, accumulating at a finite $K_\infty$.

**Step 6 — Onset of Chaos ($K \gtrsim 1$).**
Beyond $K = 1$, the system exhibits **fully developed chaos** with a **positive Lyapunov exponent**. Strange attractors and sensitive dependence appear.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Range |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Standard Circle Map | $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - \dfrac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod 1$ | Defines the dynamics |
| 2 | Derivative | $f_K'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos(2\pi\theta)$ | Slope at angle $\theta$ |
| 3 | Monotonicity Condition | $\vert f_K'(\theta) \vert > 0 \;\; \forall \theta \;\Longleftrightarrow\; K \leq 1$ | Critical threshold $K_c = 1$ |
| 4 | Rotation Number (lift) | $\rho = \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{F^n(x) - x}{n}$ | Invariant of the orbit |
| 5 | Lyapunov Exponent | $\lambda = \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \ln\vert f_K'(\theta_i) \vert$ | $\lambda<0$ stable, $\lambda>0$ chaotic |
| 6 | Critical Lyapunov | $\lambda = 0$ at bifurcation; $\lambda = \ln K$ at onset of chaos | Marks transition points |
| 7 | Mode-Locking Condition | $\rho(\Omega, K) = \dfrac{p}{q}$, $p, q \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Rational rotation number |
| 8 | Tongue Width (approx) | $\Delta\Omega_{p/q} \approx \dfrac{2}{\pi}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{1}{K^{2}}}$ for $K>1$ | Grows with $K$ |
| 9 | Feigenbaum Constant | $\delta = 4.6692016\ldots$ | Universal ratio for period-doubling |
| 10 | Inverse Iteration | $\theta_n = F^{-n}(\theta_0) \pmod 1$ | Recovering the past |
| 11 | Conjugacy to Rotation | $h(\theta_{n+1}) = h(\theta_n) + \rho$ via homeomorphism $h$ | For $K < 1$ |
| 12 | Bifurcation Value | $K = 1$ | Tangent (saddle-node) bifurcation point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Valuation Tip**: When asked to *compute* the rotation number, the lift formula $\rho = \lim (F^n(x)-x)/n$ is preferred over direct iteration, because it does not depend on the modulo wrapping and gives a unique, unambiguous value.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Circle Maps |
|--------------------|----------------------------|
| **Superconducting Electronics** | Josephson junctions driven by AC + DC bias (the resistively-shunted junction model is mathematically identical to the circle map) |
| **Telecommunications** | Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs) in radio receivers — mode-locking = signal lock, chaos = unlock/jamming |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Cardiac arrhythmia modeling — ventricular fibrillation viewed as chaotic mode competition |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Forced Duffing-like oscillators and gear-rattle systems |
| **Power Systems** | Sub-synchronous resonance and frequency-locking phenomena |
| **Optics** | Mode-locked lasers, frequency combs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Insight**: The Josephson junction equation $V = (\hbar/2e)\dot\phi$ together with the AC-driven current gives precisely the Arnold circle map. This is why NIST's voltage standard uses **mode-locked** Josephson arrays — they exploit the *Arnold tongues* to lock to a quantum-accurate frequency ratio.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rotation Number

We work with the **lifted** real-line version of the map, defined for $x \in \mathbb{R}$:

$$F(x) = x + \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi x)$$

Since $F(x+1) = F(x) + 1$, the lift property holds automatically. The rotation number is the average angular advance per iteration:

$$\rho(K, \Omega) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{F^n(x) - x}{n}$$

**Step 1 — Unperturbed case** ($K = 0$):

$$F^n(x) = x + n\Omega$$

Therefore:

$$\rho(0, \Omega) = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{x + n\Omega - x}{n} = \Omega$$

This is the trivial rotation — every point advances by exactly $\Omega$ per iteration.

**Step 2 — Small perturbation** ($0 < K \ll 1$):
We expand $F$ around the unperturbed rotation. Using the implicit function theorem for circle homeomorphisms (Denjoy's theorem for $C^1$ diffeomorphisms with $\vert f' \vert < 1$):

$$\rho(K,\Omega) = \Omega + O(K)$$

Hence for *irrational* $\Omega$ and small $K$, the rotation number stays irrational — the orbit is quasiperiodic on a smooth invariant curve.

**Step 3 — Period-locked regime**:
If $\rho = p/q$ exactly, then iterating $q$ times brings us back to a fixed point of $F^q$:

$$F^q(x) - x = p$$

This is the defining condition for mode-locking — there exists a stable periodic orbit of period $q$ with rotation number $p/q$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Lyapunov Exponent

The Lyapunov exponent measures exponential divergence (or convergence) of nearby trajectories:

$$\lambda(K, \Omega) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \ln \left| f_K'(\theta_i) \right|$$

**Step 1 — Compute the derivative:**

$$f_K'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos(2\pi\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Sign analysis:**

For $K < 1$: $\vert f_K'(\theta) \vert \in [1-K,\; 1+K]$, so $\ln\vert f_K'\vert$ is always negative $\Rightarrow$ $\lambda < 0$ (stable quasiperiodicity or periodic motion).

For $K = 1$: At $\theta = 0$, $f_K'(0) = 1 - K = 0$, so $\ln 0 = -\infty$ at a single point — the map is **non-invertible** (critical point appears).

For $K > 1$: $\vert f_K'(0) \vert = 1-K < 0$ — derivative becomes **negative**, map is **non-monotone**, and folds appear. Time-reversed chaotic regions coexist with stable ones.

**Step 3 — Ensemble average over an orbit:**
For a periodic orbit of period $q$ at parameters $(\Omega, K)$:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{q} \sum_{i=0}^{q-1} \ln \left| 1 - K\cos(2\pi\theta_i) \right|$$

**Step 4 — Critical transition:**
At the **chaos threshold** ($K \approx 1$, irrational $\Omega$), the Lyapunov exponent crosses zero:

$$\lambda(K \to 1^-) = 0$$

For $K$ slightly greater than 1:

$$\lambda \approx \ln K > 0$$

This marks the **onset of chaos**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Arnold Tongue Geometry

For each rational $p/q$ with $p, q$ coprime, define the mode-locked region:

$$T_{p/q} = \left\{ (K,\Omega) \,\Big|\, \rho(K,\Omega) = \frac{p}{q} \right\}$$

**Step 1 — Low-$K$ expansion (tongue near $\Omega = p/q$):**
For $K \ll 1$, the width of the tongue scales linearly:

$$\Delta\Omega_{p/q}(K) \approx K \cdot \left| \frac{d\rho}{d\Omega} \right|^{-1} \approx K$$

Specifically, for the $1/1$ tongue:

$$\Omega \in \left[\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{K^2}},\; 1 - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{K^2}}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Tongue boundary equation:**
The boundary of $T_{p/q}$ is determined by the **saddle-node bifurcation** of $F^q$:

$$F^q(x) - x - p = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d}{dx}[F^q(x)] = 1$$

The latter condition is equivalent to the existence of an inflection point of period $q$ with multiplier $+1$.
