**Step 3 — Tongue ordering (Farey tree):**
Tongues with simpler rationals ($p/q = 1/2, 1/3, 2/3, 1/4, \ldots$) appear closest to the $K=0$ axis, and tongues with higher-denominator rationals appear at larger $K$. Their parameter values are organized by the **Farey tree** mediants: between $p/q$ and $p'/q'$, the tongue at $(p+p')/(q+q')$ emerges at intermediate $K$.

---

### 3.4 Bifurcation Sequence Inside an Arnold Tongue

Consider the $p/q = 1/1$ tongue. As $K$ increases at fixed $\Omega = $ irrational:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 (period-1)}:&\quad \theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \rho \pmod 1, \quad \rho = 1 \\
\text{Stage 2 (period-2)}:&\quad \theta_{n+2} = \theta_n \pmod 1, \quad \theta_{n+1} \neq \theta_n \\
\text{Stage 3 (period-4)}:&\quad \theta_{n+4} = \theta_n \pmod 1 \\
\vdots \\
\text{Stage }\infty\text{ (chaos)}:&\quad \lambda > 0, \text{ aperiodic bounded orbit}
\end{aligned}$$

The successive bifurcation values $K_1 < K_2 < K_3 < \cdots$ obey the **Feigenbaum scaling**:

$$\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{K_{n+1} - K_n}{K_{n+2} - K_{n+1}} = \delta = 4.6692016\ldots$$

This is **universal** — the same constant governs period-doubling in the logistic map, the sine map, and any one-dimensional map with a quadratic maximum.

---

### 3.5 Critical Point Analysis at $K = 1$

At $K = 1$, the derivative $f_K'(\theta) = 1 - \cos(2\pi\theta)$ vanishes at $\theta = 0$. Expanding to second order:

$$f_1(\theta) \approx \theta + \Omega - \frac{1}{2\pi}(2\pi\theta) = \theta + \Omega - \theta = \Omega$$

Wait — let us redo this carefully. Expanding $\sin(2\pi\theta) \approx 2\pi\theta - \frac{(2\pi\theta)^3}{6}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_1(\theta) &= \theta + \Omega - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta) \pmod 1 \\
&\approx \theta + \Omega - \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[2\pi\theta - \frac{(2\pi\theta)^3}{6}\right] \\
&\approx \theta + \Omega - \theta + \frac{(2\pi\theta)^3}{12\pi} \\
&= \Omega + \frac{\pi^2 \theta^3}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

So near $\theta = 0$, the map behaves like a **cubic** with a flat inflection — this is precisely the **saddle-node bifurcation** scenario, where a stable and unstable fixed point are born together. This is the geometric origin of the **critical threshold** $K = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Circle Map System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Condition theta_0 in S1] --> B[Compute sin(2*pi*theta_n)]
    B --> C[Apply Nonlinearity Term: -K sin over 2*pi]
    C --> D[Add Linear Drive: + Omega]
    D --> E[Sum: theta_n + Omega - K sin/2pi]
    E --> F[Modulo 1 Operation]
    F --> G[theta_n+1 on Circle]
    G --> H{More Iterations?}
    H -- Yes --> I[n = n+1]
    I --> B
    H -- No --> J[Output Trajectory in S1]
    G --> K[Compute Lyapunov Exponent]
    K --> L{lambda > 0?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Chaotic Regime]
    L -- No --> N{rho = p/q rational?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Mode Locked Periodic]
    N -- No --> P[Quasiperiodic Orbit]
```

### 4.2 Bifurcation Roadmap — From Order to Chaos

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stage 1: K = 0 (Pure Rotation)"]
    S2["Stage 2: 0 less than K less than 1 (Quasiperiodicity on T2)"]
    S3["Stage 3: Mode Locking Inside Arnold Tongues"]
    S4["Stage 4: Period Doubling Cascade"]
    S5["Stage 5: K greater than 1 (Fully Developed Chaos)"]
    S6["Stage 6: Strange Attractor on Circle"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
```

### 4.3 Parameter Space (Omega, K) — Arnold Tongue Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ParameterSpace [Parameter Plane Omega vs K]
        AXISX["Omega axis from 0 to 1"]
        AXISY["K axis from 0 to infinity"]
        T1["Tongue 1/1 — widest"]
        T2["Tongue 1/2"]
        T3["Tongue 2/3"]
        T4["Tongue 1/3"]
        T5["Tongue 3/4 — narrower tongues deep inside"]
        CH["Chaotic Region above K = 1 outside tongues"]
        QP["Quasiperiodic Sliver between tongues"]
    end
    T1 --- CH
    T2 --- QP
    T3 --- QP
    T4 --- QP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: At $K = 0$, only isolated points $\Omega = p/q$ are mode-locked. As $K$ grows, every rational $\Omega$ sprouts a wedge-shaped **Arnold tongue** that widens with $K$. Between tongues (the *quasiperiodic slivers*), the rotation number is irrational and orbits densely fill a circle on the 2-torus. Above $K = 1$, the tongues merge with a chaotic sea surrounding them.

### 4.4 Information Flow — Computation of Rotation Number

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT["Input: x0, Omega, K, N"] --> ITER["Iterate F n times: x_n = F^n of x0"]
    ITER --> DIFF["Compute Delta = x_n - x0"]
    DIFF --> DIVIDE["Divide by n: rho_n = Delta / n"]
    DIVIDE --> LIMIT["Take limit as n approaches infinity"]
    LIMIT --> TEST["Is rho_n rational p/q?"]
    TEST -- Yes --> LOCK["Mode Locked Period q"]
    TEST -- No --> QPER["Quasiperiodic Dense Orbit"]
```

### 4.5 Lyapunov Exponent Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Pick Omega, K"] --> Q{"K less than 1?"}
    Q -- Yes --> R["f monotone increasing"]
    R --> S["Lambda less than 0"]
    S --> T["Stable: periodic or quasiperiodic"]
    Q -- No --> U{"Omega inside Arnold tongue?"}
    U -- Yes --> V["Periodic orbit, Lambda may be negative"]
    V --> W{"K beyond period doubling accumulation?"}
    W -- Yes --> X["Lambda greater than 0, chaotic"]
    W -- No --> V
    U -- No --> Y["Outside tongues, K greater than 1"]
    Y --> Z["Chaotic Sea, Lambda greater than 0"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: Define the standard circle map and identify its two key parameters. State the critical value of the nonlinearity parameter at which monotonicity is lost.**

**Model Answer:**

The standard circle map is given by:

$$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod 1$$

The two key parameters are:
1. $\Omega \in [0,1]$ — the **bare (unperturbed) rotation number**, representing the frequency ratio of the two competing oscillations.
2. $K \geq 0$ — the **nonlinearity/coupling strength**, controlling the strength of the nonlinear interaction term.

**Critical value:** Monotonicity of $f_K(\theta)$ requires $f_K'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos(2\pi\theta) > 0$ for all $\theta$. The minimum of this derivative occurs at $\theta = 0$ where $\cos(0) = 1$, giving $f_K'(0) = 1 - K$. Monotonicity is preserved iff $K \leq 1$. Hence **the critical value is $K = 1$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: Students frequently write "$K_c = 1$ because the derivative becomes zero" without justifying *which point* the derivative is evaluated at. Full marks require stating $\theta = 0$ and $\cos(2\pi\theta) = 1$ explicitly.

---

#### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — December 2023]
**Q: What is the rotation number of a circle map? How does it distinguish mode-locking from quasiperiodicity?**

**Model Answer:**

The **rotation number** $\rho$ of a circle map $f: S^1 \to S^1$ is defined using its lift $F: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ (with $F(x+1) = F(x) + 1$) as:

$$\rho = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{F^n(x) - x}{n}$$

This limit exists and is independent of the starting point $x$ for every circle homeomorphism.

**Distinguishing mode-locking from quasiperiodicity:**
- If $\rho = p/q$ is **rational**, then every orbit is asymptotically periodic with period $q$ — this is **mode-locking**.
- If $\rho$ is **irrational**, the orbit never closes and densely fills the circle — this is **quasiperiodicity**.

In the $(\Omega, K)$ parameter plane, the mode-locked regions form **Arnold tongues** around rational values of $\Omega$, while irrational-$\rho$ regions form the quasiperiodic slivers between them.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the rotation number of the unperturbed circle map ($\Omega$ arbitrary, $K = 0$) and the perturbed circle map for $K$ small. Discuss what happens at $\Omega = p/q$ rational versus irrational $\Omega$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled sketch in the $(\Omega, K)$ parameter plane, explain the structure of **Arnold tongues**. State the Farey-tree ordering and the threshold $K = 1$ for the onset of chaos.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A (a)**

**Step 1 — Lift of the map:**
For the circle map $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod 1$, the natural lift is:

$$F(x) = x + \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi x)$$

It satisfies $F(x+1) = F(x) + 1$, confirming it is a valid lift.

**Step 2 — Unperturbed case $K = 0$:**
$F(x) = x + \Omega$, hence $F^n(x) = x + n\Omega$. Therefore:

$$\rho = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{(x + n\Omega) - x}{n} = \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Perturbed case $0 < K < 1$:**
For a $C^1$ diffeomorphism of the circle with $\vert F' \vert$ bounded away from zero, Denjoy's theorem guarantees that $\rho$ is well-defined and continuous. For small $K$:

$$\rho(K, \Omega) = \Omega + O(K)$$

So for small $K$, an irrational $\Omega$ remains irrational, and a rational $\Omega = p/q$ remains close to $p/q$.

**Step 4 — Rational $\Omega = p/q$:**
By structural persistence (the implicit function theorem), for each coprime $p, q$ there exists a region $T_{p/q} \subset (\Omega, K)$-space containing $(\Omega = p/q, K = 0)$ inside which $\rho \equiv p/q$ identically. The orbit is periodic with period $q$. The map is conjugate to a pure rotation by $p/q$ steps:

$$h \circ f^q = R_p \circ h, \quad R_p(\theta) = \theta + p$$

where $h$ is a topological conjugacy homeomorphism.

**Step 5 — Irrational $\Omega$:**
For irrational $\Omega$ and $K < 1$, the orbit is **quasiperiodic** — it densely fills a smooth closed invariant curve $\gamma \subset S^1$. This curve is a **deformed circle**, and the dynamics on $\gamma$ is conjugate to a pure irrational rotation. The two-frequency motion corresponds to motion on a 2-torus $T^2$ in the original (un-modulo-ed) phase space.

**Conclusion:**
The unperturbed rotation number is exactly $\Omega$. Perturbations preserve the qualitative nature of the motion for small $K$: rational $\Omega$ stays periodic (with shifted parameter values), irrational $\Omega$ stays quasiperiodic.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A (b)**

**Arnold Tongue Structure:**
The Arnold tongues $T_{p/q}$ are the regions in the $(\Omega, K)$-plane where $\rho(K,\Omega) = p/q$ for coprime integers $p, q$.

```
Parameter Plane (Omega, K):
                K
                ^
                |         /T_1/1\          /T_1/2\
        K = 1 --|-------/--------\------/--------\----- chaos threshold
                |      /          \    /          \
                |     /   T_2/3    \  /  T_1/3     \
                |    /              \/              \
                |   /     T_3/4     /\               \
                |  /              /  \               \
                | /              /    \               \
                |______________________________> Omega
                0    1/3   1/2   2/3   1
```

**Key features (label for sketch):**
1. Each tongue $T_{p/q}$ opens from the point $(\Omega = p/q,\; K = 0)$.
2. Tongue width grows monotonically with $K$.
3. Tongue width is largest for $p/q = 1/2$ at any given $K$.
4. Between tongues (the **quasiperiodic slivers**), $\rho$ is irrational.
5. Above $K = 1$, tongues begin to **overlap**, and the dynamics becomes chaotic in the overlap regions (the **Arnold cat's eye** structure).

**Farey-Tree Ordering:**
The set of rationals $p/q \in [0,1]$ in lowest terms is partially ordered by the **Farey sum** (mediant):
$$\frac{p}{q} \oplus \frac{p'}{q'} = \frac{p+p'}{q+q'}$$
The rationals with smallest denominators ($1/1$, $1/2$, $2/3$, $1/3$, $3/4$, $2/5$, $3/5$, $\ldots$) appear **first** (at smallest $K$). Between any two tongues at level $K_n$, a new tongue with denominator $q + q'$ appears at a deeper level $K_{n+1}$.

**Onset of Chaos ($K = 1$):**
The critical value $K = 1$ marks where tongues begin to overlap densely. Within each tongue, beyond a critical $K$, a Feigenbaum period-doubling cascade occurs. The accumulated effect over all tongues produces **chaos with positive Lyapunov exponent**:

$$\lambda = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \ln|1 - K\cos(2\pi\theta_i)| > 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: A common error is to claim that "all tongues reach $K=1$" — in fact, each tongue has its *own* period-doubling accumulation point $K_\infty(p/q)$, and these are spread over a range of $K > 1$. The *global* chaos onset occurs in the *overlap regions* between tongues, not inside any single tongue.

---

#### **Question B-B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — December 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the Lyapunov exponent for the standard circle map and compute its value at the critical nonlinearity $K = 1$ for an irrational $\Omega$. Show that for $K$ slightly greater than $1$, $\lambda > 0$, indicating chaos.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the period-doubling cascade inside a single Arnold tongue. State the Feigenbaum constant and explain its universality.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B (a)**

**Definition:**
For the standard circle map $f_K(\theta)$, the Lyapunov exponent is:

$$\lambda(K, \Omega) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \ln \left| f_K'(\theta_i) \right| = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \ln \left| 1 - K\cos(2\pi \theta_i) \right|$$

**Step 1 — Compute derivative:**
$$f_K'(\theta) = \frac{d}{d\theta}\left[\theta + \Omega - \frac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta)\right] = 1 - K\cos(2\pi\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Sign of derivative:**
- $K < 1$: $\vert 1 - K\cos(2\pi\theta)\vert \in [1-K,\; 1+K] > 0$, so $\ln|\cdot|$ is bounded and negative on average $\Rightarrow$ $\lambda < 0$.
- $K = 1$: At $\theta = 0$, $f_K'(0) = 0$, so $\ln(0) = -\infty$. The orbit spends a finite fraction of time near $\theta = 0$ (for irrational $\Omega$), making $\lambda = 0$.
- $K > 1$: $|f_K'(0)| = 1 - K < 0$, and $\ln|1-K| = \ln(K-1)$ becomes large and positive. The orbit samples the negative-slope region, and $\lambda > 0$.

**Step 3 — Quantitative estimate for $K = 1 + \epsilon$ with irrational $\Omega$:**
The orbit spends time $dt \propto d\theta / \dot\theta$ near each angle. Near $\theta = 0$, $\dot\theta = f_K(0) - 0 = \Omega$, constant. The dominant contribution to $\lambda$ comes from the small interval $\theta \in [-\delta, \delta]$ where $\cos(2\pi\theta) \approx 1 - 2\pi^2\theta^2$. The local derivative:

$$f_K'(\theta) \approx 1 - K + 2\pi^2 K \theta^2 \approx -\epsilon + 2\pi^2\theta^2$$

This is negative for $\theta^2 < \epsilon/(2\pi^2)$. The logarithm:

$$\ln|f_K'(\theta)| \approx \ln(2\pi^2 \theta^2 - \epsilon) \approx \ln(2\pi^2) + 2\ln|\theta| \quad \text{for } \theta^2 > \epsilon/(2\pi^2)$$

Integrating over the orbit, one obtains:

$$\lambda \sim \frac{1}{2}\ln K - C$$

where $C$ is a positive constant depending on the orbit. For $K$ slightly greater than $1$, this yields $\lambda > 0$ — **chaos**.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B (b)**

**Period-Doubling Cascade Inside an Arnold Tongue:**

Consider a fixed tongue $T_{p/q}$ and increase $K$ along a vertical line $\Omega = \Omega^* \in T_{p/q}$. The sequence of bifurcations is:

1. **Period-$q$ orbit** stable for small $K > 0$.
2. **First period-doubling** at $K = K_1(q)$: a stable period-$2q$ orbit appears via **flip (period-doubling) bifurcation**.
3. **Second period-doubling** at $K = K_2(q)$: stable period-$4q$ orbit.
4. **$n$-th doubling** at $K = K_n(q)$: stable period-$2^n q$ orbit.
5. **Accumulation** at $K = K_\infty(q)$: period diverges to infinity.
6. **Chaotic regime** for $K > K_\infty(q)$.

**Feigenbaum Constant:**
The successive bifurcation values $K_1 < K_2 < K_3 < \cdots$ converge geometrically:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{K_{n} - K_{n-1}}{K_{n+1} - K_{n}} = \delta = 4.6692016091\ldots$$

This is the **Feigenbaum constant** — first discovered by Mitchell Feigenbaum in 1975–1978.

**Universality:**
The value $\delta \approx 4.6692$ is **universal** in the sense that it depends only on the *qualitative* shape of the map near its extremum (specifically, having a quadratic maximum). It is the same for:
- The logistic map $x_{n+1} = rx_n(1-x_n)$.
- The sine map $x_{n+1} = r\sin(\pi x_n)$.
- The standard circle map $f_K(\theta)$.
- Any one-dimensional map with a single quadratic extremum.

The universality extends beyond $\delta$ to the **Feigenbaum scaling function** $\sigma(x)$, which governs the *shape* of the attractor at the accumulation point — also universal across all such maps.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: Students often confuse **Feigenbaum universality** (which is about the *value* of $\delta$) with **structural universality** (the same bifurcation *sequence* appears). Be precise: $\delta$ is a *number*; its appearance across different maps is the universality claim.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Rapid Revision Checklist: Bifurcations and Chaos in Circle Maps**

- **Standard circle map form**: $\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \Omega - \dfrac{K}{2\pi}\sin(2\pi\theta_n) \pmod 1$. State it cold, no hesitation.
- **Two parameters**: $\Omega$ (frequency ratio, $[0,1]$) and $K$ (nonlinearity, $K \geq 0$).
- **Critical nonlinearity**: $K_c = 1$ — derivative $f_K'(\theta) = 1 - K\cos(2\pi\theta)$ first hits zero at $\theta = 0$.
- **Rotation number definition** (use the lift): $\rho = \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{F^n(x) - x}{n}$.
- **Mode-locking = rational $\rho = p/q$**; quasiperiodicity = irrational $\rho$.
- **Arnold tongues**: regions in $(\Omega, K)$-plane with $\rho = p/q$, opening from $K = 0$, widening with $K$.
- **Farey tree**: mediants $\dfrac{p}{q} \oplus \dfrac{p'}{q'} = \dfrac{p+p'}{q+q'}$ govern tongue ordering.
- **Lyapunov exponent**: $\lambda = \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \ln|1 - K\cos(2\pi\theta_i)|$.
- **Sign of $\lambda$**: negative (stable) for $K < 1$, zero at $K = 1$, positive (chaotic) for $K > 1$.
- **Period-doubling cascade** inside each tongue: $q \to 2q \to 4q \to \cdots \to$ chaos.
- **Feigenbaum constant**: $\delta = 4.6692016\ldots$, universal for one-humped maps.
- **Saddle-node (tangent) bifurcation** occurs at $K = 1$, $\theta = 0$ — the cubic expansion $f_1(\theta) \approx \Omega + \dfrac{\pi^2 \theta^3}{3}$ confirms this.
- **Engineering applications to memorize**: Josephson junctions, PLLs, cardiac dynamics, mode-locked lasers.
- **Topological fact**: Circle maps are invertible for $K \leq 1$; non-invertible (folding) for $K > 1$ — this is why chaos here is structurally different from logistic-map chaos (which is non-invertible for all $r > 4$).
- **Numerical recipe**: To compute $\rho$ in an exam, iterate $F$ from $x = 0$ for $N = 10^4$ steps and use $\rho \approx F^N(0)/N$.
- **Physical constant**: The ratio $\hbar/2e \approx 3.04 \times 10^{-8}$ V·s appears in the Josephson-junction realization — but **no numerical values need to be memorized** unless specifically asked.
- **KTU Pitfall**: Always state the *modulo 1* condition; omitting it loses a mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two-Dimensional Flows and Phase Plane Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Two-Dimensional Flows and Phase Plane Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Two-Dimensional Flow?

In the mathematical framework of **Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos**, a **two-dimensional flow** (or **planar flow**) is a continuous-time dynamical system whose state is described by exactly two state variables, $x$ and $y$, evolving in continuous time $t$ according to a system of two coupled first-order ordinary differential equations (ODEs):

$$\dot{x} = f(x, y)$$
$$\dot{y} = g(x, y)$$

Here the overdot denotes differentiation with respect to time, $\frac{d}{dt}$, while $f$ and $g$ are (typically nonlinear) scalar functions of the state variables. Geometrically, the pair $(x(t), y(t))$ traces a curve in the **state space** $\mathbb{R}^2$, called the **phase plane**, and the collection of all such trajectories forms the **phase portrait**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A *2D flow* is the continuous-time dynamical system governed by $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{X})$ where $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and $\mathbf{F} : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$. The phase plane is the geometric realization of this state space, where every point represents a unique system state and arrows depict the direction of flow in time.

## 1.2 What is a Fixed Point (Equilibrium)?

A **fixed point** (also called an **equilibrium point** or **critical point**) of the flow is a state $(x^*, y^*)$ at which the velocity vector of the system vanishes identically, i.e., the state ceases to change with time. Mathematically:

$$f(x^*, y^*) = 0, \quad g(x^*, y^*) = 0$$

Equilibria are the *backbone* of phase-plane analysis because the **long-term qualitative behavior** of almost every trajectory is dictated by the type, stability, and topological arrangement of these fixed points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Crosswalk:**
> - Fixed point $\equiv$ Equilibrium point $\equiv$ Critical point $\equiv$ Singular point $\equiv$ Stationary point
> - Phase plane $\equiv$ State space (for $n = 2$) $\equiv$ Phase portrait canvas

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: A Ball on a Hilly Terrain

Imagine a small ball rolling on a 2D hilly landscape under gravity with light friction:

- The **horizontal plane** beneath the ball is the *phase plane*, with $x$ being the east-coordinate and $y$ the north-coordinate of the ball's projection.
- The **velocity** of the ball at each $(x, y)$ is described by a 2D vector field $(f(x, y), g(x, y))$ — these are the slope-driven components of motion.
- **Valley bottoms** correspond to *stable equilibria*: the ball naturally rolls into them and stays.
- **Hilltops** correspond to *unstable equilibria*: any tiny perturbation causes the ball to roll away.
- **Saddle points** correspond to *mountain passes*: the ball is stable in one direction but unstable in the perpendicular direction.

A **phase portrait** is therefore equivalent to drawing the entire streamline map of a hilly landscape projected onto a flat map — a chart of "where the ball wants to go" from every starting point.

## 1.4 Linear vs Nonlinear Two-Dimensional Systems

A **linear 2D system** has the canonical form:

$$\dot{x} = ax + by$$
$$\dot{y} = cx + dy$$

where $a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{R}$ are constants. Such systems are solvable in closed form using eigen-decomposition and form the *linear skeleton* around which the qualitative behavior of nonlinear systems is classified.

A **nonlinear 2D system**, however, contains nonlinear functions of $x$ and/or $y$ on the right-hand side. The brilliant insight of **Hartman–Grobman theorem** is that *near a hyperbolic fixed point*, the qualitative (topological) behavior of a nonlinear system is *conjugate* — i.e., qualitatively identical — to that of its linearization via the Jacobian matrix. This single theorem is what allows us to classify nonlinear systems using the tools of linear algebra.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hartman–Grobman Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** If $(x^*, y^*)$ is a **hyperbolic** fixed point of $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{X})$ (i.e., the Jacobian evaluated at the fixed point has no eigenvalues with zero real part), then there exists a continuous, invertible, near-identity change of coordinates that maps the nonlinear phase portrait locally to that of its linearization $\dot{\boldsymbol{\xi}} = J \cdot \boldsymbol{\xi}$.
> Consequence: We can classify local behavior of *any* smooth nonlinear system by simply studying its linearization!
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Jacobian Matrix and Linearization

For a nonlinear 2D system

$$\dot{x} = f(x, y), \qquad \dot{y} = g(x, y)$$

the **Jacobian matrix** evaluated at a generic point $(x, y)$ is:

$$J(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial y} \end{pmatrix}$$

At a fixed point $(x^*, y^*)$, define the small perturbation vector $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (\xi_1, \xi_2)^T = (x - x^*,\; y - y^*)^T$. Taylor expanding $f$ and $g$ around $(x^*, y^*)$ and discarding higher-order terms yields the **linearized system**:

$$\dot{\boldsymbol{\xi}} = A \, \boldsymbol{\xi}, \qquad A \equiv J(x^*, y^*)$$

The matrix $A$ is constant (since it is evaluated at the fixed point), so the linearized system is fully solvable by eigen-decomposition.

## 2.2 Eigenvalues of the Linearized System

Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$. The eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ are roots of the **characteristic polynomial**:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

Expanding:

$$(a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) - bc = \lambda^2 - (a + d)\lambda + (ad - bc) = 0$$

Define the two scalar invariants:

$$\boxed{\tau = \text{Tr}(A) = a + d}$$
$$\boxed{\Delta = \det(A) = ad - bc}$$

Then the characteristic equation takes the canonical **trace-determinant form**:

$$\lambda^2 - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0$$

The discriminant of this quadratic governs the nature of the eigenvalues:

$$D = \tau^2 - 4\Delta$$

## 2.3 The Five Canonical Fixed-Point Types in 2D

The eigenvalues $(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$ fall into qualitatively distinct categories that define the *type* of fixed point:

### Case 1: Real, Distinct, Same-Sign Eigenvalues ($\Delta > 0$, $\tau^2 > 4\Delta$)
- If $\tau < 0$: **Stable Node** (a.k.a. sink) — both eigenvalues negative, trajectories flow *inward*.
- If $\tau > 0$: **Unstable Node** (a.k.a. source) — both eigenvalues positive, trajectories flow *outward*.

### Case 2: Real Eigenvalues of Opposite Signs ($\Delta < 0$)
- **Saddle Point** — one stable, one unstable direction; trajectories approach along the stable manifold and depart along the unstable manifold.

### Case 3: Complex Conjugate Eigenwith Negative Real Part ($\Delta > 0$, $\tau < 0$, $\tau^2 < 4\Delta$)
- **Stable Focus / Stable Spiral** — trajectories spiral *inward*.

### Case 4: Complex Conjugate Eigenvalues with Positive Real Part ($\Delta > 0$, $\tau > 0$, $\tau^2 < 4\Delta$)
- **Unstable Focus / Unstable Spiral** — trajectories spiral *outward*.

### Case 5: Purely Imaginary Eigenvalues ($\Delta > 0$, $\tau = 0$)
- **Center** — closed orbits around the fixed point; *neutrally stable* (Lyapunov stable but not asymptotically stable).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All high-yield formulas for Two-Dimensional Flows and Phase Plane Analysis are consolidated below. Memorize this table — it covers approximately 70% of Part B questions on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Linear 2D system | $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A \mathbf{X}$, $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^2$ | Generic linearized flow |
| 2 | Equilibrium condition | $f(x^*, y^*) = 0,\; g(x^*, y^*) = 0$ | Defines fixed points |
| 3 | Jacobian | $J = \begin{pmatrix} f_x & f_y \\ g_x & g_y \end{pmatrix}$ | Linearization operator |
| 4 | Trace of $A$ | $\tau = a + d$ | Sum of eigenvalues |
| 5 | Determinant of $A$ | $\Delta = ad - bc$ | Product of eigenvalues |
| 6 | Characteristic eqn. | $\lambda^2 - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0$ | Eigenvalue determination |
| 7 | Roots (eigenvalues) | $\lambda = \dfrac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}$ | Closed-form solution |
| 8 | Discriminant | $D = \tau^2 - 4\Delta$ | Controls eigenvalue nature |
| 9 | Stable Node | $\Delta > 0,\; \tau < 0,\; D > 0$ | Exponential decay |
| 10 | Unstable Node | $\Delta > 0,\; \tau > 0,\; D > 0$ | Exponential growth |
| 11 | Saddle Point | $\Delta < 0$ | Mixed stability |
| 12 | Stable Spiral | $\Delta > 0,\; \tau < 0,\; D < 0$ | Damped oscillation |
| 13 | Unstable Spiral | $\Delta > 0,\; \tau > 0,\; D < 0$ | Growing oscillation |
| 14 | Center | $\Delta > 0,\; \tau = 0$ | Conservative oscillation |
| 15 | Stable node (repeated) | $\Delta > 0,\; \tau < 0,\; D = 0$ | Star / improper node |
| 16 | Hyperbolic fixed point | $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) \neq 0\;\forall i$ | Hartman–Grobman applies |
| 17 | Non-hyperbolic | $\exists i: \text{Re}(\lambda_i) = 0$ | Higher-order analysis needed |
| 18 | Solution (real distinct) | $\mathbf{X}(t) = c_1 \mathbf{v}_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + c_2 \mathbf{v}_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}$ | General real flow |
| 19 | Solution (complex) | $\mathbf{X}(t) = e^{\alpha t}\!\left[ c_1 \mathbf{R}\cos\beta t - c_2 \mathbf{I}\sin\beta t \right]$ | Spiral flow, $\lambda = \alpha \pm i\beta$ |
| 20 | Lyapunov function (saddle) | $V(x,y) = $ signed quadratic form | Proves instability |
| 21 | Poincaré index | $\oint \frac{f\,dg - g\,df}{f^2 + g^2}$ | Topological invariant |
| 22 | Index of node / spiral / center | $+1$ | Index rule |
| 23 | Index of saddle | $-1$ | Index rule |
| 24 | Index sum rule | $\sum_i \text{Ind}(P_i) = \chi$ (Euler char.) | Global constraint |
| 25 | Energy of Hamil. sys. | $E(x,y) = \tfrac{1}{2}(x^2 + y^2) + U(x)$ | Constants of motion |

> **Note:** In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ is written using the typography $\vert \cdot \vert$ (or simply omitted) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Use the vertical bar visually without rendering it as a column delimiter.

## 2.5 Physical / Engineering Utility

Two-dimensional phase-plane analysis is the workhorse of qualitative dynamics and finds applications across:

- **Electrical Engineering:** Van der Pol oscillator (vacuum-tube / tunnel-diode circuits), Chua's circuit, PLL (phase-locked loop) capture and lock behavior.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Pendulum dynamics, robotic arm control, vehicle suspension design, aeroelastic flutter (Hopf bifurcation).
- **Control Theory:** Second-order linear systems, root locus, Nyquist plots, sliding-mode control.
- **Power Systems:** Transient stability of a two-machine swing equation (equal-area criterion).
- **Population Biology:** Lotka–Volterra predator–prey models.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Autocatalytic reactions modeled by Brusselator equations.

The beauty of the phase plane is that it converts the *time evolution* of a system into a *spatial geometry*, allowing engineers to *see* stability, oscillation, and chaos without solving the ODEs analytically.

## 2.6 Existence and Uniqueness — Lipschitz Condition

> [!NOTE]
> **Prerequisite for Phase-Plane Analysis:** The Picard–Lindelöf theorem guarantees that if $f$ and $g$ are continuously differentiable (Lipschitz continuous) on a region, then through every initial condition there passes *exactly one* trajectory. This guarantees that phase portraits are *well-defined* — trajectories never cross each other (except possibly at fixed points).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Eigenvalues of a 2×2 Linear System

### Setup

Consider a linear 2D autonomous system:

$$\dot{\mathbf{X}} = A \mathbf{X}, \qquad \mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}, \quad A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 1: Ansatz — Exponential Trial Solution

We try a solution of the form $\mathbf{X}(t) = \mathbf{v} e^{\lambda t}$, where $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^2$ is a constant nonzero vector and $\lambda \in \mathbb{C}$. Substituting:

$$\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \lambda \mathbf{v} e^{\lambda t} = A \mathbf{v} e^{\lambda t}$$

Canceling the common factor $e^{\lambda t} \neq 0$:

$$A \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad (A - \lambda I) \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$$

### Step 2: Nontriviality Condition

For a nonzero $\mathbf{v}$ to exist, the matrix $(A - \lambda I)$ must be singular, i.e.:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Determinant Explicitly

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det\!\begin{pmatrix} a - \lambda & b \\ c & d - \lambda \end{pmatrix} &= (a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) - bc \\
&= ad - a\lambda - d\lambda + \lambda^2 - bc \\
&= \lambda^2 - (a + d)\lambda + (ad - bc)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Identify Trace and Determinant

Define $\tau = a + d$ and $\Delta = ad - bc$. Then:

$$\lambda^2 - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0$$

### Step 5: Solve Using the Quadratic Formula

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}$$

### Step 6: Product and Sum Relations

From Vieta's formulas:

$$\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = \tau, \qquad \lambda_1 \lambda_2 = \Delta$$

> **Conclusion:** The complete local qualitative behavior of any linear 2D system is determined by just **two scalar invariants**: $\tau$ and $\Delta$. This is the magic that makes classification so elegant.

## 3.2 General Solution of the Linear System — Three Cases

### Case A: Real, Distinct Eigenvalues ($\tau^2 > 4\Delta$)

Let $\lambda_1 \neq \lambda_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ with eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2$. The general solution is:

$$\mathbf{X}(t) = c_1 \, \mathbf{v}_1 \, e^{\lambda_1 t} + c_2 \, \mathbf{v}_2 \, e^{\lambda_2 t}$$

As $t \to \infty$, the term with the **larger** $\vert \lambda \vert$ dominates. Both real and negative gives a stable node; both real and positive gives an unstable node; opposite signs gives a saddle.

### Case B: Complex Conjugate Eigenvalues ($\tau^2 < 4\Delta$)

Write $\lambda = \alpha \pm i\beta$ where $\alpha = \tau/2$ and $\beta = \sqrt{4\Delta - \tau^2}\,/\,2$. Let the corresponding eigenvector be $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{u} + i \mathbf{w}$. Taking real combinations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}(t) &= e^{\alpha t}\!\left[\, c_1\!\left(\mathbf{u}\cos\beta t - \mathbf{w}\sin\beta t\right) + c_2\!\left(\mathbf{u}\sin\beta t + \mathbf{w}\cos\beta t\right) \right] \\
&= e^{\alpha t} \, R(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $e^{\alpha t}$ is the **radial envelope**, while $R(t)$ performs bounded rotation at frequency $\beta$.

- $\alpha < 0$: spiraling **inward** (stable focus).
- $\alpha > 0$: spiraling **outward** (unstable focus).
- $\alpha = 0$: bounded closed orbits (center).

### Case C: Repeated Eigenvalues ($\tau^2 = 4\Delta$)

When $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = \tau/2$, the Jordan form controls behavior:

- **Two linearly independent eigenvectors** → **Star node** (or **diagonal node**): all trajectories radial.
- **One eigenvector** → **Degenerate / improper node**: trajectories tangent to the single eigenvector direction.

## 3.3 Stability Criteria — Lyapunov's First Method (Linearization Test)

For a nonlinear system $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{X})$ with equilibrium $\mathbf{X}^*$:

- $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for all $i$ → **(Asymptotically) Stable** in the sense of Lyapunov.
- $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) > 0$ for at least one $i$ → **Unstable**.
- All $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) \leq 0$ and at least one equals zero → **Marginally Stable / Indeterminate** (non-hyperbolic case — requires higher-order / Lyapunov analysis).

Equivalent algebraic conditions for asymptotic stability of a 2D linear system (**Routh–Hurwitz criterion** for $n = 2$):

$$\Delta > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \tau < 0$$

## 3.4 Example Worked Derivation: Simple Pendulum Linearization

The undamped simple pendulum is governed by:

$$\ddot{\theta} + \omega_0^2 \sin\theta = 0$$

Convert to first-order system with $x = \theta$, $y = \dot{\theta}$:

$$\dot{x} = y, \qquad \dot{y} = -\omega_0^2 \sin x$$

Fixed points satisfy $y = 0$ and $\sin x = 0$, so $(x^*, y^*) = (n\pi, 0)$ for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

Jacobian:

$$
J = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\omega_0^2 \cos x & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

At $x^* = 0$ (pendulum hanging down):

$$J(0,0) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\omega_0^2 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Trace $\tau = 0$, Determinant $\Delta = \omega_0^2 > 0$, Discriminant $\tau^2 - 4\Delta = -4\omega_0^2 < 0$.

By Case 4 (Section 2.3, modified — center case when $\tau = 0$), this is a **Center** — bounded oscillations around the downward position.

At $x^* = \pi$ (pendulum pointing up — inverted):

$$J(\pi, 0) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ +\omega_0^2 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Trace $\tau = 0$, Determinant $\Delta = -\omega_0^2 < 0$, so by Case 2 — **Saddle Point**. The unstable manifold corresponds to the unstable inverted equilibrium.

> This single example illustrates the entire classification methodology applied to a physically meaningful system.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trace-Determinant Classification Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the *decision logic* used to classify any 2D linear (or linearized) fixed point using only its trace $\tau$ and determinant $\Delta$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Evaluate Jacobian A at fixed point]) --> ComputeTau[Compute tau = trace of A]
    ComputeTau --> ComputeDet[Compute Delta = det of A]
    ComputeDet --> ComputeDisc[Compute D = tau squared minus 4 Delta]
    ComputeDisc --> DeltaSign{Is Delta negative?}
    
    DeltaSign -- Yes --> Saddle["Saddle Point\n(both real eigenvalues, opposite signs)"]
    
    DeltaSign -- No --> ZeroTau{Is tau equal to 0?}
    ZeroTau -- Yes --> CenterTest{Is Delta greater than 0?}
    CenterTest -- Yes --> CenterNode["Center\n(purely imaginary eigenvalues)"]
    
    ZeroTau -- No --> DiscSign{Is D greater than 0?}
    DiscSign -- Yes --> NodeTest{Is tau less than 0?}
    NodeTest -- Yes --> StableNode["Stable Node\n(both eigenvalues negative real)"]
    NodeTest -- No --> UnstableNode["Unstable Node\n(both eigenvalues positive real)"]
    
    DiscSign -- No --> SpiralTest{Is tau less than 0?}
    SpiralTest -- Yes --> StableSpiral["Stable Spiral / Focus\n(complex eigenvalues, Re less than 0)"]
    SpiralTest -- No --> UnstableSpiral["Unstable Spiral / Focus\n(complex eigenvalues, Re greater than 0)"]
    
    CenterTest -- No --> CenterDegenerate["Degenerate case\nDelta = 0, see advanced text"]
```

## 4.2 Trace-Determinant Plane Map (Conceptual)

The following Mermaid block renders a *block-level* version of the classic $(\tau, \Delta)$ plane, partitioning it into the canonical stability regions. This is the *single most important* picture in 2D linear systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Plane["TRACE-DETERMINANT PLANE: Horizontal Axis = tau, Vertical Axis = Delta"]
        direction TB
        
        subgraph Region1["REGION I - Saddle: Delta less than 0"]
            R1[Imaginary eigenvalue pair is impossible.\n One positive real, one negative real eigenvalue.\n Always unstable. Hyperbolic.]
        end
        
        subgraph Region2["REGION II - Stable Node or Spiral: tau less than 0, Delta greater than 0, tau squared minus 4 Delta may be positive or negative"]
            R2[If D greater than 0: stable node.\n If D less than 0: stable spiral.\n If D equals 0: stable star or degenerate node.\n Always asymptotically stable.]
        end
        
        subgraph Region3["REGION III - Center: tau equals 0, Delta greater than 0"]
            R3[Purely imaginary eigenvalues.\n Closed orbits. Neutrally stable, not asymptotically stable.\n Non-hyperbolic.]
        end
        
        subgraph Region4["REGION IV - Unstable Node or Spiral: tau greater than 0, Delta greater than 0, tau squared minus 4 Delta may be positive or negative"]
            R4[If D greater than 0: unstable node.\n If D less than 0: unstable spiral.\n Always unstable. Hyperbolic.]
        end
        
        subgraph Axis["AXIS BOUNDARY"]
            A0[Delta equals 0: at least one zero eigenvalue.\nNon-hyperbolic. Higher-order analysis required.]
        end
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Phase Plane Analysis Pipeline

The following block diagram represents the *end-to-end workflow* a KTU student should follow when solving any phase-plane analysis problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: Identify the system\nRead the two coupled first-order ODEs] --> B[STEP 2: Locate fixed points\nSolve f equals 0 and g equals 0 simultaneously]
    B --> C[STEP 3: Compute Jacobian J\nForm the 2 by 2 matrix of partial derivatives]
    C --> D[STEP 4: Evaluate J at each fixed point\nSubstitute coordinates into J]
    D --> E[STEP 5: Compute trace tau and determinant Delta\nApply trace-determinant classification]
    E --> F{Is the fixed point hyperbolic?\nBoth eigenvalues have nonzero real part}
    F -- Yes --> G[STEP 6a: Use Hartman-Grobman\nClassify using linearized eigenvalues]
    F -- No --> H[STEP 6b: Center manifold or Lyapunov\nRequires nonlinear or higher-order analysis]
    G --> I[STEP 7: Sketch phase portrait\nPlot eigenvectors, nullclines, sample trajectories]
    H --> I
    I --> J[STEP 8: Interpret physical meaning\nState stability, oscillation, response type]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e8f5e9
    style D fill:#fce4ec
    style E fill:#f3e5f5
    style F fill:#fff9c4
    style I fill:#e0f7fa
    style J fill:#ffe0b2
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Phase Portrait Components

A phase portrait consists of four families of geometric objects. The Mermaid block below shows their interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PP[Phase Portrait Construction] --> FP[Fixed Points\nEquilibria marked with dots]
    PP --> NC[Nullclines\nCurves where either f equals 0 or g equals 0]
    PP --> TR[Trajectories\nIntegral curves of the vector field]
    PP --> ST[Stable and Unstable Manifolds\nInvariant curves through saddle points]
    
    FP --> R1[Classification via eigenvalues:\nNode, Spiral, Saddle, Center]
    NC --> R2[Intersection of f equals 0 and g equals 0\nLocates fixed points]
    TR --> R3[Direction indicated by sign of f and g\nin each quadrant]
    ST --> R4[Stable: trajectories enter the saddle\nUnstable: trajectories leave the saddle]
    
    R1 --> Final[Combined diagram with all four components]
    R2 --> Final
    R3 --> Final
    R4 --> Final
```

## 4.5 Schematic: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 2D Flow System

For systems that are difficult to draw physically, the following block diagram maps the *functional* architecture of a generic 2D flow showing feedback coupling.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[(State Variable x)] -->|feeds into| F[f of x and y\nRight-hand side function]
    Y[(State Variable y)] -->|feeds into| F
    F -->|outputs| DX[Time derivative of x\nEquals x-dot]
    DX -->|integrates| INT1[Integrator Block]
    INT1 -->|updates| X
    
    X -->|feeds into| G[g of x and y\nRight-hand side function]
    Y -->|feeds into| G
    G -->|outputs| DY[Time derivative of y\nEquals y-dot]
    DY -->|integrates| INT2[Integrator Block]
    INT2 -->|updates| Y
    
    style X fill:#bbdefb
    style Y fill:#c8e6c9
    style F fill:#fff9c4
    style G fill:#f8bbd0
    style INT1 fill:#e1bee7
    style INT2 fill:#e1bee7
```

> **Reading Note:** The two integrators with mutual coupling between $f$ and $g$ represent the *coupled* nature of a 2D flow. In a *linear* system, $f$ and $g$ are *affine* functions; in a *nonlinear* system, they may contain products, powers, or trigonometric terms of $x$ and $y$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

Define a *fixed point* of a two-dimensional dynamical system. How are fixed points classified using the trace and determinant of the Jacobian matrix?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A fixed point $(x^*, y^*)$ of a 2D system $\dot{x} = f(x,y),\; \dot{y} = g(x,y)$ is a state where the velocity vanishes: $f(x^*, y^*) = 0$ and $g(x^*, y^*) = 0$.

Classification is done via the **trace** $\tau = a + d$ and **determinant** $\Delta = ad - bc$ of the Jacobian matrix $A = J(x^*, y^*)$:

- $\Delta < 0$: **Saddle point** (unstable).
- $\Delta > 0,\; \tau < 0$: **Stable node** (if $\tau^2 > 4\Delta$) or **stable spiral** (if $\tau^2 < 4\Delta$).
- $\Delta > 0,\; \tau > 0$: **Unstable node** (if $\tau^2 > 4\Delta$) or **unstable spiral** (if $\tau^2 < 4\Delta$).
- $\Delta > 0,\; \tau = 0$: **Center**.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the classification table.)*

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

State the **Hartman–Grobman theorem** and explain why it is significant for the study of nonlinear systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Hartman–Grobman theorem** states that near a **hyperbolic** fixed point (one where the linearization has no eigenvalues with zero real part), the nonlinear dynamical system is **topologically conjugate** to its linearization. That is, there exists a continuous, invertible, near-identity coordinate change mapping the nonlinear phase portrait locally onto the linearized one.

**Significance:**
1. It justifies the use of *linear* (eigenvalue-based) classification for *nonlinear* systems near hyperbolic equilibria.
2. It means we do not need to solve the nonlinear ODE explicitly — local qualitative behavior is fully captured by the Jacobian.
3. It explains why *local* bifurcations (e.g., saddle-node, Hopf) can be detected solely from changes in eigenvalues.

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for theorem statement, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, Choice Option A]**

**(a)** Consider the nonlinear system:

$$\dot{x} = x - y - x(x^2 + y^2)$$
$$\dot{y} = x + y - y(x^2 + y^2)$$

Find all the fixed points and classify each one in the $(\tau, \Delta)$ plane. Justify your classification with Hartman–Grobman. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert to polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ via $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$ and show that the system has a stable circular limit cycle of radius $r = 1$. Sketch the phase portrait. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Locate fixed points (1 mark):**

Set $\dot{x} = 0$ and $\dot{y} = 0$:

$$x - y - x(x^2 + y^2) = 0 \quad (1)$$
$$x + y - y(x^2 + y^2) = 0 \quad (2)$$

From $(1)$: $x(1 - r^2) = y$, where $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$.
From $(2)$: $y(1 - r^2) = -x$.

Add $(1)$ and $(2)$: $(x + y)(1 - r^2) = 0$, so either $y = -x$ or $r = 1$.

**Sub-case A: $r = 1$.** Then $(1 - r^2) = 0$ and the equations become $0 = y$ and $0 = -x$. So $x = 0$, $y = 0$, but $r^2 = 0 \neq 1$. Contradiction. Hence this sub-case yields no fixed points.

**Sub-case B: $y = -x$.** Substituting into $r^2 = x^2 + y^2 = 2x^2$. Equation $(1)$ becomes:

$$x(1 - 2x^2) - (-x) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x - 2x^3 + x = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2x(1 - x^2) = 0$$

So $x = 0,\; x = +1,\; x = -1$, giving fixed points:

$$P_1 = (0, 0), \quad P_2 = (1, -1), \quad P_3 = (-1, 1)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian (2 marks):**

$$
J(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - (3x^2 + y^2) & -1 - 2xy \\ 1 - 2xy & 1 - (x^2 + 3y^2) \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate and classify each fixed point (3 marks):**

**At $P_1 = (0, 0)$:**

$$J(P_1) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

$\tau = 1 + 1 = 2$, $\Delta = 1 - (-1) = 2$, $D = 4 - 8 = -4 < 0$.

Since $\Delta > 0$, $\tau > 0$, $D < 0$ → **Unstable Spiral (Focus)**.

**At $P_2 = (1, -1)$:**

$3x^2 + y^2 = 3 + 1 = 4$, $x^2 + 3y^2 = 1 + 3 = 4$, $2xy = -2$.

$$J(P_2) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - 4 & -1 - (-2) \\ 1 - (-2) & 1 - 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 1 \\ 3 & -3 \end{pmatrix}$$

$\tau = -6$, $\Delta = (-3)(-3) - (1)(3) = 9 - 3 = 6$, $D = 36 - 24 = 12 > 0$.

$\Delta > 0$, $\tau < 0$, $D > 0$ → **Stable Node**.

**At $P_3 = (-1, 1)$:** (by symmetry) — Same as $P_2$ → **Stable Node**.

**Step 4 — Hartman–Grobman Justification (1 mark):**

All three fixed points have eigenvalues with $\text{Re}(\lambda) \neq 0$ (hyperbolic). Hence Hartman–Grobman applies and the classifications above faithfully represent the *local* phase portrait of the nonlinear system.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Transform to polar (2 marks):**

With $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$, we have $\dot{x} = \dot{r}\cos\theta - r\dot{\theta}\sin\theta$ and $\dot{y} = \dot{r}\sin\theta + r\dot{\theta}\cos\theta$.

Compute:

$$x^2 + y^2 = r^2, \quad x - y = r(\cos\theta - \sin\theta), \quad x + y = r(\cos\theta + \sin\theta)$$

The system becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{r}\cos\theta - r\dot{\theta}\sin\theta &= r\cos\theta - r\sin\theta - r\cos\theta \cdot r^2 \\
\dot{r}\sin\theta + r\dot{\theta}\cos\theta &= r\cos\theta + r\sin\theta - r\sin\theta \cdot r^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decouple (2 marks):**

Multiply the first equation by $\cos\theta$ and the second by $\sin\theta$, then add:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{r}(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) &= r(\cos^2\theta - \sin\theta\cos\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta + \sin^2\theta) - r^3(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) \\
\dot{r} &= r(\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) - r^3 = r - r^3 = r(1 - r^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\dot\theta$ (1 mark):**

Similarly, $-\sin\theta \cdot (\text{eq.1}) + \cos\theta \cdot (\text{eq.2})$ yields:

$$r\dot{\theta} = (\cos\theta + \sin\theta)\cos\theta - (\cos\theta - \sin\theta)\sin\theta = 1$$

So $\dot{\theta} = 1$ (constant angular velocity).

**Step 4 — Analyze radial dynamics (1 mark):**

$\dot{r} = r(1 - r^2) = 0$ when $r = 0$ or $r = 1$.

- For $0 < r < 1$: $\dot{r} > 0$ → radius grows.
- For $r > 1$: $\dot{r} < 0$ → radius shrinks.

Thus $r = 1$ is **asymptotically stable** — trajectories from inside spiral out, those from outside spiral in, all converging to the circle of radius 1.

**Step 5 — Sketch (1 mark):** Phase portrait: outward spiral from $P_1 = (0,0)$ (unstable focus), with stable nodes $P_2 = (1,-1)$ and $P_3 = (-1, 1)$, all flows ultimately captured by the unit limit cycle. Rotation is counterclockwise ($\dot{\theta} = 1 > 0$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice Option)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, Choice Option B]**

**(a)** For the linear system $\dot{x} = x + 2y$, $\dot{y} = 4x - y$, find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Classify the origin and sketch the phase portrait. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system $\dot{x} = -x + x^3$, $\dot{y} = -2y$, locate all fixed points, linearize at each, and discuss stability. Does any fixed point *fail* the Hartman–Grobman test? Why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write the matrix and find eigenvalues (2 marks):**

$$A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 4 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Characteristic equation:

$$\lambda^2 - \tau \lambda + \Delta = \lambda^2 - 0 \cdot \lambda + (-1 - 8) = \lambda^2 - 9 = 0$$

So $\lambda_1 = +3,\; \lambda_2 = -3$.

**Step 2 — Find eigenvectors (2 marks):**

For $\lambda_1 = 3$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ 4 & -4 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

For $\lambda_2 = -3$:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 4 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — General solution (1 mark):**

$$\mathbf{X}(t) = c_1 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} e^{3t} + c_2 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix} e^{-3t}$$

**Step 4 — Classification (1 mark):**

$\Delta = -9 < 0$, real eigenvalues of opposite signs → **Saddle Point** at the origin.

**Step 5 — Phase portrait sketch (1 mark):** Trajectories approach along the stable manifold (direction $\mathbf{v}_2 = (1,-2)$) and depart along the unstable manifold (direction $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1,1)$), forming the characteristic hyperbolic shape.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Locate fixed points (2 marks):**

$\dot{x} = -x + x^3 = x(x^2 - 1) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0, \pm 1$.
$\dot{y} = -2y = 0 \Rightarrow y = 0$.

Three fixed points: $A = (0, 0)$, $B = (1, 0)$, $C = (-1, 0)$.

**Step 2 — Compute Jacobian (1 mark):**

$$J(x,y) = \begin{pmatrix} -1 + 3x^2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Linearize and classify (3 marks):**

**At $A = (0, 0)$:** $J = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$. Eigenvalues $-1, -2$. Trace $\tau = -3 < 0$, $\Delta = 2 > 0$, $D = 9 - 8 = 1 > 0$ → **Stable Node**. Hyperbolic ✓.

**At $B = (1, 0)$:** $J = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$. Eigenvalues $+2, -2$. $\Delta = -4 < 0$ → **Saddle Point**. Hyperbolic ✓.

**At $C = (-1, 0)$:** By symmetry, same as $B$ → **Saddle Point**. Hyperbolic ✓.

**Step 4 — Hartman–Grobman applicability (1 mark):**

All three fixed points are *hyperbolic* (no zero real-part eigenvalues), so Hartman–Grobman applies at each. The *global* nonlinear phase portrait, however, has an interesting feature: the unstable manifold of the saddles at $(\pm 1, 0)$ connects back to the origin along the $x$-axis, creating a *heteroclinic* orbit structure — visible only by *nonlinear* analysis, not by linearization.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing stability with hyperbolicity.** A fixed point can be unstable *and* hyperbolic (e.g., saddle, unstable node). Always report *both* the type *and* the stability.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to substitute into the Jacobian correctly.** A frequent error is computing $J(x, y)$ correctly but then plugging in the *wrong* fixed point. Double-check coordinates before evaluating partial derivatives.
> 
> 3. **Misclassifying centers as stable spirals.** A center ($\tau = 0$) is *neutrally* stable, not asymptotically stable. Trajectories neither spiral in nor out — they form closed orbits. This is *not* the same as a stable spiral.
> 
> 4. **Applying Hartman–Grobman at non-hyperbolic points.** When $\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$ for some eigenvalue, the theorem *fails*. Higher-order terms in the Taylor expansion control the dynamics. Marks are deducted if the student does not acknowledge this explicitly.
> 
> 5. **Not justifying classification with the trace-determinant table.** Always cite the conditions (e.g., "since $\Delta < 0$, the fixed point is a saddle") — do not just state the answer.
> 
> 6. **Confusing the nullclines $f = 0$ and $g = 0$ with trajectories.** Nullclines divide the phase plane into regions of monotone $x$ and monotone $y$; trajectories cross them transversally except at fixed points.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting the $e^{\alpha t}$ envelope for complex eigenvalues.** In a spiral, the *envelope* $e^{\alpha t}$ governs whether motion is in/out, while $\beta$ governs the rotation rate. Many students describe only the oscillation and omit the radial decay/growth.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Two-Dimensional Flows and Phase Plane Analysis**
> 
> - [x] A **2D flow** is $\dot{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{X})$ with $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^2$.
> - [x] **Fixed points** solve $f = 0,\; g = 0$ simultaneously.
> - [x] **Jacobian** $J = \begin{pmatrix} f_x & f_y \\ g_x & g_y \end{pmatrix}$ provides linearization.
> - [x] **Trace** $\tau = a + d$, **Determinant** $\Delta = ad - bc$.
> - [x] **Characteristic equation**: $\lambda^2 - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$.
> - [x] Eigenvalues: $\lambda = \dfrac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}$.
> - [x] **Five canonical types**: stable node, unstable node, saddle, stable spiral, unstable spiral, center.
> - [x] **Stability test** (Routh–Hurwitz, $n=2$): asymptotically stable iff $\tau < 0$ and $\Delta > 0$.
> - [x] **Hartman–Grobman theorem**: nonlinear system is topologically conjugate to its linearization near any **hyperbolic** fixed point.
> - [x] **Non-hyperbolic** fixed points ($\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$ for some $\lambda$) require **center manifold**, **normal form**, or **Lyapunov** analysis.
> - [x] **Nullclines** ($f = 0$ and $g = 0$) cross only at fixed points.
> - [x] **Phase portrait** is the geometric map of all trajectories.
> - [x] **Poincaré index**: node/spiral/center $= +1$, saddle $= -1$; sum over a closed curve $= 2$ (Bézout).
> - [x] **Polar form** of 2D systems decouples radial and angular dynamics: $\dot{r} = r\alpha(r)$, $\dot{\theta} = \beta(r)$.
> - [x] **Limit cycle** = isolated closed orbit (Part B / Module 4 territory, but foundational here).
> - [x] **Engineering applications**: Van der Pol oscillator, pendulum, Lotka–Volterra, Chua's circuit, two-machine power swing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Systems and Phase Portraits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Systems and Phase Portraits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the study of **Non-Linear Dynamics and Chaos**, a **linear system** is a continuous-time dynamical system whose governing equations are expressible as a system of first-order ordinary differential equations in which each variable and its derivatives appear only to the first power, and there are no products or nonlinear functions of the state variables. The most general homogeneous 2D autonomous linear system is written as:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}
$$

where $\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} x_{1} \\ x_{2} \end{pmatrix}$ is the **state vector** in the phase plane, and $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$ is a real $2 \times 2$ **coefficient matrix** with constant entries.

A **phase portrait** is the geometric representation of the system's dynamics plotted in the **state space (phase space)**, showing all possible trajectories $\mathbf{x}(t)$ as parametric curves in the $(x_{1}, x_{2})$-plane, together with arrows indicating the direction of time evolution. For linear systems, the phase portrait is completely determined by the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the matrix $A$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 3):** Linear systems form the *backbone* of nonlinear analysis because, by the Hartman–Grobman theorem, the qualitative behavior of a nonlinear system near a hyperbolic equilibrium point is **topologically conjugate** (locally equivalent) to that of its linearization.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hilly Terrain Map"

Imagine you are standing on a vast, smooth, hilly landscape and you release a tiny marble at any point. The marble will roll downhill following the local gradient of the terrain.

- The **shape of the terrain** (slopes, valleys, ridges, peaks) is the **phase portrait**.
- The **elevation function** over the landscape is the **vector field** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$.
- **Valleys** are **stable equilibria (attractors)** — marbles roll into them.
- **Peaks** are **unstable equilibria (repellors)** — marbles roll away.
- **Saddle points** are mountain passes — marbles roll in along one direction and away along the perpendicular direction.
- **Plateaus or flat bowls** are **centers** — marbles orbit around them in closed loops.

This terrain is mathematically described by the matrix $A$, and the "type" of every point in the landscape is governed by the **eigenvalues** of $A$ — two special numbers that completely dictate the long-term behavior of the system.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> - **Equilibrium (Fixed) Point:** A state $\mathbf{x}^{*}$ where $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = 0$, i.e., $A\mathbf{x}^{*} = 0$. For $A$ invertible, the unique equilibrium is the **origin** $\mathbf{x}^{*} = (0,0)$.
> - **Stability:** An equilibrium is **asymptotically stable** if all nearby trajectories approach it as $t \to \infty$, **unstable** if they diverge, and **Lyapunov stable (neutral)** if they remain nearby.
> - **Trajectory / Orbit:** The curve traced by $\mathbf{x}(t)$ in phase space as time evolves.
> - **Eigenvalue ($\lambda$):** A scalar such that $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ for some nonzero vector $\mathbf{v}$.
> - **Eigenvector ($\mathbf{v}$):** The nonzero vector associated with $\lambda$, pointing along the direction of pure exponential growth or decay.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General 2D Linear System — Why It Matters

Any planar linear system can be written canonically as:

$$
\dot{x} = ax + by
$$
$$
\dot{y} = cx + dy
$$

This **four-parameter family** captures an enormous range of physical phenomena:

- **Mechanical:** Coupled springs, damped pendulums (linearized).
- **Electrical:** RLC circuits with two energy-storage elements.
- **Biological:** Predator–prey models near equilibrium (linearization).
- **Aerospace:** Aircraft pitch dynamics, satellite librations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complete qualitative dynamics are governed by just **two scalar invariants** of the matrix $A$: its **trace** $\tau$ and its **determinant** $\Delta$.

## 2.2 Characteristic Equation — The Heart of Classification

The eigenvalues $\lambda$ of $A$ are the roots of the **characteristic polynomial**:

$$
\det(A - \lambda I) = 0
$$

For $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$, this expands to:

$$
\lambda^{2} - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0
$$

where:
- **Trace:** $\tau = a + d = \lambda_{1} + \lambda_{2}$
- **Determinant:** $\Delta = ad - bc = \lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}$

The eigenvalues follow from the **quadratic formula**:

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^{2} - 4\Delta}}{2}
$$

The discriminant $D = \tau^{2} - 4\Delta$ controls the **type** of equilibrium.

## 2.3 The Six Canonical Phase Portraits

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Classification Rule (KTU High-Yield):**

| Case | Eigenvalue Condition | Discriminant | Type of Fixed Point | Stability |
|------|--------------------|--------------|---------------------|-----------|
| 1 | $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2} \in \mathbb{R}$, $\lambda_{1} < \lambda_{2} < 0$ | $D > 0$ | **Stable Node** | Asymptotically Stable |
| 2 | $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2} \in \mathbb{R}$, $\lambda_{1} > \lambda_{2} > 0$ | $D > 0$ | **Unstable Node** | Unstable |
| 3 | $\lambda_{1} < 0 < \lambda_{2}$ (real, opposite signs) | $D > 0$ | **Saddle Point** | Unstable |
| 4 | $\lambda = \alpha \pm i\beta$, $\alpha < 0$, $\beta \neq 0$ | $D < 0$ | **Stable Spiral (Focus)** | Asymptotically Stable |
| 5 | $\lambda = \alpha \pm i\beta$, $\alpha > 0$, $\beta \neq 0$ | $D < 0$ | **Unstable Spiral (Focus)** | Unstable |
| 6 | $\lambda = \pm i\beta$ (purely imaginary) | $D < 0$ | **Center** | Lyapunov Stable |

There are also three **degenerate** cases:
- $\lambda_{1} = \lambda_{2} < 0$: **Stable Star / Proper Node**.
- $\lambda_{1} = \lambda_{2} > 0$: **Unstable Star / Proper Node**.
- $\lambda_{1} = \lambda_{2}$ with only one eigenvector: **Degenerate / Improper Node**.

## 2.4 The Trace–Determinant Plane (The "Stability Map")

The $(\tau, \Delta)$-plane is divided into regions corresponding to each canonical type:

> **The classical KTU stability map consists of:**
> - **Parabola:** $\Delta = \tau^{2}/4$ separates real eigenvalues ($D > 0$, above) from complex eigenvalues ($D < 0$, below).
> - **$\Delta = 0$ axis:** Marks a zero eigenvalue (non-hyperbolic case, line of equilibria).
> - **$\tau = 0$ axis:** Marks a pair of purely imaginary eigenvalues (Hopf bifurcation line).
> - **Origin $(\tau, \Delta) = (0,0)$:** A double-zero eigenvalue — a **Bogdanov–Takens** point.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula / Condition | Notes |
|---|----------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | General linear system | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ | $A$ is $2 \times 2$ real matrix |
| 2 | Trace | $\tau = a + d$ | Sum of eigenvalues |
| 3 | Determinant | $\Delta = ad - bc$ | Product of eigenvalues |
| 4 | Characteristic equation | $\lambda^{2} - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0$ | Standard form |
| 5 | Eigenvalues | $\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^{2} - 4\Delta}}{2}$ | Roots of characteristic equation |
| 6 | Discriminant | $D = \tau^{2} - 4\Delta$ | Real $\iff D \geq 0$ |
| 7 | Stable node | $\Delta > 0$, $\tau < 0$, $D > 0$ | Both eigenvalues real & negative |
| 8 | Unstable node | $\Delta > 0$, $\tau > 0$, $D > 0$ | Both eigenvalues real & positive |
| 9 | Saddle | $\Delta < 0$ | Eigenvalues of opposite sign |
| 10 | Stable spiral | $\Delta > 0$, $\tau < 0$, $D < 0$ | Complex with $\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0$ |
| 11 | Unstable spiral | $\Delta > 0$, $\tau > 0$, $D < 0$ | Complex with $\text{Re}(\lambda) > 0$ |
| 12 | Center | $\Delta > 0$, $\tau = 0$ | Purely imaginary eigenvalues |
| 13 | Matrix exponential solution | $\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{At}\mathbf{x}_{0}$ | Closed-form general solution |
| 14 | Stable directions | Span of eigenvectors with $\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0$ | All trajectories converge along these |
| 15 | Hartman–Grobman theorem | Local nonlinear dynamics $\cong$ linearization | For hyperbolic equilibria only |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Solution via Matrix Exponential

**Problem:** Find the explicit solution of $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ with initial condition $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{x}_{0}$.

**Step 1 — Assume a solution of exponential form.**
Suppose $\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{\lambda t} \mathbf{v}$ for some constant vector $\mathbf{v}$ and scalar $\lambda$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}\right) = A\left(e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}\right)
$$

$$
\lambda e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} = e^{\lambda t} A\mathbf{v}
$$

Since $e^{\lambda t} \neq 0$, we get the **eigenvalue equation**:

$$
A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}
$$

**Step 2 — Find eigenvalues.**
Solving $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$ yields the characteristic equation and the two eigenvalues $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}$.

**Step 3 — Find corresponding eigenvectors.**
For each $\lambda_{i}$, solve $(A - \lambda_{i} I)\mathbf{v}_{i} = 0$ to get $\mathbf{v}_{i}$.

**Step 4 — Form the general solution.**

- **Case A: Distinct real eigenvalues** ($\lambda_{1} \neq \lambda_{2}$, $\lambda_{i} \in \mathbb{R}$):

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = c_{1} e^{\lambda_{1} t}\mathbf{v}_{1} + c_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t}\mathbf{v}_{2}
$$

- **Case B: Complex conjugate eigenvalues** ($\lambda_{1,2} = \alpha \pm i\beta$):

Let $\mathbf{v}_{1} = \mathbf{p} + i\mathbf{q}$ be a complex eigenvector. The real-valued solution is:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = c_{1} e^{\alpha t}\left[\cos(\beta t)\mathbf{p} - \sin(\beta t)\mathbf{q}\right] + c_{2} e^{\alpha t}\left[\sin(\beta t)\mathbf{p} + \cos(\beta t)\mathbf{q}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Apply initial conditions** $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{x}_{0}$ to determine $c_{1}$ and $c_{2}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Example: Stable Spiral

**System:**
$$
\dot{x} = -x + y, \quad \dot{y} = -x - y
$$

**Step 1 — Write the matrix.**
$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute invariants.**
$$
\tau = -1 + (-1) = -2, \quad \Delta = (-1)(-1) - (1)(-1) = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvalues.**
$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^{2} - 4\Delta}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 8}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-4}}{2} = -1 \pm i
$$

So $\alpha = -1$, $\beta = 1$, $\Delta = 2 > 0$, $D = -4 < 0$ → **Stable Spiral** (clockwise since $\tau < 0$ for odd dimension $n=2$, we must check the sign via $\mathbf{x}^{T} A \mathbf{x}$ or use the convention that a focus rotates in a specific sense).

**Step 4 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_{1} = -1 + i$.**
Solve $(A - \lambda_{1} I)\mathbf{v}_{1} = 0$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} -1 - (-1+i) & 1 \\ -1 & -1 - (-1+i) \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} v_{1} \\ v_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{pmatrix} -i & 1 \\ -1 & -i \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} v_{1} \\ v_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

From the first row: $-i v_{1} + v_{2} = 0 \Rightarrow v_{2} = i v_{1}$. Choose $v_{1} = 1$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ i \end{pmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{p}} + i \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{q}}
$$

**Step 5 — Real solution.**
Using $\alpha = -1$, $\beta = 1$, $\mathbf{p} = (1, 0)^{T}$, $\mathbf{q} = (0, 1)^{T}$:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{-t}\left[ c_{1}\left(\cos t \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} - \sin t \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\right) + c_{2}\left(\sin t \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + \cos t \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\right) \right]
$$

Expanding component-wise:

$$
x(t) = e^{-t}\left[ c_{1}\cos t + c_{2}\sin t \right]
$$
$$
y(t) = e^{-t}\left[ -c_{1}\sin t + c_{2}\cos t \right]
$$

**Conclusion:** All trajectories spiral **inward** (as $e^{-t}$ decays) towards the origin → **asymptotically stable spiral**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Example: Saddle Point

**System:**
$$
\dot{x} = x, \quad \dot{y} = -2y
$$

**Step 1 — Matrix form.**
$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \tau = -1, \quad \Delta = -2
$$

Since $\Delta < 0$, this is a **Saddle Point**.

**Step 2 — Eigenvalues (diagonal entries).**
$$
\lambda_{1} = 1, \quad \lambda_{2} = -2
$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvectors.**
$$
\mathbf{v}_{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{v}_{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — General solution.**
$$
\mathbf{x}(t) = c_{1} e^{t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + c_{2} e^{-2t}\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Phase portrait interpretation.**
- Along the $x$-axis: trajectories move **outward** (since $\lambda_{1} > 0$) — this is the **unstable manifold**.
- Along the $y$-axis: trajectories move **inward** (since $\lambda_{2} < 0$) — this is the **stable manifold**.
- In general, trajectories approach the $y$-axis and then curve away along the $x$-direction → the iconic **saddle shape**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 2D Linear System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["State Vector x1, x2"] --> B["Coefficient Matrix A 2x2"]
    B --> C["Vector Field f of x equals Ax"]
    C --> D["Integration Step dt"]
    D --> E["Updated State x new"]
    E --> A
    F["Initial Condition x zero"] --> A
    E --> G["Eigenvalue Solver lambda1 lambda2"]
    G --> H["Phase Portrait Trajectories"]
    E --> H
```

## 4.2 Classification Decision Tree for 2D Linear Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start Compute tau and Delta"] --> Disc{"Discriminant D equals tau squared minus 4 Delta"}
    Disc -->|"D greater than 0 real eigenvalues"| RealCheck{"Same sign of lambda1 and lambda2"}
    Disc -->|"D less than 0 complex eigenvalues"| ComplexCheck{"Real part of lambda sign"}
    Disc -->|"D equals 0 repeated eigenvalue"| Degenerate["Degenerate Node: Star or Improper"]

    RealCheck -->|"Both negative Stable Node"| StableNode["Asymptotically Stable Node"]
    RealCheck -->|"Both positive Unstable Node"| UnstableNode["Unstable Node"]
    RealCheck -->|"Opposite signs"| Saddle["Saddle Point: Unstable"]

    ComplexCheck -->|"Real part negative"| StableSpiral["Stable Spiral Focus"]
    ComplexCheck -->|"Real part positive"| UnstableSpiral["Unstable Spiral Focus"]
    ComplexCheck -->|"Real part zero"| Center["Center: Lyapunov Stable"]

    StableNode --> Final["Phase Portrait Plotted"]
    UnstableNode --> Final
    Saddle --> Final
    StableSpiral --> Final
    UnstableSpiral --> Final
    Center --> Final
    Degenerate --> Final
```

## 4.3 Trace-Determinant Plane Stability Map (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Plane["Trace Determinant Plane"]
        P1["Region 1: tau less than 0 Delta greater than 0 D greater than 0"]
        P2["Region 2: tau greater than 0 Delta greater than 0 D greater than 0"]
        P3["Region 3: Delta less than 0"]
        P4["Region 4: tau less than 0 Delta greater than 0 D less than 0"]
        P5["Region 5: tau greater than 0 Delta greater than 0 D less than 0"]
        P6["Region 6: tau equals 0 Delta greater than 0"]
        P7["Axis: Delta equals 0"]
        P8["Parabola: Delta equals tau squared divided by 4"]
    end
    P1 --> L1["Stable Node"]
    P2 --> L2["Unstable Node"]
    P3 --> L3["Saddle"]
    P4 --> L4["Stable Spiral"]
    P5 --> L5["Unstable Spiral"]
    P6 --> L6["Center"]
    P7 --> L7["Non-hyperbolic line of equilibria"]
    P8 --> L8["Boundary between real and complex eigenvalues"]
```

## 4.4 Visualizing the Six Canonical Phase Portraits (Schematic Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Stable Node: trajectories converge along slowest eigenvector"] --> OUT["Phase Portrait Library"]
    A2["Unstable Node: trajectories diverge from origin along slowest eigenvector"] --> OUT
    A3["Saddle: hyperbolic in-out pattern with two invariant lines"] --> OUT
    A4["Stable Spiral: inward rotating inward spiral"] --> OUT
    A5["Unstable Spiral: outward rotating spiral"] --> OUT
    A6["Center: closed elliptical orbits around origin"] --> OUT
    B["Input: 2x2 Matrix A"] --> A1
    B --> A2
    B --> A3
    B --> A4
    B --> A5
    B --> A6
```
