# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define phase portrait. What information does the phase portrait of a linear system provide about its long-term behavior?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **phase portrait** is the geometric picture of a dynamical system's trajectories plotted in its state (phase) space, with arrows showing the direction of time flow. For a 2D linear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$, the phase portrait provides the following information about long-term behavior:
1. **Type of equilibrium** at the origin — node, saddle, spiral, or center (1 mark).
2. **Stability** — whether trajectories converge (stable), diverge (unstable), or remain bounded (Lyapunov stable) (1 mark).
3. **Directional structure** — stable/unstable manifolds and asymptotic directions given by eigenvectors (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**For the linear system $\dot{x} = 3x - 2y$, $\dot{y} = -x + y$, compute the trace, determinant, and classify the origin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Given $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -2 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

- **Trace:** $\tau = 3 + 1 = 4$ (0.5 marks)
- **Determinant:** $\Delta = (3)(1) - (-2)(-1) = 3 - 2 = 1$ (0.5 marks)
- **Discriminant:** $D = \tau^{2} - 4\Delta = 16 - 4 = 12 > 0$ (0.5 marks)
- **Eigenvalues:** $\lambda = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{12}}{2} = 2 \pm \sqrt{3}$ (0.5 marks)
- Both eigenvalues are real and positive. Hence the origin is an **unstable node** (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** For the linear system $\dot{x}_{1} = -3x_{1} + 2x_{2}$ and $\dot{x}_{2} = x_{1} - 2x_{2}$, find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and sketch the phase portrait. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify the equilibrium point at the origin for the system $\dot{x} = 2x + 5y$, $\dot{y} = -x - 2y$. Determine whether the origin is stable, asymptotically stable, or unstable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Form the matrix.**
$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 2 \\ 1 & -2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Trace and determinant.**
$$
\tau = -3 + (-2) = -5, \quad \Delta = (-3)(-2) - (2)(1) = 6 - 2 = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvalues.**
$$
\lambda = \frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 16}}{2} = \frac{-5 \pm 3}{2}
$$
$$
\lambda_{1} = -1, \quad \lambda_{2} = -4
$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_{1} = -1$.**
Solve $(A + I)\mathbf{v} = 0$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_{1} \\ v_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow v_{1} = v_{2}
$$

Take $\mathbf{v}_{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 5 — Eigenvector for $\lambda_{2} = -4$.**
Solve $(A + 4I)\mathbf{v} = 0$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} v_{1} \\ v_{2} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow v_{1} = -2v_{2}
$$

Take $\mathbf{v}_{2} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 6 — Phase portrait classification.**
- $\Delta = 4 > 0$, $\tau = -5 < 0$, $D = 9 > 0$.
- Both eigenvalues are real and negative → **Stable Node**.
- All trajectories approach the origin as $t \to \infty$.
- The **slow eigendirection** is along $\mathbf{v}_{1} = (1, 1)^{T}$ (since $\vert \lambda_{1} \vert < \vert \lambda_{2} \vert$).
- The **fast eigendirection** is along $\mathbf{v}_{2} = (-2, 1)^{T}$.

**Sketch description:** Trajectories enter the origin tangent to the slow direction $(1,1)$, then bend and align with the fast direction $(-2, 1)$ as they approach the origin. *(Award 1 mark for correct sketch identification.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Matrix and invariants.**
$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 5 \\ -1 & -2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \tau = 0, \quad \Delta = (2)(-2) - (5)(-1) = -4 + 5 = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvalues.**
$$
\lambda = \frac{0 \pm \sqrt{0 - 4}}{2} = \pm i
$$

**Step 3 — Classification.**
- $D = -4 < 0$ → complex conjugate eigenvalues.
- $\Delta = 1 > 0$, $\tau = 0$.
- $\lambda = \pm i$ → **Center**.

**Step 4 — Stability analysis.**
The eigenvalues are purely imaginary with $\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$. Trajectories form **closed orbits** around the origin, neither converging nor diverging. Therefore, the origin is **Lyapunov stable** (neutrally stable) but **not asymptotically stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:**
> Many students see $\Delta > 0$ and write "stable" without checking the sign of $\tau$. A center ($\tau = 0$) is **Lyapunov stable**, **not asymptotically stable**. Mark loss of 2 marks is typical if the distinction is missed.
>
> **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to verify that the real part of the eigenvalue is exactly zero. If even an infinitesimal perturbation adds damping, the center becomes a spiral — this is precisely the mechanism behind the **Hopf bifurcation**, which is a high-yield Module 3/4 topic.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Module Choice]

**(a)** State and prove the Hartman–Grobman theorem. Explain its significance in the study of nonlinear dynamical systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the nonlinear system $\dot{x} = x - y^{2}$, $\dot{y} = -y + x^{2}$. Linearize around the origin, find the eigenvalues of the Jacobian, and classify the equilibrium. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Statement of the Hartman–Grobman Theorem:**
*Let $\mathbf{x}^{*} = 0$ be a hyperbolic equilibrium of the $C^{1}$ system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})$ (i.e., $D\mathbf{f}(0)$ has no eigenvalues on the imaginary axis). Then there exists a homeomorphism $H$ defined on a neighborhood $U$ of the origin such that $H$ maps the trajectories of the nonlinear system onto the trajectories of its linearization $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$ where $A = D\mathbf{f}(0)$, preserving the direction of time.*

**Proof Sketch (Idea):** The proof uses the technique of **topological conjugacy**. Define the nonlinear flow $\varphi^{t}$ and the linear flow $e^{At}$. Construct $H$ via integration of vector fields using the **flow-box theorem** in the stable and unstable manifold directions separately. The hyperbolicity condition (no eigenvalue with zero real part) ensures that the stable and unstable manifolds intersect transversally, giving a unique decomposition of the tangent space, which extends to the nonlinear setting.

**Significance:**
1. It justifies **linearization** as a valid local analysis tool for nonlinear systems (2 marks).
2. The qualitative behavior (saddle, node, spiral) of a nonlinear system near a hyperbolic equilibrium is **completely determined** by the eigenvalues of the Jacobian (2 marks).
3. It is the foundation for **bifurcation theory** and **center manifold reduction** (1 mark).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Write the Jacobian of the system.**
Let $f_{1}(x, y) = x - y^{2}$ and $f_{2}(x, y) = -y + x^{2}$.

$$
J(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\partial f_{1}}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_{1}}{\partial y} \\ \frac{\partial f_{2}}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_{2}}{\partial y} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -2y \\ 2x & -1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at the origin $(0, 0)$.**
$$
A = J(0, 0) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Eigenvalues.**
Since $A$ is diagonal, $\lambda_{1} = 1$, $\lambda_{2} = -1$.

**Step 4 — Classification.**
- $\tau = 0$, $\Delta = -1 < 0$.
- Eigenvalues are real with opposite signs → **Saddle Point** (hyperbolic).
- By Hartman–Grobman, the nonlinear system has a saddle at the origin with the same qualitative structure as the linearization.

**Step 5 — Eigenvectors (for completeness).**
$$
\mathbf{v}_{1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \text{ (unstable direction)}, \quad \mathbf{v}_{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \text{ (stable direction)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake #3 (Linearization Step):**
> Students frequently compute the Jacobian correctly but then forget to **evaluate it at the equilibrium point**. Always substitute $(x, y) = (0, 0)$ explicitly. Also, students often skip the verification that the equilibrium is **hyperbolic** (no eigenvalue with $\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$) — this is a precondition for Hartman–Grobman. **(1–2 marks deducted if omitted.)**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear Systems and Phase Portraits**
>
> - **General form:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}$, with $A$ a $2 \times 2$ real matrix.
> - **Two scalar invariants** determine everything: $\tau$ (trace) and $\Delta$ (determinant).
> - **Characteristic equation:** $\lambda^{2} - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0$.
> - **Discriminant:** $D = \tau^{2} - 4\Delta$. Positive → real eigenvalues; Negative → complex eigenvalues.
> - **Six canonical types** (memorize the table): Stable Node, Unstable Node, Saddle, Stable Spiral, Unstable Spiral, Center.
> - **Saddle criterion:** $\Delta < 0$ — eigenvalues of opposite sign.
> - **Center criterion:** $\Delta > 0$ **AND** $\tau = 0$ — purely imaginary eigenvalues.
> - **Asymptotic stability** requires $\tau < 0$ **AND** $\Delta > 0$.
> - **Degenerate cases:** Repeated eigenvalues give Star Nodes (when two independent eigenvectors exist) or Improper Nodes (one eigenvector only).
> - **General solution:** $\mathbf{x}(t) = c_{1} e^{\lambda_{1} t}\mathbf{v}_{1} + c_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t}\mathbf{v}_{2}$ (distinct real case).
> - **Complex case:** Use real and imaginary parts of complex eigenvector; solution involves $e^{\alpha t}\cos(\beta t)$ and $e^{\alpha t}\sin(\beta t)$.
> - **Hartman–Grobman Theorem:** Local nonlinear dynamics near a hyperbolic equilibrium are topologically equivalent to its linearization — this is the *bridge* between linear and nonlinear theory.
> - **Hopf Bifurcation precursor:** A center ($\tau = 0$) is the critical threshold; small changes in $\tau$ turn it into a stable or unstable spiral.
> - **Engineering applications:** Coupled oscillators, RLC circuits, control systems, predator–prey models, chemical reactions (linearized).

---

topics.title: Nonlinear Systems and Limit Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Nonlinear Systems and Limit Cycles # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is a Nonlinear System? A **nonlinear system** is a dynamical system in which the change of the output is not proportional to the change of the input. Mathematically, if a system is described by a state equation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}, t)$, the system is nonlinear if $\mathbf{F}$ is a **nonlinear function** of the state $\mathbf{x}$ (or time $t$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Form):** > A system governed by an equation of the form $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$, where $\mathbf{F} : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$ is **not a linear map**, is called a *nonlinear dynamical system*. For such systems, the principle of **superposition fails**: if $\mathbf{x}_1$ and $\mathbf{x}_2$ are two solutions, then $c_1\mathbf{x}_1 + c_2\mathbf{x}_2$ is **not** a solution in general. ### Classification of Nonlinearities | Type | Mathematical Form | Example | |---|---|---| | **Static (Algebraic) Nonlinearity** | $y = f(x)$ with $f''(x) \neq 0$ | $y = x^2$ (square-law diode) | | **Geometric Nonlinearity** | Large deformations causing nonlinear strain-displacement | Buckling of columns | | **Material Nonlinearity** | $\sigma = f(\epsilon)$ is nonlinear | Plastic deformation, viscoelasticity | | **Movability / Dynamic Nonlinearity** | Time-evolution governed by $\dot{x} = f(x)$ with $f$ nonlinear | Pendulum, Duffing, Van der Pol | --- ## 1.2 What is a Limit Cycle? A **limit cycle** is an *isolated closed trajectory* in the phase plane of a 2D autonomous system. The word *isolated* means that neighbouring trajectories are **not** themselves closed — they either spiral towards the cycle or spiral away from it. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** > A closed orbit $\Gamma$ of a planar autonomous system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ is called a *limit cycle* if there exists a neighbourhood $U$ of $\Gamma$ such that $\Gamma$ is the **only** closed orbit lying entirely within $U$. **Limit cycles do NOT exist in linear systems.** This is a key distinction — limit cycles are an exclusively nonlinear phenomenon. ### Conceptual Analogy: The Whirlpool > [!TIP] > **Think of a limit cycle as a "mathematical whirlpool."** Imagine water spiralling down a drain: regardless of where you drop a leaf nearby, the leaf eventually gets pulled into a circular path and circles the drain at a fixed radius forever. The closed circular path is the **limit cycle** — nearby trajectories converge to it. Change the water flow rate? The radius of the whirlpool changes, but it remains a closed orbit. In linear systems, by contrast, trajectories either decay to a point (sink) or blow up to infinity — they can never form a self-sustaining loop. --- ## 1.3 Types of Limit Cycles (Stability Classification) A limit cycle is classified by the behaviour of nearby trajectories: | Type | Behaviour | Linearisation Eigenvalues | Real-World Analogy | |---|---|---|---| | **Stable (Attracting)** | Nearby trajectories spiral **towards** the cycle as $t \to \infty$ | $\lambda_1 > 0,\ \lambda_2 < 0$ (on limit cycle) | A perfect metronome — all near-misses get locked in | | **Unstable (Repelling)** | Nearby trajectories spiral **away** from the cycle as $t \to \infty$ | $\lambda_1 < 0,\ \lambda_2 > 0$ | A wobbly bicycle wheel — slightest push sends it off | | **Semi-Stable** | Stable from inside, unstable from outside (or vice versa) | $\lambda_1 = 0$ or higher-order effects | A knife-edge balance | --- ## 1.4 Why Limit Cycles Matter in Engineering - **Oscillator Design:** All real-world sinusoidal oscillators (electronic, mechanical, biological) are nonlinear and rely on limit cycles for sustained, stable amplitude. - **Control Engineering:** Unwanted limit cycles cause self-excited vibrations in bridges, aircraft wings, and turbine blades. - **Biophysics:** The human heart's pacemaker cells exhibit limit-cycle behaviour; cardiac arrhythmias are bifurcations of these cycles. - **Neuroscience:** Neural firing patterns (spike trains) are stable limit cycles. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Characteristics of Nonlinear Systems (Distinguishing from Linear) 1. **No Superposition Principle:** $\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2$ is not a solution. 2. **Multiple Equilibria:** A nonlinear system can have several isolated fixed points (e.g., a double-well potential has two stable equilibria). 3. **Finite-Time Escape:** Solutions can blow up in finite time. 4. **Limit Cycles & Strange Attractors:** Self-sustained oscillations and chaos. 5. **Bifurcations:** Qualitative changes in system behaviour as parameters vary. 6. **Multiple Time Scales:** Stiffness, slow manifolds, and relaxation oscillations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** Linear systems can have *centres* (closed orbits in linearisation) but **not** limit cycles. The *Hartman–Grobman Theorem* states that the linearisation is only valid **locally**; global phenomena like limit cycles arise purely from the nonlinear terms. --- ## 2.2 The Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem (Existence Criterion) This is the **central theorem** that guarantees limit cycles in 2D systems. > [!NOTE] > **Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem (KTU Formulation):** > Let $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ be a $C^1$ vector field on $\mathbb{R}^2$. Suppose: > 1. $D \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ is a **closed, bounded** region (compact). > 2. $D$ contains **no equilibrium points** of the system. > 3. A trajectory $\mathbf{x}(t)$ remains in $D$ for all $t \geq 0$. > > Then $\mathbf{x}(t)$ approaches a **limit cycle** as $t \to \infty$. **Why does this fail in 3D?** In $\mathbb{R}^3$, trajectories can escape by travelling along a third dimension (this is the geometric origin of **chaos** — e.g., the Lorenz attractor). --- ## 2.3 The Van der Pol Oscillator (The Canonical Example) The **Van der Pol equation** was introduced by Balthasar van der Pol in 1927 to model triode vacuum-tube circuits. It is the *prototypical* self-excited oscillator with a limit cycle. $$\ddot{x} - \mu\left(1 - x^2\right)\dot{x} + x = 0$$ where $\mu \geq 0$ is the **nonlinearity parameter**. **State-space form (Liénard transformation):** $$\begin{cases} \dot{x}_1 = x_2 - \mu\!\left(\dfrac{x_1^3}{3} - x_1\right) \\[2mm] \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 \end{cases}$$ where $x_1 \equiv x$. **Physical Interpretation of Terms:** - $-x$: Linear restoring force (like a harmonic oscillator). - $-\mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x}$: Nonlinear damping. - When $\vert x \vert < 1$: damping is **negative** (energy injected). - When $\vert x \vert > 1$: damping is **positive** (energy dissipated). - The system is **self-regulating**: amplitude locks onto a stable oscillation. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Van der Pol Equation** | $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$ | $\mu$ = nonlinearity parameter | | 2 | **Limit Cycle Definition** | An isolated closed orbit $\Gamma$ in phase plane | Not a centre (which has a family of closed orbits) | | 3 | **Poincaré–Bendixson** | Compact, no equilibrium $\Rightarrow$ limit cycle | 2D autonomous systems only | | 4 | **Stability of Limit Cycle** | Use $\xi = r - r_0$ perturbation; $\dot{\xi} \propto \xi$ | Sign of coefficient determines stability | | 5 | **Hopf Bifurcation Condition** | Eigenvalues of Jacobian: $\lambda_{1,2} = \alpha \pm i\beta$ | Cross $\lambda = 0$ as parameter varies | | 6 | **Radial Equation on Limit Cycle** | $\dot{r} = f(r,\theta)$ on $r = r_0$: $\dot{r}\vert_{r_0} = 0$ | Required for cycle to exist | | 7 | **Duffing Equation (Related)** | $\ddot{x} + \delta \dot{x} + \alpha x + \beta x^3 = \gamma \cos(\omega t)$ | Forced nonlinear oscillator | | 8 | **Rayleigh Equation** | $\ddot{x} - \mu\!\left(1 - \dfrac{\dot{x}^2}{3}\right)\!\dot{x} + x = 0$ | Mathematically equivalent to VdP under $\dot{x} = y$ | | 9 | **Amplitude of VdP (small $\mu$)** | $r \approx 2$ | $A \approx 2$, period $T \approx 2\pi$ | | 10 | **Dulac's Criterion** | If $\partial(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}) / \partial x_i$ has constant sign in a simply connected region, no closed orbit exists | Used to *exclude* limit cycles | | 11 | **Index Theory** | Index of equilibrium $=$ winding of $\mathbf{F}$ around it | Sum of indices of equilibria inside closed orbit = $+1$ | | 12 | **Bendixson–Dulac** | $\nabla \cdot (B\mathbf{F}) \neq 0$ sign-definite $\Rightarrow$ no closed orbit | Generalisation | --- ## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Real-World Applications | Engineering Field | Application of Limit Cycles | |---|---| | **Electrical Engineering** | Self-oscillating circuits (Colpitts, Van der Pol triode oscillators), voltage-controlled oscillators (VCOs) | | **Aerospace** | Flutter analysis (wing oscillations), control surface buzz | | **Mechanical** | Galloping of iced transmission lines, brake squeal, machine tool chatter | | **Biomedical** | Heart pacemaker rhythm, neuron firing (Hodgkin–Huxley), circadian rhythms | | **Communications** | Phase-locked loops, frequency synthesisers | | **Lasers & Optics** | Q-switching, mode-locking in lasers | # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Existence and Stability of a Limit Cycle via Radial Perturbation **Problem Setup:** Consider a general 2D autonomous system in polar form: $$\begin{cases} \dot{r} = f(r,\theta) \\ \dot{\theta} = g(r,\theta) \end{cases}$$ A **limit cycle** corresponds to a constant radius $r = r_0$, with $\theta$ advancing linearly: $\dot{\theta} = \omega \neq 0$. **Step 1 — Condition for the existence of a limit cycle:** The cycle exists at $r = r_0$ if and only if $\dot{r} = 0$ identically on $r = r_0$ (i.e., for all $\theta$ on the cycle), AND $\dot{\theta} \neq 0$ (so the orbit actually closes). For systems with circular symmetry, $f$ and $g$ are independent of $\theta$, so: $$f(r_0) = 0, \qquad g(r_0) \neq 0$$ **Step 2 — Linearised stability around $r = r_0$:** Let $\xi(t) = r(t) - r_0$ be a small radial perturbation. Taylor-expand $f$ around $r_0$: $$f(r_0 + \xi) = f(r_0) + \xi\, f'(r_0) + \frac{\xi^2}{2}f''(r_0) + \cdots$$ Dropping higher-order terms and using $f(r_0) = 0$: $$\dot{\xi} = f(r_0 + \xi) - 0 \approx \xi \cdot f'(r_0)$$ **Step 3 — Classification:** $$\dot{\xi} = f'(r_0)\,\xi$$ $$\begin{cases} f'(r_0) < 0: & \xi \to 0 \text{ as } t \to \infty \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{ STABLE LIMIT CYCLE (attractor)} \\[2mm] f'(r_0) > 0: & \vert\xi\vert \to \infty \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{ UNSTABLE LIMIT CYCLE (repellor)} \\[2mm] f'(r_0) = 0: & \text{need higher-order terms (semi-stable)} \end{cases}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Van der Pol Limit Cycle (Small $\mu$ via Averaging Method) **Problem:** Show that for small $\mu$, the Van der Pol oscillator has a limit cycle of radius $r \approx 2$ and period $T \approx 2\pi$. **Step 1 — Convert to polar form using $x = r\cos\theta,\ y = r\sin\theta$:** Van der Pol in state-space form (with $\dot{x} = y$): $$\begin{cases} \dot{x} = y \\ \dot{y} = -x + \mu(1 - x^2)y \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$:** $$2r\dot{r} = 2x\dot{x} + 2y\dot{y}$$ $$\dot{r} = \frac{x\dot{x} + y\dot{y}}{r}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the system equations:** $$x\dot{x} + y\dot{y} = x\,y + y\!\left[-x + \mu(1-x^2)y\right]$$ $$= xy - xy + \mu(1-x^2)y^2 = \mu(1 - x^2)y^2$$ **Step 4 — Express in polar coordinates ($x = r\cos\theta$):** $$y^2 = r^2 \sin^2\theta, \qquad x^2 = r^2\cos^2\theta$$ $$r\dot{r} = \mu\!\left(1 - r^2\cos^2\theta\right) r^2 \sin^2\theta$$ Dividing by $r$: $$\dot{r} = \mu\, r\, \sin^2\theta \left(1 - r^2 \cos^2\theta\right)$$ **Step 5 — Apply the method of averaging** (since $\mu$ is small, $r$ changes slowly over one period): Average $\sin^2\theta$ and $\cos^2\theta$ over $[0, 2\pi]$: $$\langle \sin^2\theta \rangle = \frac{1}{2}, \qquad \langle \cos^2\theta \rangle = \frac{1}{2}$$ $$\langle \sin^2\theta \cos^2\theta \rangle = \frac{1}{8}$$ So the averaged equation is: $$\dot{r}_{\text{avg}} = \mu r \left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{r^2}{8}\right] = \frac{\mu r}{8}\left(4 - r^2\right)$$ **Step 6 — Find fixed points of the averaged equation:** Set $\dot{r}_{\text{avg}} = 0$: $$r = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad r^2 = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = 2$$ **Step 7 — Stability:** $$\frac{d\dot{r}_{\text{avg}}}{dr} = \frac{\mu}{8}\left(4 - 3r^2\right)$$ At $r = 0$: derivative $= \mu/2 > 0$ (unstable, as expected — origin is an unstable focus). At $r = 2$: derivative $= \mu/8 (4 - 12) = -\mu < 0$ (STABLE limit cycle). **Conclusion:** For small $\mu > 0$, the Van der Pol oscillator has a **stable limit cycle** of radius: $$\boxed{r_0 \approx 2 \quad \text{(amplitude } A = 2\text{)}}$$ with period $T \approx 2\pi$ (for small $\mu$; for large $\mu$ the period is $T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu$). --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Poincaré–Bendixson via Winding Number Argument **Step 1:** Suppose a trajectory $\mathbf{x}(t)$ is trapped in a compact, simply connected region $D$ with no equilibrium points. **Step 2:** The trajectory cannot escape to infinity (bounded) and cannot converge to an equilibrium (none exist). **Step 3:** By continuity of $\mathbf{F}$ and the absence of equilibria, the flow must be defined for all $t$. **Step 4:** The $\omega$-limit set of $\mathbf{x}(t)$ is non-empty, compact, invariant, and contains no equilibrium. **Step 5:** The only compact, connected, invariant set in 2D without equilibrium is a **closed orbit**. **Step 6:** Therefore $\mathbf{x}(t)$ must approach this closed orbit as $t \to \infty$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** In 2D, the only way for a trajectory to "nowhere go" is to wrap around and form a closed loop. In 3D, it can "escape" through the third dimension — this topological fact is the deep reason why **chaos exists only in 3+ dimensions**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Hopf Bifurcation (Birth of a Limit Cycle) **Setup:** Consider a system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}, \mu)$ with a fixed point at $\mathbf{x}^* = 0$. The Jacobian at the origin has eigenvalues: $$\lambda_{1,2}(\mu) = \alpha(\mu) \pm i\,\beta(\mu), \qquad \beta(0) \neq 0$$ **Hopf Bifurcation Theorem (Andronov, 1941):** If: 1. $\alpha(0) = 0$ (eigenvalues cross imaginary axis at $\mu = 0$), 2. $\alpha'(0) \neq 0$ (transverse crossing), 3. A non-degeneracy (nonlinearity) condition holds, then a **limit cycle** is born at $\mu = 0$, with amplitude: $$r \sim \sqrt{\mu \cdot (\text{const})}$$ and frequency $\omega \approx \beta(0)$. **The Van der Pol oscillator is a textbook example** of supercritical Hopf bifurcation: as $\mu$ crosses zero from negative to positive, a stable limit cycle is born from the origin. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Centres vs Limit Cycles | Property | Centre (Linear) | Limit Cycle (Nonlinear) | |---|---|---| | **Nature of nearby orbits** | Infinitely many closed orbits | Isolated single closed orbit | | **Exists in linear systems?** | Yes (e.g., $\ddot{x} + x = 0$) | **No** (fundamentally nonlinear) | | **Robust under perturbation?** | No — generic perturbation destroys it | Yes (structurally stable) | | **Stability** | Marginally stable (Lyapunov) | Strictly attracting/repelling | | **Frequency** | Fixed by system | Self-selected by dynamics | | **Physical example** | Ideal frictionless pendulum | Sustained electronic oscillator | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Phase Portrait Hierarchy of Nonlinear 2D Systems ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Nonlinear 2D Autonomous System] --> B{Equilibria Analysis} A --> C{Trajectory Behaviour} B --> B1[Compute fixed points
F x = 0] B1 --> B2[Linearise at fixed point
Compute Jacobian J] B2 --> B3{Eigenvalues of J} B3 --> B31[Real + Real
Node or Saddle] B3 --> B32[Complex conjugate

Non-hyperbolic case] C --> C1[Trajectories remain bounded
and trapped] C --> C2[Trajectories escape to infinity] C1 --> D1[Apply Poincare Bendixson] D1 --> D2{Region D contains
equilibria?} D2 -->|No| D3[Limit Cycle Exists] D2 -->|Yes| D4[Apply Bendixson Dulac Criterion] D4 --> D5[If div BF has constant sign
No closed orbit] D4 --> D6[Index Theory check
Sum of indices = +1] D6 --> D7[Closed orbit confirmed] D3 --> E1[Compute Stability] E1 --> E2[Linearise radially
f prime r0] E2 --> E3{f prime r0} E3 -->|< 0| F1[STABLE LIMIT CYCLE
Attractor] E3 -->|> 0| F2[UNSTABLE LIMIT CYCLE

xi dot = f prime r0 times xi] U --> V{f prime r0} V -->|Negative| W1[Stable Limit Cycle

Repulsive Cycle] W3 --> X3[Examples: Bifurcation boundary case] style L fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff style W1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style W2 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff style W3 fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Limit Cycle Analysis Workflow (Engineering) ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Physical System
e.g. Oscillator Circuit] --> M[Mathematical Model
ODE or System of ODEs] M --> SS[State Space Form
x dot = F of x] SS --> EQ[Find Equilibria
Solve F x = 0] EQ --> JAC[Compute Jacobian J

Check Hopf Bifurcation] CLASS -->|Re lambda non zero| LR[Linear Dynamics Dominates] HB --> NORM[Normal Form Reduction] NORM --> AMP[Compute Limit Cycle
Amplitude r0] AMP --> STAB[Stability Test
