Visualisation Tool] OUT2 --> VIZ style P fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff style M fill:#455a64,color:#ffffff style HB fill:#bf360c,color:#ffffff style AMP fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff style STAB fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff style VIZ fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Flow: Centre (Linear) vs Limit Cycle (Nonlinear) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Linear_World [LINEAR SYSTEMS] L1[Closed orbit = Centre] --> L2[Family of closed orbits
at every radius] L2 --> L3[Marginally stable
Lyapunov] L3 --> L4[NOT a limit cycle
Not isolated] end subgraph Nonlinear_World [NONLINEAR SYSTEMS] N1[Closed orbit = Limit Cycle] --> N2[Isolated single orbit
at unique radius r0] N2 --> N3[Strictly stable or unstable
Asymptotic] N3 --> N4[IS a limit cycle
Structurally stable] end Linear_World -.Perturbation.- DestX[Destroyed] DestX -.-> N5[Converted to Focus
or Limit Cycle] Nonlinear_World -.Perturbation.- Surv[Survives] Surv -.-> N6[Robust under noise] style Linear_World fill:#e3f2fd,color:#000000 style Nonlinear_World fill:#fff3e0,color:#000000 style L4 fill:#ffcdd2,color:#000000 style N4 fill:#c8e6c9,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define a limit cycle. Differentiate between a centre and a limit cycle.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **limit cycle** is an *isolated closed trajectory* in the phase plane of a 2D autonomous nonlinear system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$. "Isolated" means it is the **only** closed orbit in a small neighbourhood. **Centre vs Limit Cycle:** | Feature | Centre (Linear) | Limit Cycle (Nonlinear) | |---|---|---| | Closed orbits | Infinitely many (at every radius) | Exactly one (isolated) | | Stability | Marginally stable (neutral) | Asymptotically stable or unstable | | Existence | Linear systems | **Only nonlinear systems** | | Robustness | Destroyed by any perturbation | Structurally stable | *(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – centre, 1 mark – limit cycle difference)* --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **State the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem and explain its significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** If a trajectory of a 2D $C^1$ autonomous system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ remains in a **closed bounded region $D$** containing **no equilibrium points**, then the trajectory must approach a **limit cycle** as $t \to \infty$. **Significance:** 1. It guarantees the *existence* of limit cycles without solving the system explicitly. 2. It is **valid only in 2D** — in 3D, trajectories can escape (this is the geometric reason for chaos). 3. It is the *only* general theorem in dynamical systems that certifies closed-orbit existence. *(1 mark – statement, 1 mark – conditions, 1 mark – significance)* --- ## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### QUESTION A: [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** [7 Marks] Consider the Van der Pol oscillator: $$\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0, \qquad \mu > 0$$ Convert it to a system of first-order ODEs in state-space form. Using polar coordinates, derive the averaged radial equation and show that a stable limit cycle of amplitude $r_0 = 2$ exists. **(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem, prove that the system $$\dot{x} = x - y - x(x^2 + y^2), \quad \dot{y} = x + y - y(x^2 + y^2)$$ has a unique stable limit cycle. Identify its radius. --- #### MODEL SOLUTION **Part (a) — Step-by-step (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert to first-order system. Let $x_1 = x$, $x_2 = \dot{x}$: $$\begin{cases} \dot{x}_1 = x_2 \\[2mm] \dot{x}_2 = -x_1 + \mu(1 - x_1^2)x_2 \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Convert to polar form $x_1 = r\cos\theta$, $x_2 = r\sin\theta$. As derived in §3.2: $$\dot{r} = \mu\, r\, \sin^2\theta \left(1 - r^2 \cos^2\theta\right)$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply averaging over $\theta$: $$\langle \sin^2\theta \rangle = 1/2, \quad \langle \sin^2\theta \cos^2\theta \rangle = 1/8$$ $$\dot{r}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\mu r}{8}(4 - r^2)$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Set $\dot{r}_{\text{avg}} = 0$: $$r = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad r = 2$$ **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Stability — evaluate $d\dot{r}/dr$ at $r = 2$: $$\frac{d\dot{r}_{\text{avg}}}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=2} = \frac{\mu}{8}(4 - 12) = -\mu < 0$$ **Conclusion (1.5 marks):** Since the derivative is negative, the limit cycle at $r_0 = 2$ is **stable**. The Van der Pol oscillator exhibits a stable limit cycle of amplitude 2 for any $\mu > 0$. --- **Part (b) — Step-by-step (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert to polar form. Let $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$: $$x\dot{x} + y\dot{y} = (x^2 - xy - x^2 R) + (xy + y^2 - y^2 R) = (x^2 + y^2)(1 - R)$$ where $R = x^2 + y^2 = r^2$. Hence: $$r\dot{r} = r^2(1 - r^2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{r} = r(1 - r^2)$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** For the angular equation, after standard manipulations: $$\dot{\theta} = 1 \neq 0$$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Fixed points of $\dot{r} = 0$: $$r(1 - r^2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad r = 1$$ The origin is the only equilibrium of the system; $r = 1$ is a closed orbit. **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Stability — compute $d\dot{r}/dr$ at $r = 1$: $$\frac{d\dot{r}}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=1} = 1 - 3(1)^2 = -2 < 0$$ **Conclusion (1.5 marks):** The cycle at $r = 1$ is **stable** and **unique** (Poincaré–Bendixson applies because the only equilibrium is the origin; trajectories with $r > 0$ are trapped between $r = 0$ and $r = 1$ in the limit). --- ### QUESTION B: [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (Internal Choice) **(a)** [7 Marks] Define a **Hopf bifurcation**. Show that the system $$\dot{x} = \mu x - y + x(x^2 + y^2), \quad \dot{y} = x + \mu y + y(x^2 + y^2)$$ undergoes a Hopf bifurcation at $\mu = 0$ and determine the stability of the resulting limit cycle. **(b)** [7 Marks] With a clear phase-portrait sketch (using a block diagram if needed), explain the structural difference between a **stable**, **unstable**, and **semi-stable** limit cycle. For each, write the corresponding condition on the linearised radial derivative $f'(r_0)$. --- #### MODEL SOLUTION **Part (a) — Step-by-step (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** **Definition of Hopf Bifurcation:** A Hopf bifurcation occurs when a pair of complex conjugate eigenvalues of the linearisation at a fixed point cross the imaginary axis (i.e., $\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0$) as a parameter $\mu$ varies, leading to the birth of a limit cycle. **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** The origin $(0,0)$ is a fixed point. The Jacobian is: $$J = \begin{pmatrix} \mu & -1 \\ 1 & \mu \end{pmatrix}$$ Eigenvalues: $\lambda_{1,2} = \mu \pm i$. So at $\mu = 0$, $\lambda_{1,2} = \pm i$ — purely imaginary. **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Convert to polar form. Let $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$: $$x\dot{x} + y\dot{y} = (\mu x^2 - xy + x^2 r^2) + (\mu y^2 + xy + y^2 r^2) = r^2(\mu + r^2)$$ $$r\dot{r} = r^2(\mu + r^2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{r} = r(\mu + r^2)$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Fixed points of $\dot{r} = 0$: $$r = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad r^2 = -\mu$$ For $\mu < 0$, $r^2 = -\mu > 0$ — a limit cycle of radius $r_0 = \sqrt{-\mu}$ exists. **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Stability: $$\frac{d\dot{r}}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r_0} = (\mu + r_0^2) + r_0 \cdot 2r_0 = 0 + 2(-\mu) = -2\mu$$ For $\mu < 0$: derivative $= -2\mu > 0$ — **UNSTABLE limit cycle**. **Conclusion:** A supercritical Hopf bifurcation occurs at $\mu = 0$, creating an **unstable** limit cycle of radius $\sqrt{-\mu}$ for $\mu < 0$. --- **Part (b) — Step-by-step (7 marks):** | Type | Condition on $f'(r_0)$ | Phase Portrait Behaviour | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Stable** | $f'(r_0) < 0$ | Trajectories spiral **in** from both sides | Van der Pol | | **Unstable** | $f'(r_0) > 0$ | Trajectories spiral **out** to both sides | Reverse VdP | | **Semi-stable** | $f'(r_0) = 0$ (use higher order) | Spiral in from one side, out from the other | Saddle-node of cycles | **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define $f(r) = \dot{r}$ when the system is in radial form. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Linearise: $\dot{\xi} = f'(r_0)\xi$ where $\xi = r - r_0$. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Sign analysis: $\xi \to 0$ iff $f'(r_0) < 0$ (stable); $\vert\xi\vert \to \infty$ iff $f'(r_0) > 0$ (unstable). **Step 4 (2 marks):** Sketch / block diagram (see §4.2 of these notes): arrows pointing inward = stable; outward = unstable; mixed = semi-stable. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating "centre" with "limit cycle" (–2 marks):** A centre has a *family* of closed orbits (linear); a limit cycle is *isolated* (nonlinear). Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 2. **Forgetting to check Poincaré–Bendixson conditions (–1.5 marks):** Region must be **closed, bounded, AND contain no equilibria**. Missing any condition = zero marks for the theorem application. > 3. **Sign error in radial stability (–2 marks):** Students often write "stable" when $f'(r_0) > 0$. **Remember:** $\dot{\xi} = f'(r_0)\xi$ — decay requires $f'(r_0) < 0$. > 4. **Skipping the averaging step (–1 mark):** When deriving Van der Pol's $r_0 = 2$, you MUST show the time-averaging of $\sin^2\theta$ and $\cos^2\theta$, not just state the result. > 5. **Hopf direction (–1 mark):** Always state whether the bifurcation is *subcritical* (unstable cycle in valid parameter range) or *supercritical* (stable cycle). > 6. **Units and parameter domains (–0.5 marks):** Always specify $\mu > 0$ or $\mu < 0$ explicitly when stating VdP / Hopf results. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nonlinear Systems and Limit Cycles** **Core Definitions:** - **Limit Cycle:** *Isolated* closed orbit in the phase plane of a 2D autonomous nonlinear system. - **Nonlinear System:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ with $\mathbf{F}$ nonlinear — superposition fails. - **Centre (Linear):** Family of closed orbits (not a limit cycle). - **Hopf Bifurcation:** Birth of a limit cycle as eigenvalues cross the imaginary axis. **Theorems to Memorise:** - **Poincaré–Bendixson:** Bounded trajectory in compact region with no equilibria ⇒ limit cycle (2D only). - **Bendixson–Dulac Criterion:** If $\nabla \cdot (B\mathbf{F})$ has constant sign in a simply connected region, no closed orbit exists. - **Index Theorem:** Sum of indices of equilibria inside a closed orbit = +1. **Key Equations:** - Van der Pol: $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$ — has stable limit cycle of radius $\approx 2$. - Rayleigh: $\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - \dot{x}^2/3)\dot{x} + x = 0$ — equivalent to VdP. - Polar form of $\dot{r} = r(1 - r^2)$: stable limit cycle at $r = 1$. **Stability Tests:** - $\dot{\xi} = f'(r_0)\xi$ with $\xi = r - r_0$. - $f'(r_0) < 0$: **stable** (attracting). - $f'(r_0) > 0$: **unstable** (repelling). - $f'(r_0) = 0$: **semi-stable** (higher-order). **High-Yield Numerical Facts:** - VdP amplitude: $A \approx 2$ for small $\mu$; period $T \approx 2\pi$ (small $\mu$), $T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu$ (large $\mu$). - Hopf: amplitude $r_0 \sim \sqrt{\vert\mu\vert}$, frequency $\omega \approx \beta(0)$. **Conceptual Distinctions (Frequently Tested):** - Limit cycles DO NOT exist in linear systems. - Centres are NOT limit cycles. - Bifurcations are NOT continuous deformations — they are qualitative jumps. - Poincaré–Bendixson FAILS in 3D (topological origin of chaos). **Engineering Connections (Always Mention in Long Answers):** - VCOs, PLL, Colpitts oscillators (electrical). - Flutter, brake squeal, galloping (mechanical/aerospace). - Heart pacemaker, neuron firing (biomedical). - Mode-locked lasers (optics). **Examiner Traps to Avoid:** - Don't write "stable" without showing $f'(r_0) < 0$. - Don't apply Poincaré–Bendixson to 3D systems. - Don't forget to state $\mu > 0$ (or $\mu < 0$) explicitly in VdP problems. - Don't confuse **period** of oscillation with **amplitude**.

topics.title: Limit Cycles and Oscillators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Limit Cycles and Oscillators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Limit Cycle?

In the study of nonlinear dynamical systems governed by autonomous ordinary differential equations of the form:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}), \quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$$

a **Limit Cycle** is defined as an **isolated closed trajectory** in the phase plane. Every neighboring trajectory (whether starting from inside or outside) spirals toward this closed orbit as $t \to \infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Accepted):**
> A *limit cycle* is a closed, isolated, periodic orbit of an autonomous continuous-time dynamical system. Unlike the center (elliptic point) of a linear harmonic oscillator, a limit cycle is **structurally stable** — small perturbations in initial conditions or system parameters do not destroy the oscillation, but merely shift the trajectory back onto the cycle.

### Key Distinction: Closed Orbit vs. Limit Cycle

| Feature | Linear Center (e.g., $\ddot{x} + x = 0$) | Limit Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Closed orbits | Infinite family (one per initial condition) | **Isolated** (one specific orbit) |
| Structural stability | **Unstable** (small damping destroys the cycle) | **Stable** (perturbations return trajectory to cycle) |
| Amplitude | Fixed by initial energy | Fixed by **system parameters** |
| Period | $T = 2\pi/\omega$ | $T = T(\mu)$ (parameter-dependent) |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Heart of a Mechanical Watch

Imagine a **pendulum clock** (a mechanical oscillator):

- When you wind the clock harder, the amplitude of the pendulum swing **grows** initially but **does not blow up to infinity** — it settles to a *fixed amplitude*.
- If the clock loses energy (friction), the swing **shrinks** but again does not decay to zero — it returns to the same steady oscillation.
- The pendulum traces a **closed, repeating path** in phase space $(x, \dot{x})$ — this is the **limit cycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The energy balance picture:** A limit cycle emerges when the system is *non-conservative* and there exists a *radial damping function* $g(r)$ such that:
> - For $r < r_0$ (inside cycle): **negative damping** (energy is *pumped in*).
> - For $r > r_0$ (outside cycle): **positive damping** (energy is *dissipated*).
> - At $r = r_0$: **exact balance** — the closed orbit.

This is the hallmark of a **stable limit cycle**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Limit Cycles (KTU-Syllabus Mandatory)

| Type | Behavior of Neighboring Trajectories | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Stable** (Attracting) | Both inside and outside spirals **inward** | $\alpha$-limit set = outside, $\omega$-limit set = cycle |
| **Unstable** (Repelling) | Both inside and outside spirals **outward** | $\alpha$-limit set = cycle, $\omega$-limit set = $\infty$ |
| **Semi-stable** (Half-stable) | Inside spirals in, outside spirals out (or vice versa) | Bifurcation-type behavior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Van der Pol oscillator** is the *prototypical example* of a stable limit cycle and is universally examined in KTU papers.

---

## 1.4 The Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem (Existence Guarantee)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favorite Theorem:** In $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, a non-closed trajectory that remains in a *bounded region* (a compact set) containing **no equilibrium points** must approach a **limit cycle** as $t \to \infty$.

This is the **only general tool** in planar dynamics that *proves* the existence of periodic orbits without explicit integration. It is the *two-dimensional analogue* of the existence of periodic orbits in Hamiltonian systems and is **exclusive to $\mathbb{R}^{2}$** — it fails in $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ (this is why chaos can exist in 3D Lorenz system but not in 2D Van der Pol).

---

## 1.5 Why Linear Theory Fails

For the linear oscillator $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta \omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$:

- If $\zeta = 0$ (undamped): every solution is periodic with amplitude = initial amplitude.
- If $\zeta > 0$ (damped): trajectory spirals to the **origin** (an equilibrium, not a limit cycle).
- Linear systems **never** produce *isolated* periodic orbits — they can only produce center manifolds or spiraling equilibria.

Hence, **limit cycles are a fundamentally nonlinear phenomenon**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation

Consider the **planar autonomous system**:

$$\dot{x} = P(x, y), \qquad \dot{y} = Q(x, y)$$

A **limit cycle** is a closed orbit $\Gamma$ in the $(x,y)$ plane such that:
1. $\Gamma$ is a periodic solution, i.e., there exists $T > 0$ with $(x(t+T), y(t+T)) = (x(t), y(t))$ for all $t$.
2. There exists a *tubular neighborhood* $N$ of $\Gamma$ that contains **no other** closed orbits of the system.

In **polar coordinates** $(r, \theta)$, a radial equation takes the canonical form:

$$\dot{r} = r \cdot f(r), \qquad \dot{\theta} = g(r)$$

A limit cycle corresponds to a **positive root** $r = r_0$ of $f(r_0) = 0$ with $f'(r_0) \neq 0$.

---

## 2.2 Stability Criterion for Radial Limit Cycle

The sign of $f'(r_0)$ determines stability:

- If $f'(r_0) < 0$ : **Stable** (trajectories converge inward from $r > r_0$ and outward from $r < r_0$).
- If $f'(r_0) > 0$ : **Unstable** (trajectories diverge).
- If $f'(r_0) = 0$ : **Semi-stable** (higher-order analysis required).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Theorem | Description | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{r} = r\, f(r)$ | Radial equation in polar form | 3 marks |
| 2 | $r_0$ where $f(r_0) = 0$ | Radius of limit cycle | 3 marks |
| 3 | Stability: $\text{sign}\bigl(f'(r_0)\bigr)$ | Determines attract/repel nature | 3 marks |
| 4 | $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$ | **Van der Pol equation** | 7–14 marks |
| 5 | $\ddot{x} + \delta \dot{x} - \alpha x + \beta x^3 = 0$ | **Duffing equation** | 7–14 marks |
| 6 | Period (VDP, large $\mu$): $T \approx \mu(3 - 2\ln 2)$ | Relaxation oscillation period | 5 marks |
| 7 | Hopf bifurcation: eigenvalues cross $\pm i\omega_c$ | Birth of limit cycle from equilibrium | 7 marks |
| 8 | Poincaré–Bendixson | Existence of limit cycle in $\mathbb{R}^2$ | 3–7 marks |
| 9 | $\omega_c^2 = \det(J)$ at bifurcation | Critical frequency | 3 marks |
| 10 | Amplitude after Hopf: $r_0 = \sqrt{\mu/\alpha}$ | Stable amplitude for $\mu > 0$ | 3 marks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When writing the radial equation, *always* show the **Jacobian transformation** $(x, y) \to (r, \theta)$ explicitly. Marks are awarded for the **derivation**, not the final answer.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Limit Cycles

| Engineering Domain | Limit Cycle Application |
|---|---|
| **Electronics** | Sinusoidal oscillators (Colpitts, Hartley, Crystal) — all use nonlinear amplitude-limiting (limit-cycle stabilization) |
| **Mechanical Vibration** | Self-excited vibrations (galloping of iced cables, flutter of aircraft wings) |
| **Biomedical** | Cardiac rhythms (action potential oscillations), neural firing, circadian rhythms |
| **Acoustics** | Bowed string instruments (Helmholtz motion = limit cycle of string) |
| **Control Systems** | Sustained oscillations in servo loops, relay auto-tuners, sliding-mode control |
| **Lasers** | Mode-locking, Q-switching — the laser's intensity is a limit cycle |

---

## 2.5 The Three Theorems (KTU Module 3 Mandatory List)

1. **Existence** (Poincaré–Bendixson) — guarantees *some* limit cycle exists.
2. **Uniqueness** (Poincaré–Bendixson extended) — *only one* limit cycle in a trapping region.
3. **Non-existence** (Bendixson's Criterion) — divergence $\partial P/\partial x + \partial Q/\partial y$ does not change sign in a simply-connected region $\Rightarrow$ **no closed orbits exist**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bendixson's Negative Criterion** is your *first test* in KTU problems. Always check $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = P_x + Q_y$ first — if it is sign-definite, then **no limit cycle can exist** and the problem is solved in one line.

---

## 2.6 Hopf Bifurcation — Birth of a Limit Cycle

When the linearization at a fixed point has eigenvalues $\lambda = \alpha(\mu) \pm i\omega(\mu)$ and $\alpha(0) = 0$, $\alpha'(0) \neq 0$:

$$\dot{x} = \alpha(\mu)\, x - \omega(\mu)\, y + \text{(nonlinear terms)}$$

$$\dot{y} = \omega(\mu)\, x + \alpha(\mu)\, y + \text{(nonlinear terms)}$$

In polar coordinates, the normal form reduces to:

$$\dot{r} = \alpha(\mu)\, r + b r^3 + \mathcal{O}(r^5)$$

- If $b < 0$ : **Supercritical Hopf** (stable limit cycle of radius $r_0 = \sqrt{-\alpha(\mu)/b}$ for $\mu > 0$).
- If $b > 0$ : **Subcritical Hopf** (unstable limit cycle; jumps to a distant attractor).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Radial Equation from Cartesian System

**Given:** $\dot{x} = -y + x f(r), \quad \dot{y} = x + y f(r), \quad$ where $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$.

**To find:** the radial equation $\dot{r} = r f(r)$.

### Step 1 — Differentiate $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}(r^2) = 2r \dot{r} = 2x \dot{x} + 2y \dot{y}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$:

$$2r \dot{r} = 2x\bigl[-y + x f(r)\bigr] + 2y\bigl[x + y f(r)\bigr]$$

### Step 3 — Expand:

$$2r \dot{r} = -2xy + 2x^2 f(r) + 2xy + 2y^2 f(r)$$

### Step 4 — Cancel $-2xy$ and $+2xy$:

$$2r \dot{r} = 2x^2 f(r) + 2y^2 f(r) = 2(x^2 + y^2) f(r) = 2r^2 f(r)$$

### Step 5 — Divide both sides by $2r$ (for $r > 0$):

$$\boxed{\dot{r} = r\, f(r)}$$

This is the **canonical radial equation** on which all KTU limit-cycle problems are based.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Period of the Van der Pol Oscillator (Relaxation Limit, $\mu \gg 1$)

**Van der Pol equation:**

$$\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0, \quad \mu \gg 1$$

For $\mu \gg 1$, motion alternates between **slow phases** (near the slow manifold) and **fast jumps**.

### Step 1 — Identify the slow manifold:

In the Liénard plane $(x, v = \dot{x} - \mu(x - x^3/3))$:

$$\dot{x} = v + \mu\bigl(x - \tfrac{x^3}{3}\bigr), \qquad \dot{v} = -x$$

### Step 2 — Slow dynamics near $v = 0$ (limit of large $\mu$):

Setting $\dot{v} \approx 0$ gives $x \approx 0$, so the slow manifold is the cubic:

$$v = -\mu\bigl(x - \tfrac{x^3}{3}\bigr) \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = -\mu \Phi(x)$$

where $\Phi(x) = x - x^3/3$ has zeros at $x = 0, \pm\sqrt{3}$.

### Step 3 — Traverse the slow manifold:

The orbit moves slowly from the turning point $x = +2$ (where $v = 0$, $\dot{x} = 0$) along the lower branch of the cubic toward $x = -2$.

The time spent in this slow phase is:

$$T_{\text{slow}} = \oint \frac{dx}{\dot{x}}\Big\vert_{\dot{x} = \mu \Phi(x)} = \mu \int_{-2}^{2} \frac{dx}{\Phi(x)} \cdot \text{(scaling)}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate by known result (Van der Pol, 1926):

The exact leading-order period is:

$$\boxed{T \approx \mu(3 - 2\ln 2), \quad \mu \gg 1}$$

This **grows linearly with $\mu$** — Van der Pol called such slow-fast motion **relaxation oscillation** (a KTU 14-mark favorite).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hopf Bifurcation Amplitude (Normal Form)

**Normal form in polar coordinates:**

$$\dot{r} = \mu r - r^3, \qquad \dot{\theta} = 1$$

### Step 1 — Find fixed points (limit cycles in radial sense):

Setting $\dot{r} = 0$:

$$\mu r - r^3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r(\mu - r^2) = 0$$

### Step 2 — Solutions:

- $r = 0$ : origin (equilibrium of full system).
- $r^2 = \mu$ : limit cycle of radius $r_0 = \sqrt{\mu}$, **valid only for $\mu \geq 0$**.

### Step 3 — Stability of limit cycle:

$f(r) = \mu - r^2$. Differentiate:

$$f'(r_0) = -2r_0 = -2\sqrt{\mu} < 0 \quad \text{for } \mu > 0$$

So this is a **stable** (supercritical) Hopf bifurcation.

### Step 4 — Period of the limit cycle:

From $\dot{\theta} = 1$, the period is $T = 2\pi$, independent of $\mu$ (in this normal form).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bendixson's Criterion (Non-existence Proof)

**Given system:** $\dot{x} = P(x, y), \quad \dot{y} = Q(x, y)$.

**Bendixson's Negative Criterion:** If on a simply-connected region $D \subset \mathbb{R}^2$:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial Q}{\partial y} \neq 0 \quad \text{throughout } D$$

(does not change sign and is not identically zero), then **no closed orbit** can lie entirely within $D$.

### Worked Example (KTU-style):

For the system $\dot{x} = x^2 - y^2 + 2x$, $\dot{y} = 2xy + 3y$:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial Q}{\partial y} = (2x + 2) + (2x + 3) = 4x + 5$$

This does not change sign in the strip $-5/4 < x < 0$ — wait, it does cross zero. Let us choose $D = \{(x, y): x > 0\}$:

$$4x + 5 > 5 > 0 \quad \forall\, x > 0$$

So **no closed orbit exists** in the right half-plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important caveat:** Bendixson's criterion is **sufficient but not necessary**. A system *can* have no limit cycles even if the divergence does change sign (e.g., Lotka–Volterra). But when the criterion *is* satisfied, the conclusion is rigorous.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Duffing Oscillator Phase Portrait (Hardening Spring)

**Duffing equation** (forced-free version):

$$\ddot{x} + \delta \dot{x} - \alpha x + \beta x^3 = 0, \quad \delta, \alpha, \beta > 0$$

### Step 1 — Equivalent first-order system:

$$\dot{x} = y, \qquad \dot{y} = -\delta y + \alpha x - \beta x^3$$

### Step 2 — Equilibria (where $\dot{x} = \dot{y} = 0$):

$y = 0$ and $\alpha x - \beta x^3 = 0 \Rightarrow x(\alpha - \beta x^2) = 0$

So equilibria: $(0, 0)$, $\bigl(\pm\sqrt{\alpha/\beta}, 0\bigr)$ — the latter two exist only if $\alpha/\beta > 0$ (always true here).

### Step 3 — Jacobian:

$$J(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ \alpha - 3\beta x^2 & -\delta \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Eigenvalues at the origin:

$\lambda^2 + \delta \lambda - \alpha = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = \dfrac{-\delta \pm \sqrt{\delta^2 + 4\alpha}}{2}$

For $\delta > 0$, $\alpha > 0$: $\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0$ — origin is an **unstable node** (saddle in fact — $\det(J) = -\alpha < 0$).

### Step 5 — Eigenvalues at the off-axis equilibria:

$\lambda^2 + \delta \lambda + (3\alpha - \alpha) = \lambda^2 + \delta \lambda + 2\alpha = 0$

Discriminant $= \delta^2 - 8\alpha$. For $\delta$ small: complex with **negative real part** — these are **stable spirals**.

### Step 6 — Phase portrait:

- $(0,0)$ : **saddle** — energy escapes along separatrices.
- $\bigl(\pm\sqrt{\alpha/\beta}, 0\bigr)$ : **stable spirals** — trajectories spiral inward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU insight:** Trajectories starting near the saddle's separatrices get *ejected*, and they *cannot* settle to the stable spirals if damping is *too small* (separatrices enclose a forbidden region). The Duffing oscillator with this parameter set **does not have a limit cycle** — its bounded attractors are the stable equilibria.

A limit cycle emerges only in the **forced Duffing** (with external periodic forcing) or with different sign of $\alpha$ (Duffing–Van der Pol hybrid).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Limit Cycle Comparison

| Property | Van der Pol | Duffing (autonomous) | Hopf Normal Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equation | $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$ | $\ddot{x} + \delta \dot{x} - \alpha x + \beta x^3 = 0$ | $\dot{r} = \mu r - r^3$ |
| Limit cycle? | **Yes** (stable) | **No** (autonomous) | Yes (for $\mu > 0$) |
| Period $T$ | $T \approx \mu(3-2\ln 2)$ large $\mu$ | N/A | $T = 2\pi$ |
| Amplitude $r_0$ | $r_0 = 2$ (exact, all $\mu$) | N/A | $r_0 = \sqrt{\mu}$ |
| Bifurcation at origin | Hopf at $\mu = 0$ | None | Hopf at $\mu = 0$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Limit Cycle Attractor — Stable Case

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Initial Condition x0, y0] --> B{Region Check}
    B -->|r greater than r0| C[Positive Damping Region]
    B -->|r less than r0| D[Negative Damping Region]
    B -->|r equals r0| E[Limit Cycle Gamma]
    C --> F[Energy Dissipated]
    F --> E
    D --> G[Energy Pumped In]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Periodic Oscillation of Period T]
    H --> E
```

> **Reading aid:** The arrows show the *direction* trajectories travel. Outside the cycle, energy is removed (positive damping) so $r$ decreases. Inside, energy is added (negative damping) so $r$ grows. The cycle is the unique steady-state periodic orbit.

---

## 4.2 Hopf Bifurcation Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MuLessThan0["mu less than 0 stable equilibrium"]
        S1[Origin r equals 0] -->|all trajectories| S1
    end
    subgraph MuEquals0["mu equals 0 bifurcation point"]
        S2[Center manifold]
    end
    subgraph MuGreaterThan0["mu greater than 0 stable limit cycle"]
        S3[Origin r equals 0 unstable] -->|spiral outward| S4[Stable limit cycle r0 equals sqrt of mu]
    end
    MuLessThan0 --> MuEquals0
    MuEquals0 --> MuGreaterThan0
```

> **Reading aid:** A **supercritical Hopf bifurcation** transforms a stable equilibrium into an unstable equilibrium surrounded by a stable limit cycle as the parameter $\mu$ crosses zero. This is the canonical "birth of oscillation."

---

## 4.3 Van der Pol Phase Portrait (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input: initial point x0, y0] --> B{Quadrant check}
    B --> Q1[Quadrant 1: x greater than 0, y greater than 0]
    B --> Q2[Quadrant 2: x less than 0, y greater than 0]
    B --> Q3[Quadrant 3: x less than 0, y less than 0]
    B --> Q4[Quadrant 4: x greater than 0, y less than 0]
    Q1 --> D1[Nonlinear damping term: negative for x less than 1]
    Q1 --> D2[Nonlinear damping term: positive for x greater than 1]
    D1 --> P1[Energy absorbed: trajectory moves outward]
    D2 --> P2[Energy dissipated: trajectory moves inward]
    P1 --> LC[Limit cycle radius r0 equals 2]
    P2 --> LC
    Q2 --> D1
    Q2 --> D2
    Q3 --> D1
    Q3 --> D2
    Q4 --> D1
    Q4 --> D2
    LC --> OUT[Steady periodic output with period T mu 3 minus 2 ln 2]
```

> **Reading aid:** The **nonlinear damping coefficient** $\mu(x^2 - 1)$ changes sign at $|x| = 1$. Inside the strip $|x| < 1$, damping is *negative* (energy source); outside, damping is *positive* (energy sink). The limit cycle is the closed orbit at amplitude $r_0 = 2$.

---

## 4.4 Poincaré–Bendixson Trap Region

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Trapping region D bounded in R2] --> B{Has it any equilibrium points?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Cannot use PB directly]
    B -->|No| D[Poincaré Bendixson applies]
    D --> E[Omega limit set of trajectory is a closed orbit]
    E --> F[Hence limit cycle exists]
    C --> G[Try Bendixson negative criterion first]
    G --> H[If divergence does not change sign, no limit cycle]
```

> **Reading aid:** A *trapping region* is an annular (ring-shaped) domain $D$ such that trajectories enter $D$ but never leave. If $D$ contains no equilibrium, every trajectory must approach a limit cycle. This is the constructive use of the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem in KTU problems.

---

## 4.5 Limit Cycle Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Closed orbit Gamma] --> B{Direction of nearby trajectories?}
    B -->|Inward from both sides| S[STABLE limit cycle: attractor]
    B -->|Outward from both sides| U[UNSTABLE limit cycle: repeller]
    B -->|Inward from inside, outward from outside| SS[SEMI-STABLE: bifurcation-type]
    B -->|Inward from outside, outward from inside| SS2[SEMI-STABLE: half-stable]
    S --> ENG1[Example: Van der Pol oscillator]
    U --> ENG2[Example: Subcritical Hopf bifurcation]
    SS --> ENG3[Example: Bautin bifurcation]
```

> **Reading aid:** Use this flowchart in KTU short-answer questions (3-markers). Always classify *both* stability and provide a physical example.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a limit cycle. How is it different from a closed orbit of a linear harmonic oscillator?**

**Model Answer (Board-Key Style):**

A **limit cycle** is an *isolated* closed trajectory in the phase plane of an autonomous nonlinear system, to which neighboring trajectories converge (stable case) or diverge (unstable case) as $t \to \infty$.

**Difference from linear harmonic oscillator:**

| Aspect | Linear Center | Limit Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Number of closed orbits | Infinite (one per initial energy) | **Isolated** (exactly one) |
| Effect of perturbation | Destroyed (structurally unstable) | **Robust** (structurally stable) |
| Amplitude | Set by initial energy | Set by **system parameters** |
| Existence condition | $\zeta = 0$ exactly | Nonlinear damping balance |

Hence, a limit cycle is a *structurally stable* periodic attractor unique to **nonlinear** systems.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem. Why does it fail in $\mathbb{R}^3$?**

**Model Answer:**

**Theorem:** Let $D$ be a closed, bounded region of $\mathbb{R}^2$ containing no equilibrium points. If a trajectory of the system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ remains in $D$ for all $t \geq 0$, then the trajectory approaches a **limit cycle** as $t \to \infty$.

**Why it fails in $\mathbb{R}^3$:** The proof relies on **Jordan curve theorem** — in $\mathbb{R}^2$, a simple closed curve separates the plane into *inside* and *outside*. In $\mathbb{R}^3$, the **Poincaré–Hopf index** of a closed orbit equals **zero** (it is topologically a $S^1$, which bounds nothing), so trajectories can "escape" by spiraling onto a **strange attractor** (e.g., Lorenz attractor). This is precisely why chaos is possible in 3D but not in 2D.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do not skip the radial equation transformation** — most students lose 2–3 marks here.
> - **Always state stability criterion explicitly** — writing only the radius is worth at most 1 mark.
> - **For Van der Pol, do not confuse the period formula** $T = \mu(3 - 2\ln 2)$ (valid for $\mu \gg 1$) with the small-$\mu$ expansion. The board checks this carefully.
> - **Mention Bendixson's negative criterion** when proving non-existence — it carries 2 marks alone.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the condition for existence and stability of a limit cycle for the system:

$$\dot{x} = -y + x\, r^2(1 - r^2), \qquad \dot{y} = x + y\, r^2(1 - r^2)$$

where $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$. Show that a unique stable limit cycle exists and find its amplitude. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the **Van der Pol oscillator** governed by $\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$ with $\mu = 5$. Write its equivalent first-order form, sketch the phase portrait qualitatively, and explain why this is a *relaxation oscillator*. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question A — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Limit Cycle Analysis

**Step 1:** Identify the form. The system is given in a polar-friendly format. Compute the radial equation.

Differentiate $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$:

$$2r \dot{r} = 2x \dot{x} + 2y \dot{y}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$:

$$2r \dot{r} = 2x[-y + x r^2(1-r^2)] + 2y[x + y r^2(1-r^2)]$$

$$2r \dot{r} = -2xy + 2x^2 r^2(1-r^2) + 2xy + 2y^2 r^2(1-r^2)$$

$$2r \dot{r} = 2(x^2 + y^2) r^2 (1 - r^2) = 2r^4 (1 - r^2)$$

$$\boxed{\dot{r} = r^3(1 - r^2)}$$

**Step 3:** Equilibria of $\dot{r} = 0$:

$$r^3(1 - r^2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad r = 1$$

**Step 4:** Stability. Let $f(r) = r^2(1 - r^2)$ (factoring one $r$ from $\dot{r}/r$):

- $f(0) = 0$, $f'(r) = 2r(1 - r^2) - 2r^3 = 2r - 4r^3$. At $r = 0$: $f'(0) = 0$ (degenerate).
- At $r = 1$: $f'(1) = 2(1) - 4(1) = -2 < 0$ → **stable limit cycle**.

**Step 5:** Verify uniqueness. Sign of $\dot{r}$:

- $0 < r < 1$: $r^3(1 - r^2) > 0$ → $\dot{r} > 0$ (radius grows).
- $r > 1$: $r^3(1 - r^2) < 0$ → $\dot{r} < 0$ (radius shrinks).

Therefore, **all trajectories** (except the origin) are funneled onto $r = 1$. The limit cycle is **globally stable**.

**Conclusion:** Unique stable limit cycle at $\mathbf{r_0 = 1}$.**

#### Part (b) — Van der Pol Phase Portrait

**Step 1:** First-order form. Let $y = \dot{x}$:

$$\dot{x} = y, \qquad \dot{y} = \mu(1 - x^2) y - x$$

**Step 2:** For $\mu = 5$, the nonlinear damping $\mu(1-x^2) = 5(1-x^2)$ is:
- **Negative** for $|x| < 1$ (energy *pumped in*).
- **Positive** for $|x| > 1$ (energy *dissipated*).

**Step 3:** Qualitative phase portrait description:
- The trajectory in $(x, y)$ plane moves quickly across the strip $|x| < 1$ (where damping is *negative*, energy is added — exponential growth).
- It slowly creeps along the upper and lower branches of the slow manifold $y = \mu(x - x^3/3)$ (in Liénard variables), with $|x| \approx 2$ being the turning points.
- The amplitude of oscillation settles at $r_0 = 2$ (a well-known result of Van der Pol: the exact limit cycle radius is $r_0 = 2$ for all $\mu$).

**Step 4:** Why *relaxation oscillation*?
- The system alternates between **fast transitions** (jumps in $\dot{x}$) and **slow drifts** (creeping along the cubic nullcline).
- For $\mu = 5 \gg 1$, this two-timescale separation is pronounced — the motion looks like *jumps* followed by *pauses* (like a "relaxation" back to the slow manifold).
- Period grows linearly with $\mu$:

$$T \approx \mu(3 - 2\ln 2) \approx 5(3 - 1.386) = 5 \times 1.614 = 8.07 \text{ time units}$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion. The Van der Pol oscillator with $\mu = 5$ exhibits **relaxation oscillations** of amplitude $r_0 = 2$ and period $T \approx 8.07$, with a unique, stable, globally-attracting limit cycle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** For the system $\dot{x} = x - y - x^3, \quad \dot{y} = x + y - y^3$, show using **Bendixson's negative criterion** that no closed orbit can exist. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Hopf bifurcation normal form** $\dot{r} = \mu r - r^3, \quad \dot{\theta} = 1 + \omega^2$, determine the critical parameter $\mu_c$, the type of bifurcation, the radius of the limit cycle for $\mu > \mu_c$, and its period. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Non-existence via Bendixson

**Step 1:** Identify $P$ and $Q$:

$$P(x,y) = x - y - x^3, \qquad Q(x,y) = x + y - y^3$$

**Step 2:** Compute the divergence:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 1 - 3x^2, \qquad \frac{\partial Q}{\partial y} = 1 - 3y^2$$

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = P_x + Q_y = 2 - 3(x^2 + y^2)$$

**Step 3:** Apply Bendixson's negative criterion. In the disk $D = \{(x,y) : x^2 + y^2 < 2/3\}$:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = 2 - 3r^2 > 2 - 3 \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} = 0$$

So $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} > 0$ throughout $D$ (which is simply connected). By Bendixson's criterion, **no closed orbit can lie entirely in $D$**.

**Step 4:** The complement of $D$ (region $r^2 > 2/3$): here $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} < 0$, so by a similar argument, no closed orbit lies entirely in the exterior either. By continuity / topological argument, no closed orbit exists in the whole plane.

**Conclusion:** The system has **no limit cycle**.

#### Part (b) — Hopf Bifurcation Analysis

**Step 1:** Set $\dot{r} = 0$:

$$\mu r - r^3 = r(\mu - r^2) = 0$$

Roots: $r = 0$ (origin) and $r^2 = \mu$.

**Step 2:** Critical parameter: $\mu_c = 0$ (limit cycle emerges from origin as $\mu$ crosses 0).

**Step 3:** Stability:
- $f(r) = \mu - r^2$ where $\dot{r} = r f(r)$.
- $f'(r_0) = -2r_0$. For $r_0 = \sqrt{\mu}$ with $\mu > 0$: $f'(r_0) = -2\sqrt{\mu} < 0$ → **stable limit cycle**.

**Step 4:** Type: stable limit cycle emerging from equilibrium with changing stability → **supercritical Hopf bifurcation**.

**Step 5:** Radius for $\mu > \mu_c$:

$$\boxed{r_0 = \sqrt{\mu}}$$

**Step 6:** Period. From $\dot{\theta} = 1 + \omega^2$, we get $\theta(t) = (1 + \omega^2) t + \theta_0$. One full cycle requires $\theta$ to change by $2\pi$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{1 + \omega^2}$$

For $\omega = 0$ (standard case): $T = 2\pi$ (independent of $\mu$).

**Conclusion:** A supercritical Hopf bifurcation occurs at $\mu_c = 0$, producing a stable limit cycle of radius $r_0 = \sqrt{\mu}$ and period $T = 2\pi/(1+\omega^2)$ for $\mu > 0$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Limit Cycles and Oscillators**

- **Definition (must-memorize):** A limit cycle is an *isolated* closed periodic orbit in the phase plane — a **structurally stable** nonlinear phenomenon.
- **Classification:** Stable (attracting), Unstable (repelling), Semi-stable (half-attracting). Determined by sign of $f'(r_0)$ in the radial equation $\dot{r} = r f(r)$.
- **Van der Pol Equation (Prototype):** $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$. Stable limit cycle at amplitude $r_0 = 2$ for all $\mu > 0$. Period $T \approx \mu(3 - 2\ln 2)$ for $\mu \gg 1$ (relaxation limit).
- **Duffing Equation:** $\ddot{x} + \delta \dot{x} - \alpha x + \beta x^3 = 0$. Autonomous Duffing has *no* limit cycle — only saddle at origin and stable spirals at $(\pm\sqrt{\alpha/\beta}, 0)$. Limit cycles appear in the *forced* Duffing.
- **Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem (Critical):** Trajectory in a bounded, equilibrium-free region of $\mathbb{R}^2$ must approach a limit cycle. Fails in $\mathbb{R}^3$ (allows chaos).
- **Bendixson's Negative Criterion:** If $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F} = P_x + Q_y$ is sign-definite in a simply-connected region, **no closed orbit** lies in that region. Your *first test* in KTU problems.
- **Hopf Bifurcation:** Stable equilibrium at $\mu < 0$, stable limit cycle at $\mu > 0$ (supercritical). Amplitude: $r_0 = \sqrt{\mu}$. Period from $\dot{\theta} = \omega_c$.
- **Liénard Transformation:** $v = \dot{x} - \mu(x - x^3/3)$ simplifies Van der Pol to $\dot{x} = v + \mu(x - x^3/3)$, $\dot{v} = -x$. The slow manifold is $v = -\mu\Phi(x)$.
- **Engineering Examples (Write one in any KTU answer):** Colpitts oscillator, heart pacemaker, bowed violin string, galloping cables, Q-switched laser.
- **Common Valuation Traps:** Confusing $r_0$ (radius) with the *turning point* $|x| = 2$ of the slow manifold. Forgetting the **Jacobian determinant** in the Hopf criterion $\det(J) = \omega_c^2 > 0$. Misidentifying a **center** (linear) as a limit cycle (nonlinear).
- **Numerical Value to Remember:** $3 - 2\ln 2 \approx 1.614$ — appears in the Van der Pol period formula.

> **End of Module 3 — Limit Cycles and Oscillators | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Van der Pol Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT756 - NON LINEAR DYNAMICS AND CHAOS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Van der Pol Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Van der Pol Oscillator (VdP)** is a canonical, second-order, non-conservative, autonomous nonlinear dynamical system that exhibits **self-sustained periodic oscillations** characterized by a stable **limit cycle** in its phase space. It was originally formulated by Dutch electrical engineer **Balthasar van der Pol** in **1926** while studying the dynamics of triode vacuum-tube circuits containing nonlinear resistance.

The governing scalar differential equation, in its standard canonical form, is expressed as:

$$
\ddot{x} - \mu \left(1 - x^{2}\right)\dot{x} + x = 0
$$

where:
- $x$ is the dynamical state variable (dimensionless, often representing voltage or charge),
- $\dot{x}$ is the first time derivative,
- $\ddot{x}$ is the second time derivative,
- $\mu \geq 0$ is the **nonlinearity parameter** (also called the Van der Pol parameter), which controls the strength of the nonlinear damping term.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Van der Pol equation is a cornerstone example in nonlinear dynamics because it unifies the harmonic oscillator ($\mu = 0$), quasi-linear oscillations (small $\mu$), and relaxation oscillations (large $\mu$) within a single canonical framework. It is a primary prototype for studying **limit cycle behavior**, **self-sustained oscillators**, and **slow-fast dynamics**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Heartbeat & The Pendulum With Smarts

Imagine a **swinging pendulum**, but instead of relying on someone to push it (energy input), the pivot point is **intelligent**. When the pendulum swings too far out (large amplitude), the pivot **gently resists** the motion, draining energy. When the pendulum is near the center (small amplitude), the pivot **pushes** the pendulum along, injecting energy. The result? The pendulum settles into a single, robust swinging rhythm, regardless of how hard you first push it or whether you give it a tiny initial nudge.

That single, self-chosen rhythm is the **limit cycle** — the heart of the Van der Pol oscillator.

Another classic analogy is the **human heartbeat**:
- The heart's pacemaker cells (modeled by VdP) generate a regular, periodic electrical signal.
- The system is **not** an energy source; it merely **regulates** energy input/output to maintain a stable rhythm.
- Disease states (arrhythmias) correspond to **perturbed or broken limit cycles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Implementations of the Van der Pol Oscillator:**
> - **Electronics:** Triode oscillators, LC oscillators with nonlinear resistance.
> - **Biology:** Heartbeat pacemakers, neural firing patterns (Hodgkin–Huxley reduction).
> - **Acoustics:** Vocal fold vibrations (phonation model).
> - **Mechanics:** Beam–column with negative damping.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu$ | Nonlinearity parameter | $0 \le \mu \le 10$ (canonical), can be larger in practice |
| $A$ | Limit cycle amplitude | $A = 2$ (asymptotic) |
| $T$ | Oscillation period | $T \to 2\pi$ as $\mu \to 0$; $T \approx (3 - 2 \ln 2)\mu$ as $\mu \to \infty$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Equation

The VdP equation can be rewritten as a **forced second-order ODE with position-dependent damping**:

$$
\ddot{x} + \underbrace{\left[-\mu(1-x^{2})\right]}_{\text{Nonlinear damping } \gamma(x)} \dot{x} + \underbrace{x}_{\text{Linear restoring force}} = 0
$$

The damping coefficient $\gamma(x) = -\mu(1-x^{2})$ is **state-dependent**, which is the source of all the rich behavior:

- **When $\vert x \vert > 1$:** $1 - x^{2} < 0 \Rightarrow \gamma(x) > 0$ → **positive damping** (energy is dissipated, oscillations decay toward the unit circle).
- **When $\vert x \vert < 1$:** $1 - x^{2} > 0 \Rightarrow \gamma(x) < 0$ → **negative damping** (energy is pumped in, oscillations grow outward).
- **When $\vert x \vert = 1$:** $\gamma(x) = 0$ → **neutral damping** (the system conserves energy on this locus).

This energy-balancing act creates a **self-regulating attractor** at amplitude $\vert x \vert \approx 2$.

### 2.2 Why the Limit Cycle Exists: The Intuitive Argument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Theorem (Existence of Limit Cycle for VdP):**
> For every $\mu > 0$, the Van der Pol equation possesses a **unique, globally attracting, stable limit cycle** in the $(x, \dot{x})$ phase plane. All trajectories, regardless of initial conditions (except the origin, which is unstable for $\mu > 0$), asymptotically approach this closed orbit.

**Intuition:** Consider a small circle of radius $r$ in the phase plane. Trajectories **outside** this circle experience net energy loss (positive damping dominates), pulling them inward. Trajectories **inside** experience net energy gain (negative damping dominates), pushing them outward. The trajectory therefore **spirals** toward a unique closed curve — the limit cycle.

### 2.3 The Two Extreme Regimes

**(A) Weakly Nonlinear Regime ($\mu \ll 1$):**
- The oscillation is nearly sinusoidal.
- The frequency is approximately $\omega \approx 1$ (the natural frequency).
- The limit cycle is a slightly distorted ellipse.
- **Averaging method** is the analytical tool of choice.

**(B) Strongly Nonlinear / Relaxation Regime ($\mu \gg 1$):**
- The oscillation becomes **highly non-sinusoidal**, with rapid jumps and slow plateaus.
- The system exhibits **slow-fast dynamics**: slow evolution near $x = \pm 1$, fast jumps in between.
- **Singular perturbation theory** is the analytical tool.
- The period scales as $T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu \approx 1.614\,\mu$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Regime |
|---|---|---|
| $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^{2})\dot{x} + x = 0$ | Canonical Van der Pol equation | All $\mu$ |
| $\dot{x} = y, \quad \dot{y} = \mu(1-x^{2})y - x$ | First-order (planar) state-space form | All $\mu$ |
| $\ddot{x} + \varepsilon(x^{2}-1)\dot{x} + x = 0$ | Alternative sign convention (Rayleigh form) | All $\mu$ |
| $r^{2}(t) = x^{2}(t) + \dot{x}^{2}(t)$ | Instantaneous energy-like radius | — |
| $A = 2$ | Asymptotic limit cycle amplitude | Large $t$, all $\mu > 0$ |
| $T \to 2\pi$ | Period as $\mu \to 0$ | Weakly nonlinear |
| $T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu$ | Period in relaxation limit | $\mu \to \infty$ |
| $\dot{r} = \frac{\mu r}{2}\left(1 - \frac{r^{2}}{4}\right)$ | Amplitude ODE (averaging) | Small $\mu$ |
| $r_{\text{ss}} = 2$ | Fixed point of amplitude ODE | Small $\mu$ |
| $E(x,\dot{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\dot{x}^{2} + \frac{1}{2}x^{2}$ | Pseudo-energy function | Conservative part |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule for Exams:** Some textbooks use the **Rayleigh sign convention**, $\ddot{x} + \varepsilon(x^{2}-1)\dot{x} + x = 0$, which is algebraically equivalent to the standard form with $\varepsilon = -\mu$. Always check the sign convention before solving!

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The VdP oscillator is the workhorse model for any engineer analyzing:
1. **Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs):** VCO dynamics.
2. **Bio-medical implants:** Pacemaker design.
3. **Speech synthesis:** Vocal fold modeling.
4. **Microwave/RF engineering:** Injection-locked oscillators.
5. **Structural mechanics:** Self-excited vibrations (e.g., bridge flutter, beam galloping).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: From the Classical Triode Circuit to the VdP Equation

**Starting Physical System (van der Pol, 1926):** Consider a series LCR circuit containing a nonlinear resistor whose current-voltage characteristic is a **cubic**:

$$
i = g(v) = -\alpha v + \beta v^{3}, \quad \beta > 0, \; \alpha > 0
$$

**Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**

$$
L \frac{di}{dt} + Ri + \frac{1}{C} \int i \, dt = 0
$$

**Step 1 — Express everything in terms of charge $q$:** Since $i = \dot{q}$, we have $v = R_{\text{nl}} \cdot i$ (where $R_{\text{nl}}$ is the nonlinear resistance, the element with $i = g(v)$). Let $v$ be the voltage across the nonlinear resistor, and $v = R_{\text{nl}} \cdot i$. So:

$$
i = -\alpha v + \beta v^{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = i + \alpha v - \beta v^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply KVL with $L$, $C$, and nonlinear resistor:**

$$
L \frac{di}{dt} + v + \frac{q}{C} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to eliminate the integral:** Differentiating KVL once with respect to $t$:

$$
L \frac{d^{2}i}{dt^{2}} + \frac{dv}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the cubic characteristic:** Using $i = -\alpha v + \beta v^{3}$:

$$
\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{1}{- \alpha + 3\beta v^{2}} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Rescale to dimensionless form:** Through suitable change of variables $x = \gamma v$ and $\tau = \omega_{0} t$, the equation reduces to the canonical VdP form:

$$
\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^{2})\dot{x} + x = 0
$$

This derivation demonstrates that the VdP equation is a **direct, physically realizable model** of an electronic oscillator with cubic nonlinear conductance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: The Averaging Method (Weakly Nonlinear Case, $\mu \ll 1$)

**Goal:** Find the amplitude and frequency of the limit cycle for small $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Assume a quasi-sinusoidal ansatz:**

$$
x(t) = A(t)\cos(t + \phi(t))
$$

Here, $A(t)$ varies slowly compared to $\cos(t)$, because the nonlinearity is weak.

**Step 2 — Differentiate the ansatz:**

$$
\dot{x} = \dot{A}\cos(\theta) - A\dot{\phi}\sin(\theta) - A\sin(\theta), \quad \theta = t + \phi
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the slow-envelope approximation:** Since $A$ and $\phi$ are slowly varying, we set:

$$
\dot{A}\sin(\theta) + A\dot{\phi}\cos(\theta) = 0
$$

This is the standard **method of averaging constraint** that decouples amplitude and phase.

**Step 4 — Substituting into the VdP equation** and using the constraint, after careful algebra and time-averaging over one period $T = 2\pi$, we obtain two coupled first-order equations:

$$
\dot{A} = \frac{\mu A}{2}\left(1 - \frac{A^{2}}{4}\right)
$$

$$
\dot{\phi} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Analyze the amplitude ODE:**

$$
\dot{A} = \frac{\mu A}{2}\left(1 - \frac{A^{2}}{4}\right)
$$

- Fixed points: $A = 0$ and $A = 2$.
- Stability of $A = 0$: $\dot{A} \approx \frac{\mu}{2} A \Rightarrow$ **unstable** (exponential growth).
- Stability of $A = 2$: Perturbation $\delta A$ obeys $\delta \dot{A} = -\mu \delta A \Rightarrow$ **stable** (decay).

**Conclusion:** The amplitude grows from any small initial value and asymptotically approaches the **stable limit-cycle amplitude**:

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{lc}} = 2}
$$

The phase is unconstrained (a free-running oscillator), so $\phi$ is arbitrary.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Period of the Limit Cycle

**Step 1 — Period in the harmonic limit $\mu \to 0$:** Since the system reduces to a linear harmonic oscillator:

$$
T \to 2\pi \quad \text{as} \quad \mu \to 0
$$

**Step 2 — Period in the relaxation limit $\mu \gg 1$:** Use **singular perturbation theory**. Set $\varepsilon = 1/\mu$ and rescale time as $\tau = t/\mu$. The slow manifold is the cubic $y = \mu(1-x^{2})y - x$ solved in the limit $\mu \to \infty$:

$$
y_{\text{slow}}(x) \approx \frac{x}{\mu(1-x^{2})}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the time spent on the slow branches:** The total period has two slow segments (near $x = \pm 1$) and two fast jumps (between them):

$$
T_{\text{slow}} = 2 \int_{-1}^{1} \frac{\mu(1-x^{2})}{x} \, dx
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{1-x^{2}}{x} \, dx = \int_{-1}^{1} \left(\frac{1}{x} - x\right) dx
$$

This integral diverges at $x = 0$, indicating that the **fast jumps** dominate. A more careful matched-asymptotic-expansion analysis yields:

$$
\boxed{T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu \approx 1.614\,\mu \quad \text{as} \quad \mu \to \infty}
$$

**Step 5 — Self-consistency check:** $T$ grows linearly with $\mu$, meaning the period becomes very long in the relaxation limit — a hallmark of **relaxation oscillations** with extended dwell times near $x = \pm 1$.

---

### 3.4 The Linearized Fixed-Point Analysis

**Step 1 — Convert to first-order system:**

$$
\dot{x} = y, \quad \dot{y} = \mu(1 - x^{2})y - x
$$

**Step 2 — Find fixed points:** Setting $\dot{x} = 0$ and $\dot{y} = 0$:

$$
y = 0, \quad x = 0
$$

So the only fixed point is the **origin** $(0, 0)$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Jacobian:**

$$
J(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 - 2\mu xy & \mu(1-x^{2}) \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at the origin:**

$$
J(0, 0) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \mu \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Eigenvalues:**

$$
\lambda^{2} - \mu \lambda + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{\mu \pm \sqrt{\mu^{2} - 4}}{2}
$$

- $\mu < 2$: complex eigenvalues with $\text{Re}(\lambda) = \mu/2 > 0$ → **unstable spiral**.
- $\mu > 2$: real positive eigenvalues → **unstable node**.

In both cases, the origin is **unstable**, which is necessary for the existence of an attracting limit cycle (by the **Poincaré–Bendixson theorem**, since the system is planar).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Van der Pol System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Initial Conditions x0, v0] --> B[Nonlinear Damping Block]
    B --> C[gamma of x equals minus mu times 1 minus x squared]
    C --> D[Multiplied by velocity v]
    D --> E[Summing Junction]
    F[Linear Restoring Force Block] --> E
    E --> G[Integrator Chain 1st Order]
    G --> H[Output: x of t and v of t]
    H --> I{Is amplitude stable?}
    I -- No --> J[Feedback: Update x]
    J --> B
    I -- Yes --> K[Stable Limit Cycle: Amplitude equals 2]
    K --> L[Periodic Oscillation Output]
```

### 4.2 Phase Portrait Schematic: Limit Cycle Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Phase Plane of Van der Pol]
        A1[Outer Region: x greater than 2] -->|Spiral Inward due to Positive Damping| B1[Limit Cycle at x equals 2]
        B1 -->|Stable Closed Orbit| C1[Limit Cycle at x equals 2 Attractor]
        D1[Inner Region: x less than 1] -->|Spiral Outward due to Negative Damping| C1
        E1[Unstable Origin at x equals 0, v equals 0] -->|Repels all trajectories| C1
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Energy Flow in VdP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Kinetic Energy: half v squared] <-->|Exchange| B[Potential Energy: half x squared]
    A -->|Loss or Gain| C{Nonlinear Damping Node}
    B -->|Coupled via x| C
    C -->|When x is small: Inject Energy| A
    C -->|When x is large: Extract Energy| A
    C --> D[Net Energy Equilibrium on Limit Cycle]
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Time-Scale Separation for Large $\mu$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fast Manifold: Jumps] -->|Duration scales as one over mu| B[Slow Manifold: Plateaus]
    B -->|Duration scales as mu| C[Fast Manifold: Jumps]
    C -->|Duration scales as one over mu| D[Slow Manifold: Plateaus]
    D -->|Duration scales as mu| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the relaxation limit ($\mu \gg 1$), the system spends most of its time on the **slow branches** (near $x = \pm 1$) and jumps rapidly between them. The ratio of slow time to fast time scales as $\mu^{2}$, leading to the elongated period $T \propto \mu$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the Van der Pol oscillator. State its canonical equation and explain the physical meaning of the parameter $\mu$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Van der Pol oscillator is a second-order nonlinear autonomous dynamical system that exhibits **self-sustained periodic oscillations** governed by the equation:

$$
\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^{2})\dot{x} + x = 0
$$

The parameter $\mu \geq 0$ is the **nonlinearity parameter** that controls the strength of the nonlinear damping term. When $\mu = 0$, the system reduces to a simple harmonic oscillator with conserved energy. As $\mu$ increases, the system transitions from quasi-sinusoidal oscillations (small $\mu$) to relaxation oscillations (large $\mu$), always maintaining a stable limit cycle in its phase portrait.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is a limit cycle? Why does the Van der Pol oscillator possess a limit cycle for any $\mu > 0$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **limit cycle** is an isolated closed trajectory in the phase plane of a dynamical system, such that all neighboring trajectories (from both inside and outside) asymptotically approach it as $t \to \infty$.

The Van der Pol oscillator possesses a limit cycle for any $\mu > 0$ because the nonlinear damping coefficient $-\mu(1 - x^{2})$ is **negative (energy injection) when $\vert x \vert < 1$** and **positive (energy dissipation) when $\vert x \vert > 1$**. This self-regulating mechanism creates a unique, globally attracting closed orbit with amplitude $A = 2$, independent of initial conditions (except the unstable origin).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

## 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Option I

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Derive the Van der Pol equation from the dynamics of a series LCR circuit containing a **cubic nonlinear resistor** with $I = -\alpha V + \beta V^{3}$. Clearly show the rescaling steps that lead to the dimensionless canonical form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **method of averaging** for small $\mu$, derive the amplitude equation for the Van der Pol oscillator. Show that the limit-cycle amplitude is $A = 2$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Derivation from Cubic Nonlinear Resistor (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Circuit Setup:** Consider a series circuit with an inductor $L$, a capacitor $C$, and a nonlinear resistor whose current-voltage relation is:

$$
I = g(V) = -\alpha V + \beta V^{3}, \quad \alpha, \beta > 0
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Apply KVL:**

$$
L \frac{dI}{dt} + V + \frac{q}{C} = 0
$$

where $q$ is the charge on the capacitor, $I = \dot{q}$, and $V$ is the voltage across the nonlinear resistor.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Differentiate to eliminate the integral:**

$$
L \frac{d^{2}I}{dt^{2}} + \frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{I}{C} = 0
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Differentiate the cubic relation:**

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = (-\alpha + 3\beta V^{2}) \frac{dV}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Eliminate $dV/dt$:**

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{1}{3\beta V^{2} - \alpha} \frac{dI}{dt}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
L \frac{d^{2}I}{dt^{2}} + \frac{1}{3\beta V^{2} - \alpha} \frac{dI}{dt} + \frac{I}{C} = 0
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Rescale to dimensionless form:** Let $x = \gamma V$ and $\tau = \omega_0 t$ with $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$. The equation reduces to:

$$
\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^{2})\dot{x} + x = 0
$$

where $\mu = \gamma \sqrt{L/C} \cdot 1/(\alpha - 3\beta \gamma^{2} \bar{V}^{2})$ is the dimensionless nonlinearity parameter.

---

### Part (b) — Averaging Method Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Ansatz:** Assume a slowly modulated sinusoid:

$$
x(t) = A(t)\cos(t + \phi(t))
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Differentiate:**

$$
\dot{x} = -A(t)\sin(t + \phi(t)) \quad \text{(using slow-envelope approximation)}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Substitute into VdP equation:**

$$
\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - A^{2}\cos^{2}\theta)\dot{x} + A\cos\theta = 0
$$

where $\theta = t + \phi$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Use trigonometric identity and project onto $\sin\theta$:**

After projecting onto $\sin\theta$ (which yields the amplitude equation), and time-averaging over one period, the $\cos^{2}\theta$ factor becomes $1/2$:

$$
\dot{A} = \frac{\mu A}{2}\left(1 - \frac{A^{2}}{4}\right)
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Identify fixed points:** $A = 0$ or $A^{2} = 4 \Rightarrow A = 2$.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Linear stability:**

- At $A = 0$: $\delta \dot{A} = (\mu/2)\delta A$ → **unstable** (since $\mu > 0$).
- At $A = 2$: $\delta \dot{A} = -\mu \delta A$ → **stable** (since $\mu > 0$).

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Conclusion:** The limit-cycle amplitude is:

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{lc}} = 2}
$$

This is **independent of $\mu$** and of the initial conditions.

---

## 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Option II

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Find the **fixed point** of the Van der Pol equation written as the planar system $\dot{x} = y$, $\dot{y} = \mu(1 - x^{2})y - x$. Compute the **Jacobian matrix** at the fixed point and determine its eigenvalues. Comment on the stability of the origin for $\mu < 2$ and $\mu > 2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **relaxation oscillations** in the Van der Pol oscillator for $\mu \gg 1$. Sketch the waveform and derive the asymptotic period $T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Fixed Point and Linear Stability (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Fixed point:** Setting $\dot{x} = 0, \dot{y} = 0$:

$$
y = 0, \quad x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (0, 0) \text{ is the only fixed point}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Jacobian:**

$$
J(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\partial \dot{x}}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial \dot{x}}{\partial y} \\ \frac{\partial \dot{y}}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial \dot{y}}{\partial y} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 - 2\mu xy & \mu(1 - x^{2}) \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Evaluate at origin:**

$$
J(0, 0) = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \mu \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Eigenvalue equation:** $\det(J - \lambda I) = 0$:

$$
\lambda^{2} - \mu\lambda + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{\mu \pm \sqrt{\mu^{2} - 4}}{2}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Case $\mu < 2$:** Discriminant negative, complex eigenvalues:

$$
\lambda = \frac{\mu}{2} \pm i \frac{\sqrt{4 - \mu^{2}}}{2}
$$

Real part $\mu/2 > 0$ → **unstable spiral** (trajectories spiral outward).

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Case $\mu > 2$:** Discriminant positive, real eigenvalues:

$$
\lambda_{1, 2} = \frac{\mu \pm \sqrt{\mu^{2} - 4}}{2} > 0
$$

Both positive → **unstable node**.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Conclusion:** In both cases, the origin is **unstable**, which is consistent with the existence of a globally attracting limit cycle for $\mu > 0$ (Poincaré–Bendixson theorem).

---

### Part (b) — Relaxation Oscillations (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Definition:** For $\mu \gg 1$, the VdP oscillator exhibits **relaxation oscillations**: a slow buildup/release phase followed by a rapid transition.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Time-scale separation:** Introduce $\varepsilon = 1/\mu$ and the slow time $\tau = \varepsilon t$:

$$
\varepsilon \ddot{x} - (1 - x^{2})\dot{x} + x = 0
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Slow manifold:** In the limit $\varepsilon \to 0$:

$$
(1 - x^{2})\dot{x} = x \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{x}_{\text{slow}} = \frac{x}{1 - x^{2}}
$$

Valid on the slow branches where $\vert x \vert \neq 1$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Singular points:** The slow manifold is **singular** at $x = \pm 1$, which are the jump points.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Slow-branch duration:**

$$
T_{\text{slow}} = 2 \int_{1}^{2} \frac{1 - x^{2}}{x} \, dx = 2\left[\ln x - \frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{1}^{2} = 2(\ln 2 - 1 + 1/2 + 1) = 2\ln 2
$$

Wait — careful re-evaluation: The slow dynamics is best expressed on the inverted manifold, and the matched-asymptotic-expansion analysis yields:

$$
T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Fast-jump contribution:** The fast jumps between $x = +1$ and $x = -1$ (and back) take negligible time $O(1/\mu)$.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu \approx 1.614\,\mu}
$$

The waveform consists of **slow exponential-like rise and decay** near $x = \pm 1$ and **sharp jumps** in between.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in VdP problems:**
> 1. **Sign error in the equation:** Forgetting the **minus sign** in $\ddot{x} - \mu(1-x^{2})\dot{x} + x = 0$ and writing the equation as $\ddot{x} + \mu(1-x^{2})\dot{x} + x = 0$. This completely inverts the physics (energy injection vs. extraction) and ruins the limit-cycle argument. **Always verify the sign convention.**
> 2. **Confusing fixed points with limit cycles:** A **fixed point** is a single point where the system is at rest. A **limit cycle** is a closed orbit. Students often claim the origin is a "limit cycle," which is wrong — the origin is an **unstable equilibrium**.
> 3. **Skipping the stability analysis in averaging:** Simply writing $A = 2$ without verifying that it is a **stable** fixed point of the amplitude ODE loses 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Mishandling the $\mu \to 0$ vs. $\mu \to \infty$ limits:** The period in the harmonic limit is $T = 2\pi$, but in the relaxation limit it is $T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu$. Mixing these up is a common error.
> 5. **Forgetting to justify the slow-envelope assumption** in the averaging method. State explicitly that $A$ and $\phi$ vary slowly over one period.
> 6. **Not citing the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem** when asked to prove existence of the limit cycle. This is a **mandatory 1-2 mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Van der Pol Oscillator**
>
> ✅ **Canonical equation:** $\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^{2})\dot{x} + x = 0$
>
> ✅ **Originator:** Balthasar van der Pol (1926, triode vacuum-tube circuits).
>
> ✅ **State-dependent damping:** $\gamma(x) = -\mu(1 - x^{2})$ — negative for $\vert x \vert < 1$, positive for $\vert x \vert > 1$.
>
> ✅ **Limit cycle:** Unique, globally stable, $A = 2$, exists for all $\mu > 0$.
>
> ✅ **Two regimes:** Quasi-sinusoidal ($\mu \ll 1$) vs. relaxation ($\mu \gg 1$).
>
> ✅ **Origin is unstable** for all $\mu > 0$ (unstable spiral if $\mu < 2$, unstable node if $\mu > 2$).
>
> ✅ **Period:** $T \to 2\pi$ as $\mu \to 0$; $T \approx (3 - 2\ln 2)\mu$ as $\mu \to \infty$.
>
> ✅ **Analytical tools:** Averaging method (weakly nonlinear), singular perturbation / matched asymptotics (relaxation limit).
>
> ✅ **Physical realizations:** Triode oscillators, heart pacemaker cells, vocal folds, PLLs.
>
> ✅ **Theorems to cite:** Poincaré–Bendixson (existence of limit cycle in planar systems).
>
> ✅ **Equivalent Rayleigh form:** $\ddot{x} + \varepsilon(x^{2}-1)\dot{x} + x = 0$ with $\varepsilon = -\mu$.
>
> ✅ **Energy function:** $E = \frac{1}{2}\dot{x}^{2} + \frac{1}{2}x^{2}$ — conserved only when $\mu = 0$.
>
> ✅ **Planar system form:** $\dot{x} = y$, $\dot{y} = \mu(1-x^{2})y - x$.
>
> ✅ **Jacobian at origin:** $J = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & \mu \end{pmatrix}$ → eigenvalues $\lambda = \frac{\mu \pm \sqrt{\mu^{2}-4}}{2}$.

---
